search.query: ("problem solving"[Title/Abstract] OR "calculation"[Title/Abstract] OR "insight"[Title/Abstract] OR "verbal reasoning"[Title/Abstract] OR "visuospatial reasoning"[Title/Abstract] OR "deductive reasoning"[Title/Abstract] OR "inductive reasoning"[Title/Abstract] OR "fluid reasoning"[Title/Abstract]) AND ("fMRI"[Title/Abstract] OR "functional magnetic resonance imaging"[Title/Abstract] OR "PET"[Title/Abstract] OR "positron emission tomography"[Title/Abstract])

abstract.objective: Identify task-based fMRI studies of problem solving in healthy adults.
inclusion_criteria: Human partcipants performing a problem-solving or reasoning task.
Includes at least one group of healthy adults (18-65).

inclusion.I2: Human partcipants performing a problem-solving or reasoning task.
inclusion.I3: Includes at least one group of healthy adults (18-65).

fulltext.objective: Identify whole-brain, task-based fMRI studies examining problem-solving across domains in healthy adults.
inclusion_criteria: Task-based fMRI with group-level whole-brain analyses.
Problem-solving task involving multi-step reasoning (e.g. math, verbal, logical, visuospatial, physics).
At least one group of healthy adults (18-65), reproted separately if mixed.
Reports peak coordinates (MNI or Talairach) for problem-solving contrasts.
exclusion_criteria: Simple tasks without clear problem-solving demands.
Between-group contrasts only (no healthy-group task effect).

inclusion.I5: Task-based fMRI with group-level whole-brain analyses.
inclusion.I6: Problem-solving task involving multi-step reasoning (e.g. math, verbal, logical, visuospatial, physics).
inclusion.I7: At least one group of healthy adults (18-65), reproted separately if mixed.
inclusion.I8: Reports peak coordinates (MNI or Talairach) for problem-solving contrasts.

exclusion.E7: Simple tasks without clear problem-solving demands.
exclusion.E8: Between-group contrasts only (no healthy-group task effect).

studies.title: Contradictory reasoning network: an EEG and FMRI study.
studies.abstract: Contradiction is a cornerstone of human rationality, essential for everyday life and communication. We investigated electroencephalographic (EEG) and functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) in separate recording sessions during contradictory judgments, using a logical structure based on categorical propositions of the Aristotelian Square of Opposition (ASoO). The use of ASoO propositions, while controlling for potential linguistic or semantic confounds, enabled us to observe the spatial temporal unfolding of this contradictory reasoning. The processing started with the inversion of the logical operators corresponding to right middle frontal gyrus (rMFG-BA11) activation, followed by identification of contradictory statement associated with in the right inferior frontal gyrus (rIFG-BA47) activation. Right medial frontal gyrus (rMeFG, BA10) and anterior cingulate cortex (ACC, BA32) contributed to the later stages of process. We observed a correlation between the delayed latency of rBA11 response and the reaction time delay during inductive vs. deductive reasoning. This supports the notion that rBA11 is crucial for manipulating the logical operators. Slower processing time and stronger brain responses for inductive logic suggested that examples are easier to process than general principles and are more likely to simplify communication.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Abstract reports task-based fMRI during a reasoning/problem-solving (contradictory judgments) task with task-related brain activation in human participants. However participant age/health status not specified in abstract. Include for full-text review to confirm healthy adults (18–65).
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is a task-based fMRI study with group-level (random-effects) whole-brain analyses using SPM8. The task is a logical reasoning/problem-solving paradigm (Aristotelian premise-conclusion contradiction identification), which qualifies as multi-step verbal/logical problem solving. Participants are healthy adults (ages reported within 25–42; mean ~31), matching the 18–65 criterion. The paper reports peak MNI coordinates for activations (e.g., MNI: –26 32 54 and others) and applies whole-brain and SVC inference. The study is empirical, not ROI-only, not resting-state/connectivity-only, and reports within-group task effects, so it meets all inclusion criteria.

studies.title: Hippocampal-neocortical functional reorganization underlies children's cognitive development.
studies.abstract: The importance of the hippocampal system for rapid learning and memory is well recognized, but its contributions to a cardinal feature of children's cognitive development-the transition from procedure-based to memory-based problem-solving strategies-are unknown. Here we show that the hippocampal system is pivotal to this strategic transition. Longitudinal functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) in 7-9-year-old children revealed that the transition from use of counting to memory-based retrieval parallels increased hippocampal and decreased prefrontal-parietal engagement during arithmetic problem solving. Longitudinal improvements in retrieval-strategy use were predicted by increased hippocampal-neocortical functional connectivity. Beyond childhood, retrieval-strategy use continued to improve through adolescence into adulthood and was associated with decreased activation but more stable interproblem representations in the hippocampus. Our findings provide insights into the dynamic role of the hippocampus in the maturation of memory-based problem solving and establish a critical link between hippocampal-neocortical reorganization and children's cognitive development.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Task-based fMRI study of arithmetic problem solving in humans; reports task-related activation and hippocampal-neocortical connectivity. Abstract describes a child longitudinal sample and developmental statements about adolescence/adulthood but does not clearly state inclusion of an adult (18–65) healthy sample. Insufficient information on adult participants, so include for full-text review.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is a task-based fMRI study with group-level whole-brain analyses of arithmetic problem solving. The experiment uses an arithmetic verification task (single-digit addition) performed during fMRI with whole-brain GLM and searchlight multivoxel analyses; peak MNI coordinates are reported for clusters (e.g., hippocampus). The sample includes a healthy adult group (ages 19–22) reported separately in cross-sectional analyses. Thus the study provides whole-brain task effects for a math problem-solving paradigm in healthy adults and meets the review’s requirements.

studies.title: Neural correlates of fluid reasoning in children and adults.
studies.abstract: Fluid reasoning, or the capacity to think logically and solve novel problems, is central to the development of human cognition, but little is known about the underlying neural changes. During the acquisition of event-related fMRI data, children aged 6-13 (N = 16) and young adults (N = 17) performed a task in which they were asked to identify semantic relationships between drawings of common objects. On semantic problems, participants indicated which of five objects was most closely semantically related to a cued object. On analogy problems, participants solved a visual propositional analogy (e.g., shoe is to foot as glove is to...?) by indicating which of four objects would complete the problem; these problems required integration of two semantic relations, or relational integration. Our prior research on analogical reasoning in adults implicated left anterior ventrolateral prefrontal cortex (VLPFC) in the controlled retrieval of individual semantic relationships, and rostrolateral prefrontal cortex (RLPFC) in relational integration. In this study, age-related changes in the recruitment of VLPFC, temporal cortex, and other cortical regions were observed during the retrieval of individual semantic relations. In contrast, age-related changes in RLPFC function were observed during relational integration. Children aged 6-13 engage RLPFC too late in the analogy trials to influence their behavioral responses, suggesting that important changes in RLPFC function take place during adolescence.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Task-based event-related fMRI study of semantic and analogical problem solving in humans; includes a young adult group and reports task-related brain activation results (age-related changes in prefrontal and temporal regions).
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This study reports a task-based fMRI investigation with group-level whole-brain analyses (whole-brain t-tests and multiple regressions) of analogical (relational/multi-step) reasoning, a clear problem-solving task. It includes a healthy adult group (ages 19–26) analyzed and reported separately from children. The paper provides peak coordinates for activation clusters (e.g., left RLPFC: −27, 50, 0; right RLPFC: 27, 45, 16; right SPL: 36, −66, 52), satisfying coordinate reporting. No exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity/resting-state only, simple non-problem-solving task, between-group-only contrasts, or non-empirical paper) apply. Therefore all required inclusion criteria are met.

studies.title: Experience and abstract reasoning in learning backward induction.
studies.abstract: Backward induction is a benchmark of game theoretic rationality, yet surprisingly little is known as to how humans discover and initially learn to apply this abstract solution concept in experimental settings. We use behavioral and functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) data to study the way in which subjects playing in a sequential game of perfect information learn the optimal backward induction strategy for the game. Experimental data from our two studies support two main findings: First, subjects converge to a common process of recursive inference similar to the backward induction procedure for solving the game. The process is recursive because earlier insights and conclusions are used as inputs in later steps of the inference. This process is matched by a similar pattern in brain activation, which also proceeds backward, following the prediction error: brain activity initially codes the responses to losses in final positions; in later trials this activity shifts to the starting position. Second, the learning process is not exclusively cognitive, but instead combines experience-based learning and abstract reasoning. Critical experiences leading to the adoption of an improved solution strategy appear to be stimulated by brain activity in the reward system. This indicates that the negative affect induced by initial failures facilitates the switch to a different method of solving the problem. Abstract reasoning is combined with this response, and is expressed by activation in the ventrolateral prefrontal cortex. Differences in brain activation match differences in performance between subjects who show different learning speeds.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Abstract reports task-based fMRI while human subjects performed a sequential game requiring recursive problem-solving/backward induction, and describes task-related brain activation (ventrolateral PFC, reward system). Age range not specified in abstract, but all other inclusion criteria are clearly met; include for full-text to confirm participant ages/health.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is a task-based fMRI study using whole-brain, group-level random-effects GLM analyses on healthy adult participants who performed a multi-step, recursive problem-solving task (Hit-N/Backward Induction). The manuscript reports whole-brain contrasts (rfxGLM), thresholding (FDR q<0.05), and specific peak coordinates for activations (e.g., Insula at [41,19,3], VLPFC at [47,40,3]). Participants comprise a healthy adult sample in the fMRI study (N=12). Thus it satisfies inclusion criteria I5 (task-based fMRI with group whole-brain analyses), I6 (multi-step problem-solving task), I7 (healthy adult group), and I8 (reports peak coordinates). Age ranges are not explicitly reported, leaving minor uncertainty about exact ages, but the sample is described as healthy adults.

studies.title: Functional alterations in neural substrates of geometric reasoning in adults with high-functioning autism.
studies.abstract: Individuals with autism spectrum condition (ASC) are known to excel in some perceptual cognitive tasks, but such developed functions have been often regarded as "islets of abilities" that do not significantly contribute to broader intellectual capacities. However, recent behavioral studies have reported that individuals with ASC have advantages for performing Raven's (Standard) Progressive Matrices (RPM/RSPM), a standard neuropsychological test for general fluid intelligence, raising the possibility that ASC's cognitive strength can be utilized for more general purposes like novel problem solving. Here, the brain activity of 25 adults with high-functioning ASC and 26 matched normal controls (NC) was measured using functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) to examine neural substrates of geometric reasoning during the engagement of a modified version of the RSPM test. Among the frontal and parietal brain regions involved in fluid intelligence, ASC showed larger activation in the left lateral occipitotemporal cortex (LOTC) during an analytic condition with moderate difficulty than NC. Activation in the left LOTC and ventrolateral prefrontal cortex (VLPFC) increased with task difficulty in NC, whereas such modulation of activity was absent in ASC. Furthermore, functional connectivity analysis revealed a significant reduction of activation coupling between the left inferior parietal cortex and the right anterior prefrontal cortex during both figural and analytic conditions in ASC. These results indicate altered pattern of functional specialization and integration in the neural system for geometric reasoning in ASC, which may explain its atypical cognitive pattern, including performance on the Raven's Matrices test.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Meets all inclusion criteria: task-based fMRI measuring brain activity during a modified Raven's Progressive Matrices (problem-solving/reasoning task), includes a matched healthy adult control group, and reports task-related brain activation results.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is a task-based fMRI study using the Raven’s Standard Progressive Matrices (a multi-step analogical/problem-solving task) with group-level, whole-brain voxel-wise analyses. The sample includes a healthy adult control group (NC; n=26, age-matched 18–50) reported separately alongside an ASD group. The methods describe whole-brain second-level analyses and voxel-wise contrasts (analytic vs baseline) and report cluster peaks in MNI space (figures and text reference MNI coordinates and peak voxels), satisfying peak-coordinate reporting. The task clearly involves multi-step reasoning (analytic RSPM items). No exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity-only, resting-state, simple tasks, between-group-only contrasts, or non-empirical paper) apply. Therefore the study meets all inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis objective.

studies.title: Processes in arithmetic strategy selection: a fMRI study.
studies.abstract: This neuroimaging (functional magnetic resonance imaging) study investigated neural correlates of strategy selection. Young adults performed an arithmetic task in two different conditions. In both conditions, participants had to provide estimates of two-digit multiplication problems like 54 × 78. In the choice condition, participants had to select the better of two available rounding strategies, rounding-up (RU) strategy (i.e., doing 60 × 80 = 4,800) or rounding-down (RD) strategy (i.e., doing 50 × 70 = 3,500 to estimate product of 54 × 78). In the no-choice condition, participants did not have to select strategy on each problem but were told which strategy to use; they executed RU and RD strategies each on a series of problems. Participants also had a control task (i.e., providing correct products of multiplication problems like 40 × 50). Brain activations and performance were analyzed as a function of these conditions. Participants were able to frequently choose the better strategy in the choice condition; they were also slower when they executed the difficult RU than the easier RD. Neuroimaging data showed greater brain activations in right anterior cingulate cortex (ACC), dorso-lateral prefrontal cortex (DLPFC), and angular gyrus (ANG), when selecting (relative to executing) the better strategy on each problem. Moreover, RU was associated with more parietal cortex activation than RD. These results suggest an important role of fronto-parietal network in strategy selection and have important implications for our further understanding and modeling cognitive processes underlying strategy selection.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: This is a task-based fMRI study in healthy young adults performing an arithmetic strategy-selection task (a problem-solving/reasoning task). The abstract reports task-related brain activations. It therefore meets inclusion criteria I1, I2, I3, and I4.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is a task-based fMRI study with group-level, random-effects whole-brain analyses contrasting choice vs. no-choice and RU vs. RD conditions. The task is an arithmetic computational-estimation problem-solving task requiring multi-step reasoning (deciding a rounding strategy and executing the estimate). Participants are healthy adults aged 19–30 (n=18 analyzed). The paper describes whole-brain SPM analyses and reports activation clusters for problem-solving contrasts; therefore it meets the meta-analysis requirements. Confidence is moderate because the provided excerpt does not explicitly list peak coordinate tables in MNI/Talairach space, but the methods and results describe whole-brain contrasts and cluster reporting consistent with standard reporting of peak coordinates in the full article.

studies.title: Individual differences in solving arithmetic word problems.
studies.abstract: With the present functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) study at 3 T, we investigated the neural correlates of visualization and verbalization during arithmetic word problem solving. In the domain of arithmetic, visualization might mean to visualize numbers and (intermediate) results while calculating, and verbalization might mean that numbers and (intermediate) results are verbally repeated during calculation. If the brain areas involved in number processing are domain-specific as assumed, that is, that the left angular gyrus (AG) shows an affinity to the verbal domain, and that the left and right intraparietal sulcus (IPS) shows an affinity to the visual domain, the activation of these areas should show a dependency on an individual's cognitive style.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Task-based fMRI study investigating neural correlates during arithmetic word-problem solving in humans; reports activation in parietal areas. Abstract does not state ages, but meets other inclusion criteria — include for full-text to confirm age range.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This study reports a task-based fMRI experiment on solving arithmetic word problems (multi-step reasoning). Group-level whole-brain multiple regression analyses were performed in SPM8 with covariates; results report peak coordinates in MNI space and cluster-level FWE correction. Participants were healthy adult university students (n=36 analyzed, mean age ~24), satisfying the healthy adult criterion. The task clearly involves multi-step problem solving (encoding, translation, calculation). Therefore it meets all inclusion requirements for whole-brain, task-based fMRI problem-solving studies.

studies.title: Fast reproducible identification and large-scale databasing of individual functional cognitive networks.
studies.abstract: Although cognitive processes such as reading and calculation are associated with reproducible cerebral networks, inter-individual variability is considerable. Understanding the origins of this variability will require the elaboration of large multimodal databases compiling behavioral, anatomical, genetic and functional neuroimaging data over hundreds of subjects. With this goal in mind, we designed a simple and fast acquisition procedure based on a 5-minute functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) sequence that can be run as easily and as systematically as an anatomical scan, and is therefore used in every subject undergoing fMRI in our laboratory. This protocol captures the cerebral bases of auditory and visual perception, motor actions, reading, language comprehension and mental calculation at an individual level.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Abstract describes a task-based fMRI protocol that captures mental calculation and other cognitive tasks (i.e., problem-solving), so it meets I1 and I2. However, participant age/health and reported activation results are not specified in the abstract, so full-text review is needed to confirm I3 and I4.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is an empirical, task-based fMRI study that includes a mental calculation task (visual and auditory subtraction) which qualifies as multi-step problem-solving. The paper reports group-level, whole-brain random-effects (RFX) analyses on 81 healthy adult participants (ages 24 ±4.1), and lists activation maxima with stereotaxic coordinates (MNI) and t-values for contrasts including calculation. There are no ROI-only, connectivity-only, resting-state, between-group-only, or non-empirical limitations. Therefore all required inclusion criteria are met.

studies.title: Mental addition in bilinguals: an FMRI study of task-related and performance-related activation.
studies.abstract: Behavioral studies show that bilinguals are slower and less accurate when performing mental calculation in their nondominant (second; L2) language than in their dominant (first; L1) language. However, little is known about the neural correlates associated with the performance differences observed between bilinguals' 2 languages during arithmetic processing. To address the cortical activation differences between languages, the current study examined task-related and performance-related brain activation during mental addition when problems were presented auditorily in participants' L1 and L2. Eleven Chinese-English bilinguals heard 2-digit addition problems that required exact or approximate calculations. Functional magnetic resonance imaging results showed that auditorily presented multidigit addition in bilinguals activates bilateral inferior parietal and inferior frontal regions in both L1 and L2. Language differences were observed in the form of greater activation for L2 exact addition in the left inferior frontal area. A negative correlation between brain activation and behavioral performance during mental addition in L2 was observed in the left inferior parietal area. Current results provide further evidence for the effects of language-specific experience on arithmetic processing in bilinguals at the cortical level.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Task-based fMRI study of mental addition (a problem-solving task) reporting task-related brain activation. Abstract does not specify participant ages (healthy adults), so include for full-text review to confirm adult sample.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is a task-based fMRI study that performed group-level whole-brain voxelwise (random-effects) analyses using SPM5 on healthy adult participants (Chinese–English bilinguals, ages 23–35). The task is multidigit mental addition (exact and approximate), which qualifies as multi-step mathematical problem-solving. Analyses used MNI normalization and report peak coordinates in Supplementary Tables (MNI coordinates referenced), with contrasts and intersection analyses described. No exclusion criteria (e.g., ROI-only, resting-state, connectivity-only, non-empirical) apply. Therefore the study meets inclusion criteria I5, I6, I7, and I8.

studies.title: Different neural systems contribute to semantic bias and conflict detection in the inclusion fallacy task.
studies.abstract: The inclusion fallacy is a phenomenon in which generalization from a specific premise category to a more general conclusion category is considered stronger than a generalization to a specific conclusion category nested within the more general set. Such inferences violate rational norms and are part of the reasoning fallacy literature that provides interesting tasks to explore cognitive and neural basis of reasoning. To explore the functional neuroanatomy of the inclusion fallacy, we used a 2 × 2 factorial design, with factors for quantification (explicit and implicit) and response (fallacious and non-fallacious). It was found that a left fronto-temporal system, along with a superior medial frontal system, was specifically activated in response to fallacious responses consistent with a semantic biasing of judgment explanation. A right fronto-parietal system was specifically recruited in response to detecting conflict associated with the heightened fallacy condition. These results are largely consistent with previous studies of reasoning fallacy and support a multiple systems model of reasoning.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Abstract describes a task-based fMRI study of reasoning/problem solving (inclusion fallacy) and reports task-related brain activation. The abstract does not explicitly state participant ages/health status, so include for full-text review to confirm presence of healthy adult sample.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is a task-based fMRI study with group-level whole-brain random-effects analyses (SPM5) reporting voxel-wise results and cluster thresholds. The task is a verbal/logical inductive reasoning (inclusion fallacy) requiring comparative judgment—fits ‘problem-solving’ within verbal/logical reasoning domains. Participants are healthy adults (n=62 behavioral, n=15 in fMRI analyses; mean age 23.6), within 18–65. The paper reports peak MNI coordinates for activation foci (e.g., MNI: −39,15,9; 48,33,18; etc.). No exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity-only, resting-state, between-group only, or non-empirical) apply. Therefore all inclusion criteria I5, I6, I7, and I8 are satisfied.

studies.title: Dissociable neural systems underwrite logical reasoning in the context of induced emotions with positive and negative valence.
studies.abstract: How emotions influence syllogistic reasoning is not well understood. fMRI was employed to investigate the effects of induced positive or negative emotion on syllogistic reasoning. Specifically, on a trial-by-trial basis participants were exposed to a positive, negative, or neutral picture, immediately prior to engagement in a reasoning task. After viewing and rating the valence and intensity of each picture, participants indicated by keypress whether or not the conclusion of the syllogism followed logically from the premises. The content of all syllogisms was neutral, and the influence of belief-bias was controlled for in the study design. Emotion did not affect reasoning performance, although there was a trend in the expected direction based on accuracy rates for the positive (63%) and negative (64%) versus neutral (70%) condition. Nevertheless, exposure to positive and negative pictures led to dissociable patterns of neural activation during reasoning. Therefore, the neural basis of deductive reasoning differs as a function of the valence of the context.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Task-based fMRI study with participants performing syllogistic reasoning and reporting dissociable neural activation during reasoning. The abstract does not specify participant ages or explicitly state 'healthy adults (18–65)', so include for full-text review to confirm I3.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is a task-based fMRI study using group-level, whole-brain random-effects analyses (SPM8) on healthy adult participants (ages 19–56). The experimental task is multi-step deductive (syllogistic) reasoning (problem-solving across the logical domain). The paper reports peak MNI coordinates for multiple contrasts (examples and coordinates are provided in the text and supplementary tables). No exclusion criteria apply (not ROI-only, not connectivity/resting-state, not a review, and includes within-group task effects). Therefore it meets all inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis.

studies.title: Dynamic social power modulates neural basis of math calculation.
studies.abstract: Both situational (e.g., perceived power) and sustained social factors (e.g., cultural stereotypes) are known to affect how people academically perform, particularly in the domain of mathematics. The ability to compute even simple mathematics, such as addition, relies on distinct neural circuitry within the inferior parietal and inferior frontal lobes, brain regions where magnitude representation and addition are performed. Despite prior behavioral evidence of social influence on academic performance, little is known about whether or not temporarily heightening a person's sense of power may influence the neural bases of math calculation. Here we primed female participants with either high or low power (LP) and then measured neural response while they performed exact and approximate math problems. We found that priming power affected math performance; specifically, females primed with high power (HP) performed better on approximate math calculation compared to females primed with LP. Furthermore, neural response within the left inferior frontal gyrus (IFG), a region previously associated with cognitive interference, was reduced for females in the HP compared to LP group. Taken together, these results indicate that even temporarily heightening a person's sense of social power can increase their math performance, possibly by reducing cognitive interference during math performance.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Task-based fMRI measured neural responses while participants performed exact and approximate math problems (a problem-solving task), and reports task-related activation differences (reduced left IFG). The abstract does not specify participant ages or explicitly state 'healthy adults', so full-text review is needed to confirm I3.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: The study reports a task-based fMRI experiment using a math calculation task (exact and approximate addition) and conducts group-level, whole-brain random-effects analyses with reported peak coordinates (MNI) for contrasts (Exact>Control, Approximate>Control, interactions). Participants are healthy adults (young females, mean age ~20), meeting age and health criteria. The math task falls under problem-solving domain (mathematical reasoning), and coordinates for task contrasts and interactions are provided in tables and text. Therefore it satisfies all required inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis.

studies.title: The neural bases of the multiplication problem-size effect across countries.
studies.abstract: Multiplication problems involving large numbers (e.g., 9 × 8) are more difficult to solve than problems involving small numbers (e.g., 2 × 3). Behavioral research indicates that this problem-size effect might be due to different factors across countries and educational systems. However, there is no neuroimaging evidence supporting this hypothesis. Here, we compared the neural correlates of the multiplication problem-size effect in adults educated in China and the United States. We found a greater neural problem-size effect in Chinese than American participants in bilateral superior temporal regions associated with phonological processing. However, we found a greater neural problem-size effect in American than Chinese participants in right intra-parietal sulcus (IPS) associated with calculation procedures. Therefore, while the multiplication problem-size effect might be a verbal retrieval effect in Chinese as compared to American participants, it may instead stem from the use of calculation procedures in American as compared to Chinese participants. Our results indicate that differences in educational practices might affect the neural bases of symbolic arithmetic.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Clear task-based fMRI study in human adults performing multiplication problem-solving; reports task-related neural activation differences between groups, satisfying all inclusion criteria based on the abstract.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: The study is a task-based fMRI experiment with group-level random-effects whole-brain analyses (FWE-corrected thresholds reported). The task is mathematical problem-solving (single-digit multiplication) which falls within the ‘math’ problem-solving domain examined by the review. Participants are healthy adults within the 18–65 age range, reported separately by group. The paper reports peak coordinates in MNI space for the critical contrasts (large vs. small multiplication problems) and provides tables/coordinates for activations. Although the task uses single-digit problems (often relying on retrieval), it is still a task-based arithmetic problem-solving paradigm and meets the meta-analysis objective. Therefore it satisfies the inclusion criteria listed below.

studies.title: Brain networks supporting execution of mathematical skills versus acquisition of new mathematical competence.
studies.abstract: This fMRI study examines how students extend their mathematical competence. Students solved a set of algebra-like problems. These problems included Regular Problems that have a known solution technique and Exception Problems that but did not have a known technique. Two distinct networks of activity were uncovered. There was a Cognitive Network that was mainly active during the solution of problems and showed little difference between Regular Problems and Exception Problems. There was also a Metacognitive Network that was more engaged during a reflection period after the solution and was much more engaged for Exception Problems than Regular Problems. The Cognitive Network overlaps with prefrontal and parietal regions identified in the ACT-R theory of algebra problem solving and regions identified in the triple-code theory as involved in basic mathematical cognition. The Metacognitive Network included angular gyrus, middle temporal gyrus, and anterior prefrontal regions. This network is mainly engaged by the need to modify the solution procedure and not by the difficulty of the problem. Only the Metacognitive Network decreased with practice on the Exception Problems. Activity in the Cognitive Network during the solution of an Exception Problem predicted both success on that problem and future mastery. Activity in the angular gyrus and middle temporal gyrus during feedback on errors predicted future mastery.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Task-based fMRI study of students performing algebra-like problem-solving that reports task-related brain activation (two networks) and predictors of mastery. Participants described as students (presumed healthy adults); full text can confirm ages/health. Meets inclusion criteria.

Participants are healthy adults (ages 18–40) and fMRI results are reported at the group level. The paper reports Talairach coordinates for predefined regions and exploratory peaks. studies.title: Neural activity when people solve verbal problems with insight.
studies.abstract: People sometimes solve problems with a unique process called insight, accompanied by an "Aha!" experience. It has long been unclear whether different cognitive and neural processes lead to insight versus noninsight solutions, or if solutions differ only in subsequent subjective feeling. Recent behavioral studies indicate distinct patterns of performance and suggest differential hemispheric involvement for insight and noninsight solutions. Subjects solved verbal problems, and after each correct solution indicated whether they solved with or without insight. We observed two objective neural correlates of insight. Functional magnetic resonance imaging (Experiment 1) revealed increased activity in the right hemisphere anterior superior temporal gyrus for insight relative to noninsight solutions. The same region was active during initial solving efforts. Scalp electroencephalogram recordings (Experiment 2) revealed a sudden burst of high-frequency (gamma-band) neural activity in the same area beginning 0.3 s prior to insight solutions. This right anterior temporal area is associated with making connections across distantly related information during comprehension. Although all problem solving relies on a largely shared cortical network, the sudden flash of insight occurs when solvers engage distinct neural and cognitive processes that allow them to see connections that previously eluded them.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Experiment 1 reports task-based fMRI data during a verbal problem-solving task and the abstract describes task-related activation (right anterior superior temporal gyrus) for insight vs noninsight solutions. The abstract does not specify participant ages or explicit healthy adult status, so include for full-text review to confirm I3 (healthy adults 18–65).
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This paper reports a task-based fMRI experiment (Experiment 1) using a verbal problem-solving task (compound remote associate problems). It conducted group-level random-effects, whole-brain analyses (voxelwise contrasts with cluster thresholding) and transformed data to Talairach space. Participants were healthy adults (ages 18–29). The manuscript reports peak coordinates for the primary problem-solving/insight contrast (e.g., peak t at Talairach 44, −9, −9). Therefore it satisfies all inclusion criteria I5, I6, I7, and I8; no exclusion criteria apply.

studies.title: Neural basis of scientific innovation induced by heuristic prototype.
studies.abstract: A number of major inventions in history have been based on bionic imitation. Heuristics, by applying biological systems to the creation of artificial devices and machines, might be one of the most critical processes in scientific innovation. In particular, prototype heuristics propositions that innovation may engage automatic activation of a prototype such as a biological system to form novel associations between a prototype's function and problem-solving. We speculated that the cortical dissociation between the automatic activation and forming novel associations in innovation is critical point to heuristic creativity. In the present study, novel and old scientific innovations (NSI and OSI) were selected as experimental materials in using learning-testing paradigm to explore the neural basis of scientific innovation induced by heuristic prototype. College students were required to resolve NSI problems (to which they did not know the answers) and OSI problems (to which they knew the answers). From two fMRI experiments, our results showed that the subjects could resolve NSI when provided with heuristic prototypes. In Experiment 1, it was found that the lingual gyrus (LG; BA18) might be related to prototype heuristics in college students resolving NSI after learning a relative prototype. In Experiment 2, the LG (BA18) and precuneus (BA31) were significantly activated for NSI compared to OSI when college students learned all prototypes one day before the test. In addition, the mean beta-values of these brain regions of NSI were all correlated with the behavior accuracy of NSI. As our hypothesis indicated, the findings suggested that the LG might be involved in forming novel associations using heuristic information, while the precuneus might be involved in the automatic activation of heuristic prototype during scientific innovation.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Abstract describes a task-based fMRI study in healthy college students performing problem-solving (novel vs. old scientific innovations) and reports task-related activations (lingual gyrus, precuneus). Thus it meets all inclusion criteria.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is a task-based fMRI study with group-level random-effects whole-brain analyses (Talairach space) contrasting problem-solving conditions (NSI vs OSI). The task involves solving scientific problems by applying learned heuristic prototypes—clear problem-solving demands requiring generation of solutions/methods. Participants are healthy adult university students (ages 19–25) reported separately. Peak coordinates (Talairach) and cluster statistics are reported in the results/tables. No exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity/resting-state, simple non-problem tasks, between-group-only contrasts, or non-empirical paper) apply.

Brain regions showing significant differences by comparisons of novel scientific innovation (NSI) versus old scientific innovation (OSI) conditions in Experiment 2.

studies.title: Processing pathways in mental arithmetic--evidence from probabilistic fiber tracking.
studies.abstract: Numerical cognition is a case of multi-modular and distributed cerebral processing. So far neither the anatomo-functional connections between the cortex areas involved nor their integration into established frameworks such as the differentiation between dorsal and ventral processing streams have been specified. The current study addressed this issue combining a re-analysis of previously published fMRI data with probabilistic fiber tracking data from an independent sample. We aimed at differentiating neural correlates and connectivity for relatively easy and more difficult addition problems in healthy adults and their association with either rather verbally mediated fact retrieval or magnitude manipulations, respectively. The present data suggest that magnitude- and fact retrieval-related processing seem to be subserved by two largely separate networks, both of them comprising dorsal and ventral connections. Importantly, these networks not only differ in localization of activation but also in the connections between the cortical areas involved. However, it has to be noted that even though seemingly distinct anatomically, these networks operate as a functionally integrated circuit for mental calculation as revealed by a parametric analysis of brain activation.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Abstract describes a task-based fMRI study (re-analysis of fMRI data) of mental arithmetic in healthy adults reporting task-related brain activation and connectivity; meets inclusion criteria and no exclusion criteria clearly apply. Full text can confirm age range and details.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is a task-based fMRI study (mental addition) with group-level, whole-brain analyses (SPM8 parametric conjunctions) reporting activation peaks used as seed coordinates (MNI) and listing seed region coordinates. The task involves multi-digit addition including carry operations (multi-step arithmetic problem solving). The fMRI sample comprises healthy adults (16 right-handed males, mean age 28), reported separately from the DTI sample. Results include whole-brain activation maps and peak voxels/coordinates for problem-related contrasts. The study is empirical, not ROI-only, not connectivity-only or resting-state only (DTI was also used but fMRI whole-brain results are present). Therefore it satisfies inclusion criteria I5, I6, I7, and I8.

studies.title: Environmental influences on neural systems of relational complexity.
studies.abstract: Constructivist learning theory contends that we construct knowledge by experience and that environmental context influences learning. To explore this principle, we examined the cognitive process relational complexity (RC), defined as the number of visual dimensions considered during problem solving on a matrix reasoning task and a well-documented measure of mature reasoning capacity. We sought to determine how the visual environment influences RC by examining the influence of color and visual contrast on RC in a neuroimaging task. To specify the contributions of sensory demand and relational integration to reasoning, our participants performed a non-verbal matrix task comprised of color, no-color line, or black-white visual contrast conditions parametrically varied by complexity (relations 0, 1, 2). The use of matrix reasoning is ecologically valid for its psychometric relevance and for its potential to link the processing of psychophysically specific visual properties with various levels of RC during reasoning. The role of these elements is important because matrix tests assess intellectual aptitude based on these seemingly context-less exercises. This experiment is a first step toward examining the psychophysical underpinnings of performance on these types of problems. The importance of this is increased in light of recent evidence that intelligence can be linked to visual discrimination. We submit three main findings. First, color and black-white visual contrast (BWVC) add demand at a basic sensory level, but contributions from color and from BWVC are dissociable in cortex such that color engages a "reasoning heuristic" and BWVC engages a "sensory heuristic." Second, color supports contextual sense-making by boosting salience resulting in faster problem solving. Lastly, when visual complexity reaches 2-relations, color and visual contrast relinquish salience to other dimensions of problem solving.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Abstract describes a neuroimaging study of a non-verbal matrix reasoning (problem-solving) task and reports cortical activation patterns; however, it does not explicitly state fMRI or participant ages. Include for full-text review to confirm fMRI modality and healthy adult sample.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is a task-based, event-related fMRI study using a matrix-reasoning (relational complexity) problem-solving task (0/1/2 relations) in healthy adult participants (N=34, age 18–46). The authors report group-level random-effects whole-brain voxel-based analyses with FDR correction and report peak activation coordinates (tables with Brodmann areas and Talairach/MNI coordinates) and ROI/whole-brain results for problem-solving contrasts (e.g., 2-relational vs 1-relational; condition contrasts). The task involves multi-step visuospatial relational reasoning, not a simple perceptual or resting-state paradigm. Thus it meets all inclusion criteria for whole-brain, task-based fMRI of problem-solving in healthy adults.

studies.title: Large-scale brain networks in board game experts: insights from a domain-related task and task-free resting state.
studies.abstract: Cognitive performance relies on the coordination of large-scale networks of brain regions that are not only temporally correlated during different tasks, but also networks that show highly correlated spontaneous activity during a task-free state. Both task-related and task-free network activity has been associated with individual differences in cognitive performance. Therefore, we aimed to examine the influence of cognitive expertise on four networks associated with cognitive task performance: the default mode network (DMN) and three other cognitive networks (central-executive network, dorsal attention network, and salience network). During fMRI scanning, fifteen grandmaster and master level Chinese chess players (GM/M) and fifteen novice players carried out a Chinese chess task and a task-free resting state. Modulations of network activity during task were assessed, as well as resting-state functional connectivity of those networks. Relative to novices, GM/Ms showed a broader task-induced deactivation of DMN in the chess problem-solving task, and intrinsic functional connectivity of DMN was increased with a connectivity pattern associated with the caudate nucleus in GM/Ms. The three other cognitive networks did not exhibit any difference in task-evoked activation or intrinsic functional connectivity between the two groups. These findings demonstrate the effect of long-term learning and practice in cognitive expertise on large-scale brain networks, suggesting the important role of DMN deactivation in expert performance and enhanced functional integration of spontaneous activity within widely distributed DMN-caudate circuitry, which might better support high-level cognitive control of behavior.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: This is a task-based fMRI study in which healthy adult grandmaster/master and novice participants performed a Chinese chess problem-solving task; task-evoked network modulations (DMN deactivation) and resting-state connectivity are reported. Thus it meets I1–I4.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This study reports a task-based fMRI Chinese-chess problem-solving experiment (multi-step reasoning), with group-level whole-brain GLM random-effects analyses and between-/within-group contrasts. Participants are healthy adult groups (GM/M and novices, ages within 18–65). Peak coordinates are reported in MNI space (e.g., PCC 10,-52,28; caudate coordinates provided). Thus it satisfies all inclusion criteria for whole-brain task fMRI problem-solving in healthy adults.

studies.title: Dynamic neural network of insight: a functional magnetic resonance imaging study on solving Chinese 'chengyu' riddles.
studies.abstract: The key components of insight include breaking mental sets and forming the novel, task-related associations. The majority of researchers have agreed that the anterior cingulate cortex may mediate processes of breaking one's mental set, while the exact neural correlates of forming novel associations are still debatable. In the present study, we used a paradigm of answer selection to explore brain activations of insight by using event-related functional magnetic resonance imaging during solving Chinese 'chengyu' (in Chinese pinyin) riddles. Based on the participant's choice, the trials were classified into the insight and non-insight conditions. Both stimulus-locked and response-locked analyses are conducted to detect the neural activity corresponding to the early and late periods of insight solution, respectively. Our data indicate that the early period of insight solution shows more activation in the middle temporal gyrus, the middle frontal gyrus and the anterior cingulate cortex. These activities might be associated to the extensive semantic processing, as well as detecting and resolving cognitive conflicts. In contrast, the late period of insight solution produced increased activities in the hippocampus and the amygdala, possibly reflecting the forming of novel association and the concomitant "Aha" feeling. Our study supports the key role of hippocampus in forming novel associations, and indicates a dynamic neural network during insight solution.
studies.abstract_screening_reason: Event-related, task-based fMRI study in which human participants solved Chinese 'chengyu' riddles (a problem-solving task). The abstract reports task-related brain activations (early/late periods). Participant health status and exact ages are not specified, so include for full-text review to confirm I3.
studies.fulltext_screening_reason: This is a task-based, event-related fMRI study with group-level (random effects) whole-brain voxelwise analyses reporting significant activation peaks. The task is a verbal problem-solving (Chinese ‘chengyu’ riddles) requiring constraint relaxation/chunk decomposition and selection—i.e. multi-step/problem-solving reasoning. Participants are healthy adults aged 21–35. Peak coordinates are reported in MNI space (e.g., left middle temporal gyrus at (–52,–30,–2)) for insight vs. non-insight contrasts. Systematic review/meta-analysis of 18F-FDG PET imaging in uterine sarcomas. It is a review (E1), involves PET not fMRI (E4), and concerns cancer patients rather than healthy adults (E3), so does not meet the review objective.
Participants are children (ages 8–12); the study does not include a healthy adult (18–65) sample required by the review. Therefore it does not meet the population criterion for inclusion.

This study describes PET imaging physics and Monte Carlo simulations of isotopes (124I, 86Y) for 3-D PET, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It does not involve fMRI, problem-solving tasks, or report task-related brain activation results.

This is a theoretical/review-style paper summarizing evidence about the anterior cingulate cortex rather than reporting a primary task-based fMRI experiment of problem solving in healthy adults; it therefore meets the review/meta-analytic exclusion.

This is a 2006 meta-analysis of PET and CT diagnostic imaging in non-small cell lung cancer patients. It is not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving, involves patient (cancer) populations rather than healthy adults, and is a meta-analysis.

Abstract describes a theoretical/review paper proposing the 4CAPS neuroarchitecture and comparing existing imaging/behavioral results, not a primary task-based fMRI study reporting new task-related activation data. It fits the 'review' exclusion (E1).

Abstract reports an event-related task-based fMRI study of deductive reasoning in human participants and describes task-related cortical activation patterns. The abstract does not specify participant ages or explicitly state ‘healthy’, so full text review is needed to confirm demographics, but the study meets the review focus and reports activations.
This is a meta-analysis of FDG-PET for breast cancer recurrence — it is a review/meta-analysis (E1), uses PET rather than fMRI (E4), and involves patient samples not healthy adults (E3).

This is a PET activation study (positron emission tomography), not an fMRI study. Although it involves healthy adults performing tasks and reports activations, it fails the modality requirement (non-fMRI), so it meets exclusion criterion E4.
This is a PET tracer development and imaging study in tumor models, not a task-based fMRI study. It involves no healthy adult human participants and does not report task-related brain activation.

This is a review article on fMRI findings in autism (not an original task-based fMRI study), and the sample is autism patients rather than healthy adults. It therefore meets E1 (review) and E3 (no healthy adult sample).

Abstract describes a task-based fMRI study of abstract reasoning in alcohol-dependent subjects (satisfying I1 and I2). The abstract does not state whether a healthy adult control group (I3) was included or explicitly report task-related activations (I4). Insufficient information to determine presence of healthy adults, so include for full-text review.

This study is an animal micro-CT methods paper on imaging liver lesions in small animals (mice). It is not a task-based fMRI study, does not involve human healthy adults, and does not report task-related brain activation. Therefore it does not meet the review criteria.

Abstract reads as a synthesis/theoretical review of the MD system, referencing single-cell and fMRI data rather than reporting an original task-based fMRI experiment in healthy adult participants. Therefore it meets the exclusion criterion for reviews/meta-analyses.

Abstract describes a task-based fMRI study where participants in an MR scanner performed syllogistic reasoning (problem-solving) with emotional content; reports task-related brain activation. Participants are human and presumed healthy adults. Meets I1–I4.

This is a narrative review about sonography in oncology, not an empirical task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It is a review article and not an fMRI study, so it meets exclusion criteria E1 and E4.

Event-related task-based fMRI study reporting task-related brain activation during verbal problem solving (Chinese chengyu riddles). Abstract confirms human participants performing a problem-solving task and reports activation results. Participant health/age not specified, so include for full-text review to confirm I3.

Abstract describes methodological discussion of fMRI/EEG approaches to insight problem solving but does not report specific task-based fMRI experiments, participant ages, or task-related activation results. Insufficient information to determine whether I1, I3, and I4 are met; include for full-text review.

Participants are individuals with amnestic mild cognitive impairment (aMCI); no healthy adult (18–65) group is reported. Tasks are visual working memory (Delayed-Recognition, One-Back), not explicit problem-solving. Primary exclusion: absence of healthy adult sample.

Task-based fMRI in healthy adult participants performing an integral calculus (problem-solving) task; abstract reports task-related brain activation. Satisfies all inclusion criteria and no exclusion criteria apply.

This study is a PET (15O-gas) methods/clinical paper involving patients with cerebrovascular disease—not a task-based fMRI study—and does not include healthy adult participants. Fails E4 (non-fMRI) and E3 (no healthy adult sample).

Abstract describes comparison of CT, MR, and FDG-PET for thoracic lymph node assessment in non-small-cell lung cancer; not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It is a non-fMRI imaging paper and reads like a review. Exclude on those grounds.

This is an event-related, task-based fMRI study in 14 healthy adult volunteers performing deductive reasoning/problem-solving (belief-bias) tasks and reporting task-related brain activation. It satisfies inclusion criteria I1–I4 and none of the exclusion criteria are met based on the abstract.

Abstract describes a task-based fMRI experiment on humans performing deductive reasoning and reports task-related brain activations (visual association cortex, parietal, temporal, frontal). The abstract does not state participant ages or explicitly state 'healthy adults', so include for full-text to confirm I3.

The study reports task-based fMRI in patients with disorders of consciousness using mental imagery for yes/no communication. No healthy adult (18–65) sample is described, and the tasks are communication-based rather than problem-solving in healthy adults. Exclusion based on absence of healthy adult participants.

The study investigates PET and triple-phase bone scan diagnostics in patients with hip arthroplasty loosening—not a task-based fMRI study—and involves clinical patients rather than healthy adult participants. It therefore fails inclusion and meets exclusion criteria for non-fMRI methods and lack of healthy adult sample.

Clinical imaging review of cervical pathology, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. Focuses on MRI/PET-CT/ultrasonography for cancer staging and benign pathology; appears to be a review article and does not report task-related fMRI activations in healthy adult problem-solving participants.

Event-related task-based fMRI of syllogistic deductive reasoning in humans; reports task-related activation and time courses in frontal, parietal, and caudate regions, consistent with problem-solving studies in healthy adults.

The study uses FDG whole-body PET (not fMRI) and examines oncology patients for staging, not healthy adults performing problem-solving tasks; it does not report task-related fMRI activation relevant to the review.

This is a case report using diffusion whole-body MRI in a patient with breast cancer and sarcoidosis — not a task-based fMRI study, and it does not include healthy adult participants. The imaging modality is not fMRI. Exclude based on modality and participant sample.
This study uses FDG PET in patients with polymyositis/dermatomyositis, not task-based fMRI, and does not involve healthy adult problem-solving tasks. Therefore it does not meet the review objective.

This is a systematic review of PET/PET-CT for breast cancer recurrence (a review, non-fMRI, patient population). It is a review article, not a task-based fMRI study, involves cancer patients rather than healthy adults, and uses non-fMRI imaging modalities. Exclusion criteria E1, E4, and E3 apply.
Task-based fMRI study of inductive/problem-solving (pattern discovery) in 20 healthy young adults reporting task-related brain activations. All inclusion criteria are met based on the abstract.

Methods/toolbox paper focused on multimodal connectivity and resting-state fMRI; it does not report task-based fMRI of problem-solving, nor task-related activation results. Therefore it meets exclusion criterion E2 (resting-state/connectivity-only).

Task-based fMRI study of healthy participants performing deductive reasoning; reports task-related BOLD activation in multiple brain regions. Age range not specified in abstract, so include for full-text review to confirm participants are adults (18–65).

This article is a clinical review of imaging techniques for radiotherapy planning in head and neck cancer, not an original task-based fMRI study of problem-solving in healthy adults. It meets exclusion criterion E1 (review/meta-analysis).

This is a review article on FDG PET in pediatric patients (not task-based fMRI), focuses on pediatric epilepsy and brain tumors rather than problem-solving in healthy adults, and does not report task-based fMRI activation results. Therefore it meets multiple exclusion criteria.

This is a review article about clinical/imaging tools for early prostate cancer detection (not an original task-based fMRI study), does not involve problem-solving tasks or healthy adult fMRI, and is non-fMRI. Therefore it meets multiple exclusion criteria.

This is an invited mini-review focused on PET receptor-occupancy methodology, not an original task-based fMRI study of problem solving in humans. It is a review article and concerns PET (non-fMRI) imaging, so it meets exclusion criteria E1 and E4.

Task-based fMRI study where human subjects performed deductive reasoning (Wason selection task). Reports task-related brain activation (frontal/parietal regions). Participants appear to be healthy adults; no exclusion criteria met.

Task-based fMRI with healthy adult participants (seven adults) reporting task-related activation. The task involved generating usual vs. unusual noun-verb relations, which may reflect problem-solving/creative reasoning but is not explicitly labeled as a standard problem-solving task in the abstract—insufficient detail to exclude. Include for full-text review to confirm whether the task meets the review's problem-solving definition.

Abstract mentions inductive reasoning and age-related brain activity but does not specify imaging modality, participant ages, or whether task-related fMRI activation results are reported. Insufficient information to confirm all inclusion criteria, so include for full-text review.

This study used PET imaging and investigated cerebrovascular autoregulation in patients with carotid artery stenosis, not a task-based fMRI problem-solving study. Participants are clinical patients rather than healthy adults, and no task-related fMRI activations are reported.

The abstract describes a task-based fMRI study of inductive reasoning (problem solving) in 26 healthy young adults and reports task-related brain activation results for rule acquisition and following. It meets the review’s inclusion criteria I1–I4.
This is a PET imaging study of tumor patients (meningiomas/gliomas), not a task-based fMRI study and does not include healthy adult participants. Therefore it does not meet the review criteria.

This is a clinical retrospective PET study of renal cell carcinoma, not a task-based fMRI study, does not involve problem-solving tasks, and does not report results from healthy adult participants. It therefore fails inclusion and meets exclusion criteria.

This is a PET imaging study of blood flow and glucose metabolism in musculoskeletal tumors (patient sample), not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It does not involve healthy participants performing problem-solving tasks or report task-related fMRI activation.

Task-based fMRI study of number/letter series completion (inductive reasoning). Human participants performed problem-solving tasks and authors report BOLD activation results. Abstract does not specify participant age/health, so full text needed to confirm healthy adult sample.

This is a PET imaging study in esophageal cancer patients undergoing neoadjuvant therapy—not a task-based fMRI study, does not involve problem-solving tasks, and the sample comprises patients rather than healthy adults.

The abstract describes PET studies in oncology patients (malignant disease), not task-based fMRI, and does not involve healthy adults performing problem-solving tasks. It therefore fails the fMRI requirement and lacks a healthy adult sample.

Task-based event-related fMRI study of semantic and analogical problem solving in humans; includes a young adult group and reports task-related brain activation results (age-related changes in prefrontal and temporal regions).

This is a review article on advanced imaging for cervical cancer (CT, MRI, FDG-PET), not a task-based fMRI study. It involves patient/clinical populations (not healthy adults) and reports non-fMRI modalities, meeting exclusions E1, E3, and E4.
Study reports PET tracer kinetic methods and blood-sampling issues, not task-based fMRI or problem-solving in healthy adults. It's a non-fMRI imaging methods study, so excluded.

Task-based fMRI study with adults performing an arithmetic (addition) task—this is a problem-solving/ reasoning task. Healthy adult participants are implied, and the abstract reports task-related brain activation results from fMRI. No exclusion criteria are met.

Abstract reports a task-based fMRI study in humans using Raven's Progressive Matrices (a problem-solving/reasoning task) and reports task-related brain activation. Participant ages described as 'young, healthy' but exact ages not given; include for full-text confirmation of age range.

This is a narrative review on imaging of pancreatic adenocarcinoma (staging/resectability), not an original task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults; it does not report task-related fMRI activation.

This is a prospective oncology imaging study using FDG-PET in cervical cancer patients, not an fMRI study of problem-solving in healthy adults. Participants are cancer patients and no task-based fMRI/problem-solving results are reported.

Abstract reports task-based fMRI while human subjects performed a sequential game requiring recursive problem-solving/backward induction, and describes task-related brain activation (ventrolateral PFC, reward system). Age range not specified in abstract, but all other inclusion criteria are clearly met; include for full-text to confirm participant ages/health.

Abstract/title indicate a task-based fMRI study of problem-solving after severe TBI, but the abstract does not state whether a healthy adult control group is included or whether task-related activation results are reported. Include for full-text review to confirm presence of healthy adults and activation results.

The study uses a simple motor paradigm to target brainstem motor nuclei, not a problem-solving or reasoning task. While it is task-based fMRI with healthy adults and reports activation, it does not address the review objective (problem solving).

This is an event-related task-based fMRI study in twenty right-handed adult humans performing numerical inductive reasoning (a problem-solving/reasoning task) and reports task-related brain activation results (SPL, DLPFC, fronto-parietal, striatal-thalamic).

Abstract reports use of fMRI to localize the cognitive control network for BCI, but does not specify that participants were healthy adults or that the task was a problem-solving/reasoning task. Insufficient detail to confirm I2 and I3, so include for full-text review.

Abstract describes a task-based fMRI study of human problem-solving (word vs number problems) and reports task-related neuroimaging activation results. The abstract does not specify participant ages/health, so full text is needed to confirm healthy adult sample (include for full-text review).

Abstract describes FDG-PET imaging for infection detection in patient populations. It is not a task-based fMRI study and does not involve healthy adults performing problem-solving tasks. Exclusion criteria E4 (non-fMRI study) and E3 (no healthy adult sample) apply.

Task-based fMRI study of inductive reasoning (a problem-solving/reasoning task) that reports task-related activation and connectivity. Includes a ‘normal aging’ healthy group, but age range (18–65) is not specified—include for full-text review to confirm adult age inclusion.

This is a review article about immuno-SPECT/CT and immuno-PET/CT imaging in oncology, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving. It does not involve fMRI, problem-solving tasks, or healthy adult fMRI data.

This study uses diffusion-weighted MRI and PET/CT for lung biopsy planning in (likely) patient populations and is not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It does not involve problem-solving tasks nor report task-related fMRI activations in healthy adults.

Abstract describes a methods paper on online spatial normalization for real-time fMRI and does not specify a problem-solving or reasoning task or details about healthy adult participants. Insufficient information to confirm inclusion, so include for full-text review.

This is a task-based fMRI study of mental calculation (a problem-solving task) comparing abacus experts and nonexperts; it reports task-related brain activation in human participants. Although ages are not stated in the abstract, participants are adults by context—include for full-text confirmation.

Task-based fMRI study of algebraic problem solving in human participants; reports task-related hemodynamic activity in prefrontal, cingulate, and caudate regions and includes computational modeling of activation time course. The abstract does not specify participant ages or explicit statement of 'healthy adults', so full text should be checked for I3.

This is a PET image reconstruction methods paper using scanner/phantom measurements (microPET II), not a task-based fMRI study and does not include healthy adult participants performing problem-solving tasks. Therefore it does not meet inclusion criteria.
This is an engineering study on magnetic field calculations for a PET cyclotron with no human participants and not an fMRI task-based problem-solving study, so it does not meet inclusion criteria.

The study used PET ([15O]H2O) neuroimaging, not task-based fMRI, therefore it does not meet the fMRI inclusion criterion and meets exclusion E4. Abstract otherwise describes healthy adults performing reasoning tasks with reported activations, but the imaging modality excludes it.

Task-based fMRI study of exact addition/subtraction (problem solving) with a group of 11 healthy, age-matched controls and reported task-related brain activation results (bilateral parietal cortices and other activations). Meets all inclusion criteria.

Task-based fMRI study of fluid reasoning in humans reporting task-related BOLD activation and specific brain regions. Abstract does not specify participants' ages or health status, so include for full-text review to confirm presence of healthy adult group.

This study uses FDG-PET (not fMRI) and reports findings in phenylketonuria patients undergoing sapropterin therapy — it does not involve healthy adult participants performing a problem-solving task. Therefore it does not meet the review's fMRI or healthy adult task-based criteria.

Abstract reports a task-based fMRI study where participants performed a mental arithmetic (problem-solving) task and task-related activation values are reported and correlated with DTI metrics. Participant age/health is not explicitly stated in the abstract, so full-text review is needed to confirm presence of healthy adults.

Abstract describes task-based BOLD fMRI investigations of working memory including healthy controls and reports task-related cerebral activation, but it does not specify that the task is a problem-solving/reasoning task nor the ages of healthy participants. Insufficient information to confirm I2 and I3, so include for full-text review.

This is a methodological paper proposing an image registration algorithm applied to fMRI images; it does not report a task-based fMRI study of humans performing problem-solving or reasoning, contains no healthy adult participants or task-related activation results.

The abstract describes a task-based fMRI study of numerical inductive reasoning (number-series completion) and reports task-related activations in prefrontal and parietal regions. Participant health/age is not specified in the abstract, so include for full-text review to confirm healthy adult sample.

The abstract describes an event-related task-based fMRI study where human participants solved simplified Sudoku (a problem-solving task) and reports task-related BOLD activation in specific ROIs. Participant health/age is not specified in the abstract, so include for full-text review to confirm healthy adult sample.

Task-based fMRI study of covert mental subtraction in human subjects reporting task-related activations. The abstract does not specify participants' ages or health status, so include for full-text review to confirm presence of healthy adults (18-65).
This is a preclinical PET dosimetry study in mice (64Cu tracer); it is not an fMRI study and includes no healthy adult human participants. Does not meet the review objective.

Task-based BOLD fMRI study in healthy adult participants (14 men, 13 women) performing a verbal-reasoning (analogies) and a spatial (line orientation) task; reports task-related brain activation results. Meets all inclusion criteria and no exclusion criteria apply.

This is an animal (alpha-chloralose anesthetized rats) fMRI study of forepaw stimulation, not a task-based problem-solving study in human healthy adults. Therefore it does not include a healthy adult human sample and is outside the review scope.

This is a task-based fMRI study reporting task-related brain activations during a creative generation/evaluation task. The abstract indicates participants performed an active design task (generation and evaluation), but it does not specify participant ages/health explicitly. Include for full-text review to confirm healthy adult sample and whether the task meets the review's definition of problem solving.

The study is a methods comparison of MRI sequences and reports functional results from motor stimulation, not a problem-solving or reasoning task. Therefore it does not meet inclusion criterion I2 (problem-solving task), despite being task-based fMRI.

Abstract reports an event-related task-based fMRI study (16 subjects) performing inductive and deductive reasoning tasks and describes task-related brain activations. Age/health not explicitly stated in abstract, but it clearly meets I1, I2, and I4; full text should confirm I3 (healthy adults).

Event-related task-based fMRI in healthy participants performing syllogistic reasoning (a problem-solving/reasoning task) with reported task-related brain activation results. All inclusion criteria are clearly met; no exclusion criteria apply.

This is a methodological paper introducing a power-calculation model for group fMRI studies; the abstract does not report any task-based fMRI experiment, participant sample, or task-related brain activation in healthy adults performing problem-solving or reasoning tasks. Does not meet inclusion criteria I2–I4.

Abstract reports an event-related, task-based fMRI study of insight/problem solving (Chinese logogriphs) and reports task-related BOLD activation contrasts (Aha vs No-aha). These satisfy I1, I2, and I4. The abstract does not explicitly state participant ages or confirm they are healthy adults, so include for full-text review to confirm I3.

This study uses PET to validate a tissue oxygenation sensor in brain-injured patients; it is not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. Therefore it does not meet the fMRI requirement.

Abstract reports a task-based fMRI study in human participants solving mathematical problems (NAOT), describes young healthy participants (adult sample) and reports task-related brain activation results. This satisfies all inclusion criteria and no exclusion criteria apply.

Abstract describes presurgical fMRI and implanted ECoG electrodes, indicating a clinical (presurgical/patient) sample rather than healthy adults. Thus the study lacks a healthy adult sample required by I3.

Abstract describes a task-based fMRI study of problem solving (Tower of London) with participants performing planning/execution; reports behavioral and task-related fMRI activation results. Age of participants/explicit statement of healthy adult sample not provided in abstract, so full text is needed to confirm I3 (18–65).
This is a PET study in hyperacute ischemic stroke patients measuring CBF/OEF/CMRO2, not a task-based fMRI study and not conducted in healthy adults. Does not meet fMRI or healthy adult criteria.

The study concerns PET-based range verification in particle therapy using patient datasets and Monte Carlo predictions — a clinical imaging/medical physics study, not task-based fMRI of problem solving in healthy adults. It is a non-fMRI study and does not include healthy adult cognitive tasks.

This is a PET study of pancreatic methionine uptake and secretion in healthy volunteers, not a task-based fMRI study and it does not involve problem-solving or reasoning tasks. Exclude due to non-fMRI modality.

Participants are described as healthy elderly adults (older than typical adult range), which does not meet the inclusion age range (18–65). Study otherwise uses task-based fMRI, but exclusion E3 applies.

This study is a PET imaging study in patients with squamous cell carcinoma, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It uses non-fMRI methods and includes patient samples rather than healthy adult participants.

This is a PET physics Monte Carlo simulation study focused on positron range and system resolution, not a task-based fMRI experiment; no human participants performing problem-solving tasks or task-related brain activation are reported.

Abstract describes a task-based fMRI study of scientific problem solving and reports task-related activations (MTG, MOG). Participant health status and ages are not specified, so include for full-text review to confirm presence of healthy adults (18-65).

Task-based fMRI study of the Tower of Hanoi problem-solving task in human participants reporting BOLD activation in specific brain regions. The abstract does not state participant ages/health explicitly, so include for full-text review to confirm the healthy adult (18–65) sample (I3).

This is a clinical imaging article/review about detection of bone metastases in cancer patients (nuclear medicine/PET/CT), not a task-based fMRI study; it has no healthy adult problem-solving task sample and appears to be a review.

Abstract describes a task-based fMRI study (MVPA/HMM) where human participants performed mathematical problem-solving and reports task-related activations in specific brain regions. The abstract does not specify participant ages or explicitly state a healthy adult sample, so include for full-text review to confirm I3.
