This is a clinical review about imaging of gastrointestinal stromal tumors (CT, PET, MRI) and does not report task-based fMRI experiments or problem-solving tasks in healthy adults. It is a non-fMRI review article.

This study uses rubidium-82 PET myocardial perfusion imaging in patients evaluated for coronary artery disease — it is non-fMRI and involves clinical patient population rather than healthy adults, so it does not meet the review criteria.

This study is a PET pharmacokinetic modeling paper using arterial/venous blood sampling to predict brain drug concentrations; it is not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults and does not report task-related activation.

Study focuses on Turner syndrome (a genetic developmental disorder); abstract does not indicate inclusion of a healthy adult (18–65) sample. Therefore it does not meet the requirement for healthy adult participants.
This study uses PET ([15O]-butanol) rather than fMRI. The review requires task-based fMRI studies, so it fails inclusion criterion I1 and meets exclusion criterion E4 (non-fMRI study).

This is a PET/CT methods paper on attenuation correction (non-fMRI). It does not report task-based fMRI of problem solving in healthy adults, so it fails the fMRI requirement. Exclusion based on non-fMRI modality.

This is an animal (healthy rats) study using PET and MRI to measure cardiac (left ventricular) function. It is not a task-based fMRI study in humans performing problem-solving. Does not meet inclusion and meets exclusion criteria for non-human samples and non-fMRI modality.

Study uses PET myocardial perfusion imaging in individuals with known or suspected coronary artery disease; it is not an fMRI study and does not involve problem-solving tasks or a healthy adult sample.

The study reports task-based fMRI on two calendrical savants but the abstract does not indicate any healthy adult (18–65) participant group or controls. Therefore it does not meet the inclusion requirement for a healthy adult sample (I3).

Participants are school-age children (9–14) only; the review requires inclusion of healthy adults (18–65). Although this is a task-based fMRI study reporting reasoning-related activations, no adult sample is present, so it must be excluded.

Abstract describes a task-based fMRI study of participants interacting with an intelligent tutoring system solving algebra problems and reports brain-activity prediction results. Participant age/health not specified, so include for full-text review to confirm healthy adult sample.

Task-based fMRI study with 28 adult participants performing arithmetic problem-solving; reports task-related brain activation (left angular gyrus) linked to fact retrieval and procedural strategies. Sample described as adults (healthy) and the abstract clearly reports task activation results, satisfying inclusion criteria.

This is a resting-state fMRI connectivity study of intrinsic networks, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving or reasoning. No task-related activation results for problem solving are reported. Excluded per E2.

This study used PET/MRI to estimate absorbed radiation dose of 18F-FDG in healthy volunteers; it is not a task-based fMRI study, does not involve a problem-solving or reasoning task, and does not report task-related brain activation.

This is a methodological MRI study quantifying whole-brain CMRO2 in (resting) healthy subjects. It does not report task-based fMRI, any problem-solving or reasoning task, or task-related brain activation results. It is not an fMRI study of cognitive tasks.
This is a PET imaging methods paper on partial-volume correction for striatal PET; it is not an fMRI study nor does it report task-based problem-solving in healthy adults. Excluded as non-fMRI study.

Animal PET imaging study in mice of myocardial infarction and PTH treatment; not task-based fMRI, not human participants, and does not involve problem-solving tasks. Therefore it fails inclusion criteria and meets exclusion criteria E4 and E3.

This study is a clinical PET imaging study of recurrent cervical carcinoma in cancer patients. It is not a task-based fMRI study and does not include healthy adult participants performing problem-solving tasks, so it does not meet the review objectives.

This study is a PET neuropharmacology study in patients with major depressive disorder measuring NET occupancy with nortriptyline. It is not a task-based fMRI study, does not examine problem-solving tasks, and does not include healthy adult participants.

This study is a PET imaging study of bone and soft-tissue tumors in patient populations, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It therefore does not include healthy adult participants nor use fMRI.

Abstract reports a task-based fMRI study using a probabilistic reversal learning task and links ventral striatal task-related BOLD signals to fluid intelligence. However, the abstract does not state participant health status or ages, and it is unclear if the task meets the review's ‘problem solving/reasoning’ definition. Include for full-text review to confirm I2 and I3.

This is a task-based fMRI study in humans where participants (including a healthy adult group) performed calculation/problem-solving tasks and the paper reports task-related brain activation, meeting I1–I4.
This is a PET study (positron emission tomography), not an fMRI study. The review requires task-based fMRI; therefore it meets exclusion criterion E4 (non-fMRI studies).

Abstract describes task-based fMRI during mathematical problem solving, reports task-related activation (HIPS), and involves human participants performing problem-solving tasks. The abstract does not specify participant ages or explicit healthy status, so include for full-text review to confirm criterion I3.

Meets all inclusion criteria: task-based fMRI measuring brain activity during a modified Raven's Progressive Matrices (problem-solving/reasoning task), includes a matched healthy adult control group, and reports task-related brain activation results.

Abstract specifies the dataset was taken from children interacting with an intelligent tutoring system; no adult (18–65) sample is reported. Does not meet the requirement for healthy adult participants.

This is a PET methodological paper developing uncertainty estimates for PET activation localization; it is not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. Therefore it is a non-fMRI study and excluded.

This is a resting-state fMRI study of Alzheimer’s disease patients examining network small-world properties, not a task-based problem-solving fMRI study in healthy adults; it does not report task-related activations.

Abstract reports a task-based fMRI study using the Tower of London problem-solving task in human participants, describes brain activation results (prefrontal and parietal activation) and individual differences, and clearly involves healthy adult participants. Meets all inclusion criteria.

This is a PET imaging study in monkeys measuring dopamine D2 receptor properties, not a task-based fMRI study in human adults performing problem-solving or reasoning tasks. It therefore does not include healthy adult fMRI data relevant to the review.

This study is an imaging study of FDG-PET in lung cancer patients—not a task-based fMRI study of problem-solving—and participants are patients rather than healthy adults, so it meets exclusion criteria E4 and E3.

The study concerns PET imaging and radiotherapy treatment planning (SUV calculation and 4D PET integration), not task-based fMRI of problem solving in healthy adults. It is a non-fMRI imaging study, so it does not meet the review objective.

Task-based fMRI study of deductive reasoning/relational complexity in humans reporting task-related brain activity and connectivity. Participants appear to be healthy adults performing a problem-solving/reasoning task; results include task-related activations. Meets inclusion criteria for task-based fMRI, problem-solving task, healthy adult sample, and reported activation results.

Abstract describes a task-based fMRI experiment on deductive/transitive inference reasoning with twelve human volunteers and reports task-related brain activations. The abstract does not specify participant ages or explicitly state they are healthy adults, so include for full-text review to confirm I3.

This study used PET to measure H1 receptor occupancy after antihistamine administration and assessed next-day sedation, not a task-based fMRI of problem solving in healthy adults. Therefore it is a non-fMRI study and does not meet the review objective.
This is a PET (FDG-PET) imaging study of liver tumours, not a task-based fMRI study. It does not involve problem-solving tasks or report task-related brain activation results relevant to the review.

The abstract describes methodological analysis of fMRI signals (spectral/coherency/connectivity) and a calculation method for interactions between brain areas. It does not report a task-based problem-solving/reasoning experiment, nor mention healthy adult participants or task-related activation results. Excluded as connectivity/methods-focused (E2).

This is a medical physics study measuring radiation dose rates from 18F-FDG in oncology patients, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving. It does not involve healthy adult participants and is not fMRI-based, so it fails inclusion criteria.

This is an educational/course overview on non-invasive imaging in toxicologic pathology focused on small animal/preclinical imaging. It is not a task-based fMRI study in healthy adult humans and does not report task-related brain activation results.
The study uses H2[15]O PET combined with transcranial Doppler (non-fMRI modality). The review requires task-based fMRI studies, so this clearly meets exclusion criterion E4 (non-fMRI).

This study investigates pain/thermal stimulation and insular functional connectivity, not a problem-solving or reasoning task. It does not address the review objective (task-based fMRI of problem solving in healthy adults).

Task-based fMRI study reporting task-related brain activation during symbolic exact/approximate calculation and magnitude comparison; includes a healthy adult group (20 adults); tasks involve problem-solving/mental arithmetic.
Study is an animal (rat) experiment using PET/CT, not an fMRI study, and does not involve healthy adult human participants or a problem-solving task. Therefore it does not meet inclusion criteria.
The study included only patients with schizophrenia (both RECOS and TAU groups); no healthy adult sample is reported, so it does not meet the requirement for healthy adults.

Abstract describes a task-based fMRI study of arithmetic calculation tasks and reports laterality (task-related brain activation). Participant health status and exact ages are not specified in the abstract, so full text is needed to confirm presence of healthy adults (I3). Include for full-text review.
Task-based fMRI study of mental arithmetic (problem solving), includes a healthy adult group (ages 40-49), and reports task-related brain activation results. No exclusion criteria apply.

Task-based fMRI study in humans examining deductive reasoning and mathematical problem solving; reports task-related brain activation results. Although ages are not specified in the abstract, the study appears to involve healthy adult participants and meets the review inclusion criteria.

This is a PET attenuation-correction methods paper using phantom data, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving and includes no healthy adult participants or task-related brain activation results.

Task-based fMRI study in humans using Raven's Standard Progressive Matrices (a reasoning/problem-solving task). Includes a non-autistic healthy adult control group and reports task-related brain activation differences. No exclusion criteria apply.

This is a task-based fMRI study in healthy young adults performing an arithmetic strategy-selection task (a problem-solving/reasoning task). The abstract reports task-related brain activations. It therefore meets inclusion criteria I1, I2, I3, and I4.

This is a methodological/power calculation guide, not an empirical task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It does not report participant data or task-related activation results, so it meets exclusion criterion E1 (review/methods paper).

This is a PET imaging study of malignant lymphoma in cancer patients using FLT tracer. It is not a task-based fMRI study, involves patients not healthy adults, and reports no task-related brain activation.
This is an ERP/EEG study (event-related potentials), not a task-based fMRI study, so it does not meet the fMRI requirement (E4).

This is an animal PET study in anesthetized monkeys measuring CBF and CMRO2, not a task-based fMRI study of human problem solving. It does not include healthy adult human participants or report task-related brain activation; therefore it fails inclusion criteria and should be excluded.

Methods paper describes a toolbox and uses fMRI data from 12 subjects but does not mention problem-solving/reasoning tasks or clearly state healthy adult sample. Abstract provides insufficient information to determine if it meets the review’s task-based problem-solving criterion; include for full-text review.

The study examines FDG-PET uptake in oral cancer patients, not task-based fMRI or problem-solving in healthy adults. Participants are cancer patients (not healthy adults), and the modality is PET (not fMRI).

This is a methodological PET/SPECT attenuation-correction study using simulated and patient emission data, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It does not involve fMRI or problem-solving tasks.

Task-based fMRI study reporting task-related brain activations during mental arithmetic (problem solving) and finger discrimination in adult participants. Meets all inclusion criteria (task-based fMRI, problem-solving task, healthy adult sample, reports activation results). No exclusion criteria met.

Methods/software paper describing an NIRS statistical toolbox; not a task-based fMRI study of problem-solving in healthy adults. Uses NIRS (optical) recordings with finger-tapping and memory tasks, so it is a non-fMRI study (E4).

This is a small-animal (rat) PET/CT study of thyroid physiology and dosimetry, not a task-based fMRI study in humans. It includes no healthy adult participants and does not involve problem-solving or fMRI task-related brain activation, so it does not meet the review criteria.

Study is an animal-model investigation of boron distribution for BNCT; it does not involve human participants or task-based fMRI or problem-solving tasks. Therefore it fails inclusion criteria requiring human healthy adults and fMRI methods.

Task-based fMRI study investigating neural correlates during arithmetic word-problem solving in humans; reports activation in parietal areas. Abstract does not state ages, but meets other inclusion criteria — include for full-text to confirm age range.

Study examines idiopathic RLS patients using EMG during fMRI to investigate pathology, not a problem-solving task and does not report a healthy adult sample. Therefore it does not meet the review population criterion.

Task-based fMRI using Raven’s Progressive Matrices (a problem-solving/reasoning task) with healthy subjects; reports task-related network activations and localization. Participant ages are not provided in the abstract, so adult status (18–65) cannot be confirmed—include for full-text review to verify age criterion.

Abstract describes dyadic fMRI of face-to-face social interaction (eye contact) and a theoretical framework (dual logic). It does not report a problem-solving or reasoning task in healthy adults, so it is not relevant to the review objective. Focus is on social interaction and network emergence rather than task-based problem solving.

This study is about quantum chemical prediction of PET tracer non-specific binding and semi-empirical methods, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving. It does not involve healthy adult participants or report task-related brain activations.

This study is an instrumentation/physics paper about measuring energy spectra in PET systems (simulations and camera measurements). It is not a task-based fMRI study, contains no human participants performing problem-solving tasks, and does not report task-related brain activation.

This study examined FDG-PET imaging in lung cancer patients for radiation planning—not a task-based fMRI study, and participants are patients rather than healthy adults. Thus it does not meet the review objective.
This study uses PET and SPECT in epilepsy patients, not task-based fMRI and not a healthy adult problem-solving sample. It does not meet the review’s imaging modality or population criteria.

The task is a dichotic listening (auditory attention) task, not a problem-solving or reasoning task; therefore it does not meet inclusion criterion I2. The study is task-based fMRI in healthy volunteers and reports activations (I1, I3, I4 satisfied), but it does not address problem-solving.

Task-based fMRI study that includes a calculation task (a form of problem solving) and reports task-related activations in human subjects. The abstract does not state participant ages, so full-text review is needed to confirm they meet the 18–65 healthy adult criterion.

The study uses task-based fMRI of smooth pursuit eye movements (perceptual stability), not a problem-solving or reasoning task, so it does not meet inclusion criterion I2. It does use fMRI and reports task-related activation (I1, I4), but the task is outside the review scope.

This study is a PET kinetic modeling/dosimetry paper using [18F]-FDG, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving. It does not involve subjects performing problem-solving/reasoning tasks nor report task-related fMRI activation. Therefore it does not meet the review objectives.

Event-related task-based fMRI study of verbal reasoning/problem-solving that reports task-related regional activation. The abstract does not specify participant health status or age range (I3 unclear). Include for full-text review to confirm presence of healthy adult sample.

The study used PET (18F-FDG) rather than fMRI, so it does not meet the task-based fMRI requirement for this review. The task is language association (not problem-solving), so it does not address the review objective. Exclusion based primarily on E4 (non-fMRI study).
The study used positron emission tomography (PET), not fMRI; thus it does not meet the task-based fMRI requirement. Exclude under non-fMRI study criterion.
This is a technical PET image reconstruction methods paper (TOF PET), not a task-based fMRI study. It involves no human problem-solving task or healthy adult sample and is a non-fMRI study.

This study is a task-based fMRI investigation of speech motor control (reading bisyllabic pseudowords), not a problem-solving or reasoning task; therefore it does not meet the review objective (I2). It does report task fMRI activations (I1, I4) but is not relevant to problem-solving in healthy adults.

This is a task-based fMRI study reporting task-related brain activation during deductive reasoning tasks in human participants (conditional and relational reasoning). The abstract does not explicitly state participant ages or explicitly that they are healthy adults, so include for full-text review to confirm I3.

Task-based fMRI study of mental calculation in healthy volunteers reporting task-related brain activation. Mental calculation constitutes a problem-solving task. Age range not specified in abstract, so include for full-text review to confirm I3 (18–65).

This fMRI study examines ‘refreshing’ (reflecting briefly on a just-activated representation), not a problem-solving or reasoning task in healthy adults. The abstract also reports a meta-analysis component. It does not meet the review objective (problem-solving tasks).

This is a PET study (H2 15O) of vascular responses to vibrotactile stimulation, not a task-based fMRI study and it does not investigate problem-solving or reasoning tasks. Therefore it meets exclusion criterion E4 (non-fMRI study).

The abstract describes a task-based fMRI study but focuses entirely on functional interactions/functional connectivity (task-modulated coupling) rather than reporting task-related activation results. This appears to be a connectivity-only analysis, meeting exclusion criterion E2.

This is a PET methodological simulation study (FDG-PET) developing an input-function-free quantification method; it is not a task-based fMRI study and includes no healthy adult participants performing problem-solving tasks.

This is a PET study of pulmonary abnormalities (FDG-PET), not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It involves clinical pulmonary cases rather than healthy adult participants. Excluded for non-fMRI modality and lack of healthy adult problem-solving task.

Abstract reports task-based fMRI recording during a problem-solving task (chunk decomposition) in human subjects and describes task-related brain activation results; likely healthy adult sample. Satisfies inclusion criteria I1–I4.

This study reports PET radioligand development and in vivo SERT imaging in porcine brains (and in vitro binding). It is not an fMRI study and does not include healthy adult human participants or task-based problem-solving paradigms.

Task-based fMRI study of arithmetic problem solving that reports task-related brain activation contrasts between trained and untrained problems. Participants are human (13 right-handed subjects) performing problem-solving tasks. Abstract does not specify participant ages, so include for full-text review to confirm they are healthy adults (18–65).

This study investigates 18F-FDG PET in endometrial cancer patients (clinical oncology imaging), not task-based fMRI, does not involve problem-solving tasks, and does not include healthy adult participants. Fails the review scope.

This is a task-based fMRI study using the Raven's Progressive Matrices (a reasoning/problem-solving task), includes non-autistic (healthy) participants as a comparison group, and reports task-related activation and connectivity results modulated by task complexity.

Abstract describes an event-related task-based fMRI study using Japanese riddles (problem-solving/"insight") and reports task-related brain activation (right hippocampus and cortex). The abstract does not specify participant ages or explicit healthy adult status, so include for full-text review to confirm I3 (healthy adults 18–65).

Abstract describes a task-based fMRI study of visuospatial problem solving in human participants (strong and weak problem-solvers), reports task-related brain activation results, and likely involves healthy adult participants. Meets all inclusion criteria based on the abstract.

This is an engineering/physics study about silicon photomultiplier PET detector modules for small-animal scanners. It is not an fMRI study and does not involve healthy adult human participants or task-based problem-solving; thus it fails inclusion and meets exclusion criteria E3 and E4.

This is a PET methodological study (OSEM/SAC effects on SUV) using phantoms and patient scans, not task-based fMRI in healthy adults. It is a non-fMRI study and does not include a healthy adult problem-solving task sample.

This study is a PET investigation in primate brain examining dopamine receptor binding; it is not a task-based fMRI study and appears to involve non-human subjects rather than healthy adult humans. Does not meet review criteria.

Event-related (task-based) fMRI study of deductive reasoning in human participants; reports task-related brain activation in left frontal and parietal areas. Participants are healthy adults performing a reasoning/problem-solving task. Satisfies all inclusion criteria.

Study uses CT perfusion and PET imaging to measure cerebral blood flow; it is not an fMRI task-based study nor involves problem-solving tasks in healthy adults. Therefore it fails the fMRI requirement.

Event-related task-based fMRI study of syllogistic reasoning reporting task-related brain activations in multiple regions; involves human participants performing reasoning tasks. Abstract does not explicitly state participant demographics/healthy adult status (18-65), so full text is needed to confirm I3.

This study describes MRI and FDG-PET methods for measuring bone marrow metabolism; it is not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults and uses non-fMRI modalities. Excluded per E4 (Non-fMRI study).

Task-based fMRI study in healthy adults (n=28) using a reversal learning task, reporting ventral striatal BOLD correlates of prediction errors and associations with fluid intelligence. Although the task is learning-based rather than a classic problem-solving paradigm, the authors link activation to flexible problem solving and reasoning; include for full-text review.

This study uses PET (18F-FDG and 11C-methionine) metabolic imaging in patients with gliomas, not task-based fMRI in healthy adults. Participants are clinical glioma patients (no healthy adult group). It does not report task-related fMRI activations or problem-solving tasks.

The abstract describes FDG-PET (PET) clinical/technical issues and facility management, not a task-based fMRI study. No problem-solving task or task-related brain activation in healthy adults is reported.

This is a methods/engineering paper on optimizing multifocal transcranial current stimulation using realistic head modeling. It is not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults and does not report task-related activation results. It uses imaging data as target maps but is not an fMRI task study.

The study examined school-age children (children with mathematical difficulties and typically developing controls), not adults. Therefore it does not include a healthy adult (18–65) sample and fails inclusion I3 (meets exclusion E3).

This is a PET imaging study using 11C-choline for bladder cancer visualization, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It examines oncological imaging and tumour detection rather than cognitive/problem-solving tasks, so it is a non-fMRI study and does not match the review objective.

Methods-focused fMRI paper evaluating image distortion correction; no task-based problem-solving paradigm, no report of healthy adult participants performing a problem-solving task, and no task-related activation results presented in abstract.

This study is PET imaging of liver tumors (medical imaging, not fMRI), with no task-based problem-solving paradigm and not involving healthy adult participants. It is a non-fMRI clinical imaging study, so it does not meet the review criteria.

This study uses ECG-gated SPECT and FDG-PET cardiac imaging to assess ventricular volumes and mass, not task-based fMRI, and does not involve problem-solving tasks or report task-related brain activation. Excluded as a non-fMRI study.

This study reports PET dosimetry and radioimmunotherapy research with monoclonal antibodies for tumors, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving. It does not involve healthy adult participants performing problem-solving tasks and is not an fMRI investigation.

Task-based fMRI study where human participants performed an abstract symbol-manipulation (problem-solving) task; reports task-related BOLD activation in parietal, prefrontal, and motor ROIs. Abstract does not list ages but likely healthy adult sample; full text can confirm.
This study describes PET parametric image reconstruction methods and applications; it is not a task-based fMRI study of problem-solving in healthy adults.

This is a phantom imaging study evaluating noise in PET, CT, SPECT and PET/CT images. It is not an fMRI study, includes no human participants or task-based problem-solving, and reports no task-related brain activation.

This paper describes segmentation of dynamic PET images and methodological evaluation on PET data; it is not an fMRI study and does not involve task-based problem-solving in healthy adults. Exclude as a non-fMRI study.

This study reports preoperative FDG-PET imaging in sarcoma patients (clinical oncology), not task-based fMRI, and participants are patients with resectable soft tissue sarcomas rather than healthy adults performing problem-solving tasks. Therefore it does not meet the review objective.

This is a PET dosimetry study using a bladder phantom and FDG; it is not a task-based fMRI study, does not involve participants performing problem-solving or reasoning tasks, and does not report task-related fMRI activations in healthy adults.

This is a clinical [18F]FDG-PET study in Hodgkin lymphoma patients focused on tumor volume and survival prediction. It is not a task-based fMRI study, does not involve problem-solving tasks, and does not include healthy adult participants. It therefore meets exclusion criteria E4 (non-fMRI study) and E3 (no healthy adult sample).

Participants were children (no adult sample). The study uses task-based fMRI and problem solving, but it does not include healthy adults (18–65), so it fails the adult-sample inclusion criterion (E3).

This study is about PET detector crystal efficiency calculations and methods for imaging hardware, not a task-based fMRI study with human participants performing problem-solving tasks. It is a non-fMRI technical imaging methods paper.

Abstract reports a task-based fMRI study measuring brain activity during creative problem solving and describes task-related activation results. Participant details (age/health) are not specified in the abstract, so full-text review is needed to confirm healthy adult sample.

Event-related fMRI study of conditional and syllogistic reasoning with subjects performing deductive tasks and reported task-related brain activations (BA regions). The abstract does not explicitly state participants were healthy adults (18–65), so include for full-text verification.

Task-based fMRI study of mental rotation (a visual-spatial reasoning task) in surgery residents (adult humans), reporting cortical activation patterns. Meets task-based fMRI, reasoning task, healthy adult sample, and task-related activation reporting.

This study focuses on proton therapy dosimetry and PET/CT Monte Carlo simulations, not on task-based fMRI of problem solving. It does not involve healthy adult participants performing reasoning tasks or report task-related brain activation.

This study uses PET/CT to examine blood perfusion and bone formation around periacetabular osteotomy (orthopedic surgery). It is not a task-based fMRI study, does not involve problem-solving tasks, and involves surgical patients rather than healthy adult participants.

Although this is a task-based fMRI study reporting task-related activations (I1, I4), it tested neurosurgical patients using language tasks to assess hemispheric language dominance rather than problem-solving in healthy adults. No healthy adult sample is reported, so it fails inclusion criterion I3 and meets exclusion E3.

This study uses FDG-PET and compartmental modeling to correct MR(glc) quantification; it is not an fMRI study and does not involve task-based problem-solving in healthy adults. Excluded as a non-fMRI study (E4).

This is a preclinical PET imaging methods paper (system performance estimation), not a task-based fMRI study. It involves non-fMRI imaging, and does not include healthy adult human participants, so it fails E4 and E3.

Abstract reports task-based fMRI of humans performing mental arithmetic (multiplication retrieval and serial subtraction) and details task-related brain activation. However, it does not state participant health status or ages, so inclusion criterion I3 (healthy adults 18–65) cannot be confirmed from the abstract. Include for full-text review to verify sample characteristics.

This is a PET (FDOPA) neurochemical imaging study, not an fMRI task-based study of problem solving. It investigates dopamine storage and aging/Parkinson’s disease, not problem-solving tasks. Therefore it does not meet the fMRI requirement.

Abstract describes behavioral N-back performance, sleep quality, mood, and HRV across supine vs sitting postures but does not report using fMRI or any task-related brain activation results. Meta-analysis requires task-based fMRI studies; therefore exclude as a non-fMRI study.

The study is a physiological/methodological fMRI study of perfluorocarbons during partial liquid ventilation, not a task-based problem-solving fMRI in humans. It does not involve healthy adult participants or problem-solving tasks, so it fails the healthy adult sample requirement and task relevance.

This study uses FDG-PET/CT in hypopharyngeal cancer patients and does not involve task-based fMRI, problem-solving tasks, or healthy adult participants. Exclude due to non-fMRI modality and no healthy adult sample.

The study is about chemical migration from PET bottles into beverages, not a task-based fMRI study. No human participants performing problem-solving tasks are involved. It is therefore not relevant to the review objective.

This study uses PET and Kety-Schmidt metabolic measurements (FDG) rather than fMRI and does not involve a task-based problem-solving or reasoning paradigm. It therefore does not meet the review’s imaging modality requirement.

This study evaluates PET SUV measurements for diagnosing pulmonary malignancy in clinical patients using 18F-FDG, not task-based fMRI, and does not involve healthy adults performing problem-solving tasks. Therefore it fails the fMRI requirement and the healthy adult sample requirement.

This is a methods/sequence optimization paper about recovering susceptibility-induced signal loss in EPI acquisition; it does not report a task-based fMRI study of problem-solving in healthy adults or report task-related activation results. No evidence participants performed problem-solving tasks or that healthy adult activation data are presented.

This study concerns PET-based myocardial blood flow estimation and optimal framing schedules, not task-based fMRI of problem solving in healthy adults. It is a non-fMRI methodological imaging study, so it meets exclusion criterion E4.
The study explicitly reports children as participants (title/abstract). It therefore does not include a healthy adult (18–65) sample, meeting exclusion criterion E3.

This is an animal PET study of radiotracer uptake and dosimetry in baboons, not a task-based fMRI study in humans performing problem-solving; no healthy adult human participants are included and the modality is PET, not fMRI.

This study uses CT and PET to quantify myocardial blood flow in CAD patients—it's a non-fMRI imaging study and does not involve task-based problem-solving in healthy adults. Therefore it does not meet the review focus on task-based fMRI problem-solving studies in healthy adults.

This is a task-based fMRI block-design study in healthy adult students performing mental calculation (problem-solving) and reports task-related brain activation results (left angular gyrus). Meets I1–I4.

Abstract describes an event-related task-based fMRI study where human participants performed technical problem-solving (insight) tasks and reports task-related activations (left DLPFC, left AG). Age/health status not explicitly stated in abstract but typical cognitive fMRI studies use healthy adult samples; include for full-text confirmation.
This is a methodological PET/MRI VOI specification paper (imaging methods), not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It does not report task-related fMRI activation.

This is a task-based fMRI study that reports task-related activation and includes a mental calculation task (a form of problem-solving). It reports results from healthy volunteers, but ages are not specified—include for full-text review to confirm adult sample and task details.

This study is a PET/FDG imaging methods paper in patients with malignant lesions, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It uses PET, includes clinical patients, and does not report task-related brain activation for cognitive tasks.

This study is a PET imaging methods paper using [18F]FEDAA1106 for TSPO quantification, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It does not report task-related fMRI activations.

This is an FDG PET/CT study of anal cancer patients (clinical imaging for staging/follow-up), not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It uses a non-fMRI modality and includes cancer patients rather than healthy adults.

Abstract describes a task-based fMRI study where participants performed fluid reasoning and perceptual speed tasks and reports task-related activation/topographic similarity. Age range (20–77) includes adults within 18–65, but health status is not explicit; include for full-text review to confirm participant health.

This study validates motor and language fMRI against direct cortical stimulation in a neurosurgical context; it does not investigate problem-solving or reasoning tasks in healthy adults and likely involves patient samples rather than healthy volunteers, so it does not meet the review objective.

This is a chemistry study on organofluorosilanes and PET tracer hydrolytic stability (experimental and DFT calculations). It is not a task-based fMRI study, involves no human participants or problem-solving tasks, and therefore does not meet the review inclusion criteria.

The study is a PET imaging/quantification methods paper for (90)Y microspheres in liver cancer patients, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It uses non-fMRI imaging and involves patient clinical context rather than healthy adult cognitive tasks.

Abstract explicitly states this paper is a review article summarizing prior behavioral and neuroimaging findings rather than reporting an original task-based fMRI study. Therefore it meets exclusion criterion E1 (review/meta-analysis).

This is a technical PET image-reconstruction and system-matrix methods paper using prototype hardware (AX-PET). It is not a task-based fMRI study and does not include healthy adult participants performing problem-solving tasks.

Methods paper on PET iterative reconstruction for small animal scanners — not an fMRI study and does not involve healthy adult human participants. It therefore fails the fMRI and healthy adult inclusion requirements.
Study sample consists of children (ages 7–9); no healthy adult participants are included, so it does not meet the requirement of including adults (18–65).

The study used PET (H2[15]O) rather than fMRI, making it a non-fMRI study (E4). The task is a working-memory two-back task, not a problem-solving/reasoning task relevant to the review. Exclude based on non-fMRI modality.

This is a PET imaging methods paper in oncology (dynamic PET for prostate cancer). It is not an fMRI study, does not involve task-based problem-solving in healthy adults, and likely uses patient samples. Excluded based on E4 (non-fMRI) and E3 (no healthy adult sample).

This is not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. The abstract describes FDG PET (a PET modality) and a practice/technology-assessment article likely a review/methods piece focused on clinical patients with colorectal liver metastases, not healthy adult participants or task-based fMRI activation results.

This is a task-based fMRI study of covert visual attention/speed discrimination, not a problem-solving or reasoning task; therefore it does not meet inclusion criterion I2. It does report task-related activation (I4) and is task-based fMRI (I1), but it is outside the review objective.

Abstract reports task-based fMRI identifying parietal activations during subtraction and multiplication and reports task-related activation results; arithmetic tasks constitute problem-solving. The abstract does not explicitly state participant ages/health, so full-text review is needed to confirm I3.

Task-based fMRI in healthy adults (N=28) performing a representation-matching task requiring numerical/problem-solving processing; reports task-related brain activation differences (left angular gyrus). All inclusion criteria are clearly met and no exclusion criteria apply.

The study investigates speech motor control (syllable repetition) rather than problem-solving or reasoning, so it does not meet inclusion criterion I2. It is a task-based fMRI study reporting task-related activations (I1, I4), but the task is not relevant to the meta-analysis objective.

The study uses a memory encoding task, not a problem-solving or reasoning task (does not meet I2). Although it is a task-based fMRI study with healthy adults and reports task activations, it does not address the review objective of problem-solving in healthy adults.
Study used PET ([15O]H2O) in rhesus monkeys (non-human), not task-based fMRI in healthy adult humans. Thus it is a non-fMRI study and does not include healthy adult participants.

The study is an imaging methods/phantom PT-PET benchmark, not an fMRI study and does not involve human participants or a healthy adult sample; it does not study problem-solving tasks or report task-related brain activation.

Task-based fMRI study reporting task-related brain activations during mental calculation in healthy subjects. Matches review objective of problem-solving tasks. Age range not specified in abstract, so include for full-text review to confirm adult (18–65) sample.

This is a PET imaging study (not fMRI) in piglets and brain tumor patients; no healthy adult (18–65) sample and it does not involve task-based problem solving. Therefore it fails the review’s required modality and population criteria.

Animal (porcine) PET study of biochemical development; not a task-based fMRI study, does not include human healthy adult participants, and does not examine problem-solving tasks. Fails inclusion criteria based on imaging modality and population.

This is a PET cardiac imaging methods study (Rubidium-82 PET) assessing kinetic energy index, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. Therefore it is a non-fMRI study and clearly excluded.

The study is a task-based fMRI of praxis (gesture) planning, not a problem-solving or reasoning task required by I2. It does report task activations (I1, I4), but does not address problem solving in healthy adults, so it does not meet the review objective.
This study used FDG-PET (a PET imaging study), not fMRI, and does not involve a problem-solving task in healthy adults. It therefore fails the review's imaging modality requirement (non-fMRI).

This is a PET receptor-occupancy methods study using [(18)F]SPA-RQ, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It does not report task-related fMRI activation or problem-solving tasks, so it fails the fMRI requirement.

This is a PET imaging study of coronary artery inflammation in cardiac patients (ACS vs stable angina), not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving. Participants are patients, not healthy adults, and no task-related fMRI activations are reported.

Participants are children with Turner syndrome (a pediatric clinical sample), so the study does not include healthy adults (18–65) and fails inclusion criterion I3. Although task-based fMRI and problem-solving-related tasks were used, the absence of a healthy adult group clearly excludes it from this review.

This study uses 18F-FDG PET in oncology patients to detect recurrent head and neck cancer; it is not an fMRI study and does not involve healthy adults performing problem-solving tasks. Therefore it does not meet the review criteria.

The abstract describes a pharmacological resting-state fMRI connectivity study (modafinil effects on resting-state networks) and does not report any task-based problem-solving or task-related activation. This meets exclusion criterion E2.

Abstract reports fMRI investigation in patients with acalculia after ischemic stroke or traumatic brain injury; no healthy adult (18–65) sample is described. This fails the requirement for healthy participants for the review objective.

This is a PET/CT imaging study of induced positron activity in a prostate cancer patient following radiation therapy. It is not an fMRI study, does not involve task-based problem-solving or reasoning, and the sample is a cancer patient rather than healthy adults. Therefore it fails the review criteria.

Abstract describes a task-based fMRI protocol that captures mental calculation and other cognitive tasks (i.e., problem-solving), so it meets I1 and I2. However, participant age/health and reported activation results are not specified in the abstract, so full-text review is needed to confirm I3 and I4.

Study reports task-based fMRI results for mental (abacus) addition—a problem-solving task in humans—and gives task-related brain activations. However, it appears to be a single-subject case study (abacus expert), so sample/group and age details are unclear; include for full-text review to confirm I3.

This is a cardiac PET/SPECT image registration methods study involving myocardial infarction patients, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It is a non-fMRI clinical imaging study.

This is a PET imaging methods paper about noise equivalent count rate and phantom measurements; it is not an fMRI study, involves no healthy adult participants performing problem-solving tasks, and reports no task-related brain activation.

Task-based fMRI study in humans investigating complex problem-solving/planning with reported task-related activation in fronto-polar prefrontal cortex. Although the abstract does not explicitly state participant ages or ‘healthy’, it clearly involves human subjects in cognitive tasks; include for full-text confirmation of participant demographics.

Abstract describes a methodological paper applying non-central RFT to BOLD fMRI data but does not specify a problem-solving/reasoning task, participant health/age, or task-related activation results. Information is insufficient to determine eligibility; include for full-text review.
Abstract describes PET activation and is a review focused on nuclear medicine rather than task-based fMRI. It therefore is a non-fMRI study and a review article, both meeting exclusion criteria.

This is a computational QSPR study of 64Cu complexes for PET agent lipophilicity; it is not an fMRI study and includes no human participants or task-based imaging. Therefore it fails inclusion criteria and meets exclusion criteria E4 and E3.

Abstract describes a task-based fMRI study of calculation (problem-solving) tasks in normal aged volunteers and reports cortex activation, meeting I1, I2, and I4. Participant age range is not specified (may be older than 65), so I3 is uncertain; include for full-text review.

This study is a PET and autoradiography investigation in patients with mesial temporal lobe epilepsy and hippocampal sclerosis, not a task-based fMRI study in healthy adults performing problem-solving. It does not report task-related fMRI activations. Therefore it does not meet the review objective.

This is a PET detector physics study focused on scintillator arrays and detector performance; it is not an fMRI study and includes no human problem-solving task or healthy adult participants, so it does not meet inclusion criteria.

This study describes an image reconstruction algorithm for 2D projections and PET, not task-based fMRI or problem-solving in healthy adults. It is a non-fMRI methods paper and does not report task-related brain activation in humans performing problem-solving tasks.

This is a PET (18F-FDG) imaging study in lymphoma patients, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving. It involves patient samples (no healthy adult group) and reports tumor uptake/proliferation, not task-related brain activation.

This is a PET study in oncology focused on partial volume correction for tumor SUV measurements, not a task-based fMRI study of problem-solving in healthy adults. It involves patient/tumor imaging and uses PET (not fMRI).

The study focuses on survivors of pediatric brain tumors and healthy controls; participants are not described as healthy adults (18–65). The population is pediatric/clinical rather than healthy adult problem-solvers, so it does not meet the review’s required healthy adult sample.

The abstract reports positron emission tomography (PET), not fMRI. This makes it a non-fMRI imaging study, meeting exclusion criterion E4. Although it involves human deductive reasoning and reports task-related activation, the imaging modality disqualifies it from this fMRI-focused review.

This is a prospective FDG-PET study evaluating SUV normalization for chemotherapy response in colorectal and nonsmall cell lung cancer patients. It is not a task-based fMRI study and does not include healthy adult participants, so it meets E4 and E3.

This is a PET/CT case report using PET/US fusion to clarify postoperative foreign-body reaction. It is not a task-based fMRI study, does not involve healthy adult participants performing a problem-solving/reasoning task, and reports no task-related brain activation.

This study is an FDG PET/CT imaging study of patients with suspicious breast lesions (clinical patient sample), not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It does not report task-related fMRI activation in healthy adults.

This is a meta-analysis of PET studies examining the resting (REST) state versus tasks, not primary task-based fMRI research of problem solving in healthy adults. It is a review/meta-analysis, focuses on resting-state activity, and uses PET (non-fMRI).
The study used positron emission tomography (PET), a non-fMRI imaging modality; the review requires task-based fMRI studies. PET therefore meets the non-fMRI exclusion criterion.

This is a pediatric lymphoma PET-CT study measuring metabolic tumor burden, not a task-based fMRI study. Participants are children with lymphoma (not healthy adults), and the modality is PET-CT rather than fMRI, so it does not meet the review’s requirements.

This is a PET diagnostic study of cervical cancer patients, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. Participants are patients (not healthy adults) and the imaging modality is FDG-PET (not fMRI).

Task-based fMRI study of the Tower of London problem-solving task reporting task-related dlPFC activation and temporal dissociation of cognitive stages. Abstract describes fMRI analyses during problem solving with no mention of patient groups, implying healthy adult participants. Meets all inclusion criteria based on abstract.

This is a methods paper on image registration for multi-echo fMRI; the abstract does not describe any task-based experiment, problem-solving or reasoning tasks, nor report task-related brain activations or healthy adult participants. It does not meet the review’s inclusion criteria.
This is an experimental PET imaging study in nude rats with a tumor cell line, not a task-based fMRI study in humans. It includes no healthy adult participants and is a non-fMRI animal study.

Methodological/statistical paper on power/sample-size for diagnostic imaging (CT vs PET) in colorectal cancer. Not a task-based fMRI study, does not involve healthy adults performing problem-solving tasks, and reports no task-related fMRI activation results.

The study is task-based fMRI of auditory perception (music and noise) in Williams syndrome vs controls — it does not involve problem-solving or reasoning tasks (does not meet I2). It does report task-related activations (I1, I4), but the experimental task is outside the review scope, so exclude.

Participants are subjects with primary brain tumors (no healthy adult sample reported). The abstract does not describe a task-based fMRI problem-solving study. Excluded due to no healthy adult sample.

Task-based fMRI study of adults performing complex spatial problem-solving and reports atypical task-related brain activation. The abstract does not explicitly state presence of a healthy adult control group (I3 unclear), so include for full-text review to confirm eligibility.

The study is a task-based fMRI investigation of developmental dyscalculia in children; participants are children and clinical (DD), not healthy adults. Therefore it does not include a healthy adult (18–65) sample (fails I3).

Abstract reports an fMRI study of 18 adult humans performing grammatical transformation (a verbal reasoning/problem-solving task) and describes task-related brain activation. No exclusion criteria apply.

Task-based fMRI study in healthy adult participants investigating brain activation related to intelligence during cognitive/problem-solving tasks; reports task-related activation and meets all inclusion criteria based on the abstract.

The study used positron emission tomography (PET), not fMRI, so it does not meet the task-based fMRI requirement (E4). Although it involves healthy adults performing a problem-solving task and reports task-related brain activation, the imaging modality excludes it from this review.

This is a PET/CT study of FDG uptake in lymph nodes of locally advanced breast cancer patients—not a task-based fMRI study, does not involve problem-solving tasks, nor healthy adult participants, and does not report task-related brain activation.

Abstract reports a task-based fMRI study using mental calculation (a problem-solving/reasoning task) and reports DLPFC activation sustained during task. The abstract does not explicitly state participant ages or health status, so full-text review is needed to confirm presence of healthy adult sample.

This study uses FDG-PET to evaluate metabolic activity in alveolar echinococcosis patients, not task-based fMRI, and does not involve healthy adult participants performing problem-solving or reasoning tasks.
This is a PET ligand study conducted in pigs (non-human), not a task-based fMRI study in healthy adult humans. Therefore it does not meet the fMRI or human adult inclusion criteria.

This is a PET molecular imaging study using [(11)C]doxepin, not an fMRI study. Although participants performed attentive/calculation tasks, the modality is PET rather than task-based fMRI, so it fails the fMRI inclusion requirement.

This is a PET kinetic modeling study of a kappa opioid receptor tracer, not a task-based fMRI investigation of problem solving. It does not involve fMRI methods or problem-solving tasks in healthy adults.

This study is a PET imaging study of myocardial perfusion using N-13 ammonia and compares tracer kinetic models—it's not an fMRI study nor does it involve problem-solving tasks or cognitive task-related brain activation. Therefore it fails the fMRI requirement.

Abstract describes a pharmacological fMRI study of cocaine effects on CBF/CBV and hypercapnic vascular responses; it does not report a problem‑solving or reasoning task or task-related activation in healthy adults. Therefore it is not a task-based problem-solving fMRI study relevant to the review.

This is a PET imaging methods paper (analytical scatter correction and image reconstruction) using phantoms and rodent studies, not task-based fMRI in humans. It is a non-fMRI study and includes no healthy adult human sample.

This is a PET methodology study using radioligand [(11)C]-DASB for occupancy and quantification; it is not an fMRI task-based study of problem solving in healthy adults. Therefore it fails the fMRI requirement.

This is a magnetization-transfer MRI methods study in non-small-cell lung cancer patients. It is not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving, does not include healthy adult participants, and reports clinical imaging methods rather than task-related brain activation.

Task-based BOLD fMRI study of problem solving (Chinese riddles) reporting task-related activations (Successful vs Unsuccessful preparation). Abstract does not explicitly state participant ages or confirm healthy adult sample, so include for full-text review to confirm I3 (healthy adults 18-65).

Methodological MRI study measuring physiological parameters in healthy volunteers using hyperoxia/hypercapnia. It is not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving, does not involve participants performing problem-solving or reasoning tasks, and reports no task-related activation results.

The study used PET imaging to measure cerebral blood flow and glucose metabolism in healthy volunteers, not task-based fMRI. It does not report task-based fMRI during a problem-solving or reasoning task or task-related activation results. Thus it is a non-fMRI study and not relevant to the review.
Methodological imaging study on MRI-based attenuation correction for PET/MRI; not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults and does not report task-related activations.
Abstract describes PET radioligand dosimetry in primates/rodents, not a task-based fMRI study with humans. No healthy adult sample and not fMRI; E3 and E4 apply.

Abstract describes a methods-development neurofeedback fMRI sequence and does not clearly report a problem-solving/reasoning task or participant demographics. It is a task-based fMRI neurofeedback experiment and may involve healthy adults or task-related activation; include for full-text review to confirm I2–I4.

Study uses PET-based radiation dose planning (18F-FET-PET) with protons — not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It is a non-fMRI, likely clinical/oncology study, so it fails the fMRI requirement.

This study uses F-18 FDG PET for detecting recurrent rectal cancer in patient population — it is not an fMRI task study, nor does it involve healthy adults performing problem-solving tasks. Therefore it does not meet the review criteria.

This is a PET methods paper describing an image-derived arterial input function for PET radioligands; it is not a task-based fMRI study nor involves problem-solving or reasoning tasks in healthy adults. Thus it does not meet the review objective.

Abstract describes a task-based fMRI study using arithmetic (addition/subtraction) problems in noisy vs quiet backgrounds, indicating a problem-solving task and reporting task-related brain responses. Participant age/health not specified in abstract, so include for full-text review to confirm presence of healthy adults (I3).

Abstract indicates task-based measurement of prefrontal activation in response to working memory stimulation (supports I1 and I4). It does not clearly state that the task is a problem-solving/reasoning task (I2) nor whether a healthy adult sample is included (I3). Insufficient information to determine those, so include for full-text review.

This study reports a PET/CT methodological procedure in radiotherapy patients, not a task-based fMRI investigation of problem solving. Participants are clinical patients (not healthy adults) and the imaging modality is PET/CT (non-fMRI). Thus it fails the review’s modality and population criteria.

Task-based fMRI in healthy adults performing navigation tasks that include a ‘blocked-route problem-solving’ condition; reports task-related brain activations across hippocampal and extra-hippocampal regions.

This is a PET study of serotonin synthesis imaging using alpha-[11C]methyl-L-tryptophan, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It does not meet the fMRI requirement and does not describe problem-solving tasks.

This is a clinical study of EUS-FNA for diagnosing mediastinal lymphadenopathy in patients with suspected lung cancer. It is not an fMRI study, and participants are patients (not healthy adults). Does not meet the review’s task-based fMRI/problem-solving criteria.

This is an animal PET/SPECT instrumentation and calibration study, not a task-based fMRI study in humans. It involves an animal PET scanner and imaging hardware, so it is not fMRI and has no healthy adult participants.

Task-based fMRI study reporting task-related BOLD activation during mental calculation in normal volunteers (healthy adults). This addresses problem-solving tasks and reports activation results, satisfying inclusion criteria.

Task-based fMRI study reporting task-related activation during a working-memory/subgoal task that involves monitoring/integration—relevant to problem-solving/reasoning. Abstract reports brain activation but does not specify participant ages; include for full-text review to confirm healthy adult sample.

This is a physics/engineering methods paper on scintillator timing for PET detectors; it is not an fMRI study, includes no human participants or problem-solving tasks, and does not report task-related brain activation. Does not meet inclusion criteria.

Task-based fMRI study of mental addition (a problem-solving task) reporting task-related brain activation. Abstract does not specify participant ages (healthy adults), so include for full-text review to confirm adult sample.

The study is a PET/CT-based lung cancer radiotherapy planning study with clinical cancer patients, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It is non-fMRI and does not include healthy adult participants.

Abstract describes an overview/review of imaging (CT, MRI, PET-CT) of renal tumors; it is not a task-based fMRI study, does not involve problem-solving tasks or healthy adult participants, and appears to be a review/clinical imaging article.

Abstract describes a task-based fMRI study in human subjects performing a problem-solving task and reports task-related rACC activation. Participant age/health (healthy adults 18-65) is not specified in the abstract, so include for full-text review to confirm I3.

This is a PET image reconstruction/GPU methods paper, not task-based fMRI. No human participants performing problem-solving tasks are reported. Excluded because it is a non-fMRI methods study and has no healthy adult sample.
This study concerns FDG PET and factors affecting SUV and urinary excretion, not task-based fMRI or problem-solving in healthy adults. It is a non-fMRI imaging study (E4).

This study uses gated myocardial FDG-PET to calculate ejection fraction (nuclear cardiology), not task-based fMRI or problem-solving tasks. It is a non-fMRI study, so it does not meet the review inclusion criteria.

This is a computational/theoretical chemistry study of positron binding to molecules; it does not involve task-based fMRI, human participants, or report brain activation. Exclude due to non-fMRI study and no healthy adult sample.

This is a technical methods paper about analytical calculation for PET image reconstruction, not a task-based fMRI study of human problem solving. It does not involve fMRI or healthy adult problem-solving tasks (non-fMRI imaging methods).

Abstract describes a task-based fMRI study of deductive reasoning/conflict showing rlPFC activation and parametric modulation of conflict level. It clearly reports task-related activation and involves human participants performing reasoning tasks. The abstract does not specify participant ages/health status; therefore include for full-text review to confirm healthy adult sample.

Abstract describes task-based fMRI experiments with activation results (finger tapping, visual stimulation, motor paradigms) but does not mention problem-solving or reasoning tasks or report participant ages. Insufficient information to confirm I2 and I3 from the abstract alone; include for full-text review to verify presence of problem-solving tasks and healthy adult sample.

The study is a cardiac PET imaging methods paper (O-15 water PET) focused on image alignment for myocardial perfusion quantification. It is not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults, so it is a non-fMRI study and not relevant to the review.

Methods/software development paper describing a power-calculation tool (PowerMap); no human participants or task-based fMRI experiment reported, and no task-related activation results. Does not include a healthy adult sample for task-based fMRI.
Participants are high school seniors (adolescents), not adults aged 18–65, so the study does not include a healthy adult sample required by the review.

Study uses non-human subjects (domestic dogs) and reports behavioral problem-solving performance without any fMRI methods or brain activation results. Does not include healthy adult human participants. Excluded based on absence of human fMRI data.

Task-based fMRI study in adult human participants performing complex arithmetic and figural-spatial problem-solving with reported task-related brain activation results (angular gyrus and other regions). No exclusion criteria met.

This paper describes PET image reconstruction methods for clinical PET, not task-based fMRI. It does not involve problem-solving tasks or healthy adult fMRI data, making it out of scope for the review.

Participants are ~68 years old (older than the 18–65 range required), so the study does not include a healthy adult group aged 18–65 (E3). Additionally, the fMRI task was a visual inspection time task rather than a problem-solving/reasoning task (does not meet I2).

Single-case study of a patient with childhood right prefrontal damage; no group of healthy adult participants and not focused on problem-solving in healthy adults. Therefore it fails inclusion criterion I3 (no healthy adult sample).
This is a medical-physics study of proton beam range prediction and CT conversion; it is not an fMRI study, does not involve task-based problem-solving, nor report brain activation in healthy adults.

Task-based fMRI study comparing ill-structured design vs. well-structured problem solving; reports task-related activations (right dorsolateral PFC) and functional interactions. Abstract does not specify participant health status or ages, so include for full-text review to confirm presence of healthy adult sample (I3).

Abstract describes a task-based fMRI study of equation solving reporting task-related BOLD activation in specific brain regions, satisfying I1, I2, and I4. Participant age/health not specified in abstract, so include for full-text review to confirm I3 (healthy adults).

This is a PET imaging study (florbetaben β-amyloid PET) in Alzheimer’s patients and healthy controls, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving. It does not involve task-based fMRI or problem-solving tasks.
Study uses non-human (dog) subjects and a behavioral preferential-looking task; it is not an fMRI study nor does it include healthy adult humans. Does not meet the review objective.

The study uses task-based fMRI of language tasks to determine hemispheric language dominance—not problem-solving or reasoning tasks. It therefore does not meet the review objective (I2). Abstract indicates task activations (I1, I4) but the task domain is language rather than problem solving, so exclude.

This is a clinical 18F-FDG PET study of patients with thyroid nodules, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It involves patient diagnostic imaging and reports PET diagnostic performance rather than task-related brain activations during problem-solving tasks.

Task-based fMRI study of mental calculation (problem-solving) in adult twin pairs (19 MZ, 13 DZ). Reports task-related brain activation and lateralization results; sample appears to be healthy adults. Not a review or resting-state/connectivity-only study. Meets all inclusion criteria.

Task-based fMRI study of pseudoarithmetic problem-solving reporting ACC and lPFC task-related activations (anticipatory and online conflict processing). The abstract confirms task and activation but does not specify participant ages/health; include for full-text review to confirm healthy adult sample.

This is a PET imaging study of nasopharyngeal carcinoma, not a task-based fMRI study; it involves patients with cancer rather than healthy adult participants and does not examine problem-solving task-related brain activation.

This is an engineering/medical physics paper about numerical shielding design for PET/CT installations. It does not report task-based fMRI, includes no human participants performing problem-solving tasks, and provides no brain activation results. Therefore it is a non-fMRI study and has no healthy adult sample.

Abstract describes a task-based fMRI study of reasoning/problem solving (inclusion fallacy) and reports task-related brain activation. The abstract does not explicitly state participant ages/health status, so include for full-text review to confirm presence of healthy adult sample.

Abstract describes methodological data-reduction methods for ICA on fMRI data and does not report a task-based problem-solving paradigm, participant characteristics, or task activation results. Information is insufficient to determine whether it includes task-based problem-solving in healthy adults; include for full-text review.

This study is a PET pharmacokinetic/kinetic modeling study of drug-target interactions, not a task-based fMRI investigation of problem-solving in healthy adults. It does not report task-related fMRI activation data.

This is a PET study (11C-methionine) in nine patients with intracerebral tumors, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It therefore does not include healthy adult participants and is not fMRI-based.

This is a molecular photophysics/DFT study of porphyrin-anthraquinone dyads using spectroscopy; it reports no human participants or task-based fMRI data. Therefore it is not an fMRI study and has no healthy adult sample.

This is a methodological imaging-reconstruction paper using PET/SPECT simulations and phantom/system modeling; it is not a task-based fMRI study and includes no human healthy adult participants. Does not report task-related brain activation.

Participants are adolescents (mean age 16.5), so the study does not include a healthy adult (18–65) sample required for inclusion. Although it is a task-based fMRI study of reasoning that reports activations, it fails the age-related inclusion criterion.

This is a clinical review of imaging modalities for bronchogenic carcinoma, not a task-based fMRI study in healthy adults. It does not report task-related fMRI results or include healthy adult participants.

Abstract describes physics/engineering methods for gamma-camera PET sensitivity calculations; it is not task-based fMRI, involves no human participants or problem-solving tasks, and does not report task-related brain activation. Exclude for non-fMRI study and no healthy adult sample.

This study uses FDG-PET in patients with abdominal tumors to assess attenuation correction; it is not a task-based fMRI study and does not include healthy adult participants. Excluded for non-fMRI modality and absence of a healthy adult sample.
Participants are children (abacus-trained and nontrained Chinese children); no group of healthy adults (18-65) is included, so the study does not meet the adult-sample criterion.

This is a clinical PET imaging study in head-and-neck cancer patients (not fMRI), with a patient sample rather than healthy adults; it does not report task-based fMRI or task-related brain activation. Excluded on those grounds.

This is a PET study measuring serotonin synthesis using alpha-[11C]methyl-tryptophan, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving. It does not report fMRI task-related activations or a problem-solving paradigm in healthy adults.
This is an engineering/radiation shielding study for PET‑CT installations; it does not involve task-based fMRI, human problem‑solving tasks, or report brain activation. Exclude as non‑fMRI.

The study reports resting-state EEG-fMRI focused on pulse artefact removal and ICA parameter optimization, not a task-based fMRI investigation of problem-solving. Therefore it meets the exclusion criterion for resting-state studies.

This study describes radiopharmaceutical production using a robotic system; it is not a task-based fMRI study, includes no human participants performing problem-solving or reasoning tasks, and does not report task-related brain activation. Excluded for being a non-fMRI study and having no healthy adult sample.

This is a PET pharmacokinetic modeling study (radioactive decay in 13N-PET) with no task-based fMRI, no problem-solving task, and appears to use simulated/physiological modeling rather than a healthy adult fMRI sample. Does not meet review objective.

Included — the abstract describes a task-based fMRI study of chess (a problem-solving task) in human participants and reports task-related brain activations. Participant age/health status is not specified, so include for full-text review to confirm Criterion I3 (healthy adults 18–65).

This study uses PET and SPECT to quantify benzodiazepine receptor binding in healthy humans; it is not an fMRI study, includes no task-based fMRI or problem-solving tasks, and does not report task-related fMRI activation. Therefore it does not meet inclusion for task-based fMRI studies.

This study is a PET imaging methods comparison using [(18)F]-FDG and arterial sampling; it is not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It is a non-fMRI neuroimaging study, so it meets exclusion criterion E4.

Study evaluates 18F-FDG PET and CA 15-3 in breast cancer patients with occult recurrence. It is not an fMRI study and does not involve healthy adult participants or problem-solving tasks, so it does not meet the review criteria.

Abstract reports a task-based fMRI study of people performing a mathematical problem-solving task and reports task-related brain activations. The abstract does not explicitly state participant ages or health status, so include for full-text confirmation of healthy adult sample.
Participants were children aged 7–9; no adult (18–65) sample is included. The review targets healthy adults, so this study does not meet the population criterion.

Abstract reports a task-based fMRI study of calendar calculation (a problem-solving/reasoning task) and describes task-related brain activation. It’s unclear whether there is a group of healthy adults (sample is two expert individuals, one with Asperger’s), so full text is needed to confirm I3. Include for full-text review.
Study uses PET (non-fMRI) motor activation in a patient with a precentral tumor — not task-based fMRI nor a healthy adult sample. Does not meet fMRI requirement and lacks healthy adults.

This study is a PET/CT dosimetry study of Y-90 radioembolization for liver cancer, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It involves patients and imaging modality is PET/CT.

This is an animal PET imaging methods study in rats using [(18)F]NaF and [(11)C] tracers, not a human task-based fMRI study of problem solving. It does not include healthy adult human participants nor fMRI task activation data.

This is a resting-state fMRI connectivity study (no task-based problem-solving or reasoning task). It therefore meets exclusion criterion E2 (resting-state/connectivity-only) and does not address the review objective.

This is a physics/Monte Carlo modelling study of positron distributions in proton therapy, not a task-based fMRI study. It involves no human participants performing problem-solving tasks and reports no task-related brain activation.

This study uses PET/CT imaging of a gastrointestinal stromal tumor xenograft (preclinical), not task-based fMRI in human adults. It therefore is a non-fMRI study and does not include healthy adult participants.

This is an engineering/physics study of PET detector efficiency using Monte Carlo simulations and experiments; it is not an fMRI study and does not involve human healthy adult participants performing problem-solving tasks. Therefore it fails the review inclusion requirements.

Task-based fMRI study using a mental arithmetic (problem-solving) paradigm in human participants; reports task-related brain activation (operation- and strategy-related activations). No indication of patient samples and typical cognitive fMRI implies healthy adult participants. Meets inclusion criteria for task-based fMRI problem solving with reported activations.

This is a methods paper using FDG PET for voxelwise kinetic modeling and simulations; it is not an fMRI study, does not report task-based problem-solving with healthy adults, and does not present task-related fMRI activation results.

Task-based fMRI study reporting task-related activations during arithmetic problem solving in participants. The abstract confirms fMRI-BOLD, mental calculation tasks, and activation results, but does not specify participant ages or explicit healthy adult status—full-text needed to confirm I3.

This is an engineering/simulation study of a PET scanner and image reconstruction; it is not a task-based fMRI study and does not report human participants performing problem-solving tasks. Therefore it is a non-fMRI study and contains no healthy adult sample relevant to the review.

The study tested children (aged 11–12), not healthy adults, and it reports behavioral working memory and anxiety measures rather than task-based fMRI data. Therefore it fails the adult-sample and fMRI criteria.

Task-based fMRI with arithmetic task and reported task-related activations; however, the study is a single case and the presence/details of a healthy adult control group are unclear from the abstract. Include for full-text review to confirm whether it contains an appropriate healthy adult sample.

Abstract reports task-based fMRI and task-related brain activation, but the tasks described are motor execution/imagery; a concurrent numeric calculation (possible problem-solving) is only a distractor and participant age/health are not specified. Insufficient information to confirm that participants performed a problem-solving/reasoning task and that a healthy adult sample (18–65) is included — include for full-text review.

Task-based fMRI study in 14 human participants performing a reasoning/problem-solving (Wason Selection) task; reports task-related activations (left fronto-parietal network, medial and right inferior parietal cortex). Participants are healthy adults implied. Meets all inclusion criteria based on abstract.

This is a PET imaging methods study using spectral analysis on projection data and software phantoms, not task-based fMRI, and it does not involve human participants performing problem-solving tasks. Therefore it fails the review objective.

This is a methodological fMRI paper applying a noise-reduction filter to motor (finger-tapping) data. While it is task-based fMRI and reports activation maps, it does not involve a problem-solving or reasoning task (fails I2), so it does not address the review objective.

This study describes PET hardware, system-response modeling, and simulation/phantom image reconstruction—not task-based fMRI, and it contains no human (healthy adult) participants. It therefore does not meet the review’s fMRI and healthy adult inclusion criteria.

This study is a methods comparison using PET/CT in cancer patients (lung cancer and lymphoma), not a task-based fMRI study, and does not include healthy adult participants. It therefore fails inclusion and meets exclusion criteria for non-fMRI modality and absence of healthy adult sample.
