This is not an fMRI task study. It is a dosimetric planning study using PET/CT and contrast-enhanced CT for IMRT in NSCLC, focusing on imaging and dose calculation rather than task-based fMRI or problem-solving in healthy adults.

The study investigates perception of 3D shape from motion using MEG-fMRI, not a problem-solving or reasoning task; thus it does not meet inclusion criterion I2. It does use task-based fMRI and reports task-related activations (I1, I4) but fails the key requirement of studying problem-solving.
Methods paper developing wavelet variance estimation for neuroimaging; does not report a task-based fMRI study of problem-solving in healthy adults or present task-related activation results.
This study uses N-13 ammonia PET to assess myocardial blood flow (cardiac PET), not task-based fMRI, and does not involve problem-solving tasks in healthy adults.

The task is a lexical semantic judgment (language) task, not a problem-solving or reasoning task required by the review (I2 not met). The study is task-based fMRI with healthy control adults and reports activations, but it does not address problem-solving in healthy adults.

This is an animal PET study (rats and monkeys) assessing dopaminergic innervation, not a task-based fMRI study and includes no healthy adult human participants. Exclude due to non-fMRI modality and absence of healthy adult sample.

This study used (18)F-FDG PET in laryngeal/hypopharyngeal cancer patients undergoing chemoradiation. It is not an fMRI study and does not involve healthy adult participants performing problem-solving tasks. Exclusion is clear from the abstract.

This study is not a task-based fMRI investigation of problem solving. It concerns (18)F-NaF PET/CT imaging for cardiovascular calcification and reads like a review/article about imaging methods. It does not involve problem-solving tasks or report fMRI activation. Exclusion criteria E4 (non-fMRI study) and E1 (review/meta-analysis) apply.

Abstract clearly reports arithmetic problem-solving tasks and discusses fMRI paradigms but does not explicitly state that this paper acquired task-based fMRI data, brain activation results, or participant age range. Include for full-text review to confirm I1, I3, and I4.

Task-based fMRI study where participants (patient and 4 age-matched healthy controls) performed calculation tasks showing task-related activations. Includes healthy adult controls and reports activation results, so meets inclusion criteria.

Task-based fMRI study in human subjects performing mental subtraction (a problem-solving/reasoning task) that reports task-related cortical activation. The abstract does not specify participant ages or explicitly state they are healthy adults, so I3 should be confirmed in full text.

This study reports PET/MRI measures in patients with temporal lobe epilepsy (clinical patient sample), not task-based fMRI in healthy adults. It therefore lacks a healthy adult sample and is a non-fMRI study relevant to the review.

The abstract describes a connectivity/methods study using fMRI to detect scale-free properties and hardware implementation. It does not describe a task-based problem-solving or reasoning paradigm, nor report task-related activations. This meets exclusion criterion E2 (connectivity-only study).

Study evaluates PET scans for diagnosing recurrent prostate cancer in patient population; it is not a task-based fMRI study, does not involve problem-solving tasks, and participants are patients rather than healthy adults.

This study is an engineering/methods paper about PET system matrix computation with no fMRI data, no human participants performing problem-solving tasks, and no task-related brain activation results. It is a non-fMRI study and thus does not meet the review objective.

Methods-focused fMRI acquisition paper; no task-based experiment, no problem-solving or reasoning task reported, and no healthy adult participant sample or task-related activation results are described in the abstract.

This is a PET image reconstruction methods paper using phantom and clinical PET data; it is not an fMRI study and does not involve task-based problem-solving in healthy adults. Exclude as a non-fMRI study.

This is a PET scanner/count-rate optimization study (LSO detectors), not an fMRI task-based study. It does not involve problem-solving or reasoning tasks, healthy adult fMRI participants, nor report task-related brain activation.
The study used PET and near-infrared spectroscopy (NIRS), not fMRI. The review requires task-based fMRI studies, so this clearly meets exclusion criterion E4 (non-fMRI study).

Task-based fMRI study (serial subtraction) reporting task-related brain activation in 29 right-handed subjects performing a mental calculation (problem-solving) task. Participants appear to be healthy adults, though ages (18–65) are not specified in the abstract — full text should confirm age range.
This is a PET methods study (dual-tracer PET for CMRO2/CBF measurement), not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It does not report task-related fMRI activation results.

This study uses PET to examine attenuation correction and regional cerebral glucose metabolism, not task-based fMRI or problem-solving tasks. It is a non-fMRI imaging methods paper, so it meets exclusion criterion E4 (Non-fMRI study).

Abstract reports task-based fMRI measurements during a mental calculation task (a form of problem solving) and describes task-related brain activation results (frontal lobe differences). Participant age/health not specified, so full-text review is needed to confirm adult healthy sample.

This study reports PET/CT dosimetry in patients evaluated for mild cognitive impairment. It is not a task-based fMRI study and appears to involve patient (not healthy adult) participants, so it does not meet the review criteria.

This is an engineering/analytical chemistry paper on PET microchip fabrication and electrode fracture; it is not an fMRI study and involves no human participants or problem‑solving tasks. Therefore it fails the fMRI and healthy adult criteria.

Task-based fMRI study with participants performing syllogistic reasoning and reporting dissociable neural activation during reasoning. The abstract does not specify participant ages or explicitly state 'healthy adults (18–65)', so include for full-text review to confirm I3.

This is a task-based fMRI study that reports task-related brain activation during a virtual navigation/memory task. The abstract does not clearly state participants are healthy adults nor that the task qualifies as ‘problem-solving/reasoning’ per the review definition, so include for full-text review to determine eligibility.
This study uses SPECT imaging (neuropsychological SPECT activation probes), not task-based fMRI, so it fails the imaging modality requirement for inclusion. Exclude based on non-fMRI methodology.

The task is a double-step saccade (eye-movement) paradigm processing extraretinal signals, not a problem-solving or reasoning task, so it does not meet inclusion criterion I2. Abstract does describe task-based fMRI (I1) and reports activations (I4), but it is not relevant to problem solving in healthy adults.

Clinical study using functional MRI to assess extracapsular extension in prostate cancer patients. Not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving, and does not include healthy adult participants. Excluded based on absence of healthy adult sample relevant to the review question.

This is a methods paper describing an fMRI data post-processing algorithm; the abstract does not describe a task-based study of problem solving, does not report task-related activation results, and does not mention healthy adult participants performing a problem-solving or reasoning task.

The abstract describes a PET image segmentation method for 11C-PIB, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It does not report task-related brain activation. Therefore it is a non-fMRI methods paper and excluded.

This article is an overview/review of nuclear medicine imaging (SPECT/PET), not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It is a non-fMRI study and a review article, so it meets exclusion criteria.

Study participants were eight children with epilepsy (no healthy adult sample). The task was covert verb-generation (language), not a problem-solving task relevant to the review. Therefore it fails inclusion criteria I3 (no healthy adults) and I2. E3 applies.

Study uses PET to quantify coronary blood flow in healthy volunteers; it is not an fMRI study nor does it involve problem-solving tasks. Does not meet inclusion for task-based fMRI, so exclude as a non-fMRI study.

This is a task-based fMRI study reporting brain activation during a simple calculation task (a form of problem solving) in human participants; results report task-related activation differences. Age range of participants (18–65) is not specified in the abstract, so full text is needed to confirm I3.

Methods paper presenting an fMRI data-analysis technique; abstract describes image-processing steps and testing on GRE/EPI data but reports no human participants performing a problem-solving or reasoning task. Does not include a healthy adult task-based sample.

Task-based fMRI investigating mental arithmetic; abstract reports task-related activations in fronto-parietal number-related regions. Participant health/ages not explicitly stated, so I3 is unclear — include for full-text confirmation.

The abstract describes PET-CT target volume calculation methods (a non-fMRI imaging methods/methods discussion). It does not report a task-based fMRI study, any problem-solving/reasoning task, or a healthy adult fMRI sample. Therefore it is not relevant to the fMRI problem-solving review.

Participants are older adults (~age 70) with no healthy adult group aged 18-65, and the task is an inspection-time perceptual speed task rather than a problem-solving/reasoning task relevant to the review. Therefore it fails the healthy adult age criterion and the task-type criterion.

This is a single-case rehabilitation study of a patient with multiplication difficulties (case-study). Although it uses task-based fMRI and reports activations, no healthy adult sample or group is included. Therefore it fails the requirement for healthy adult participants (E3).

Abstract describes task-based fMRI analysis applied to a mental calculation experiment and reports detection of task-related activations. It does not specify participant ages or explicitly confirm healthy adult sample, so full-text review is needed to confirm I3.

The abstract describes a connectivity-focused fMRI analysis using dynamic causal modeling (effective connectivity) for a subtraction task, without reporting typical task-activation results—this is a connectivity-only study and meets exclusion criterion E2.

Abstract reports a task-based fMRI study using mental calculation (a problem-solving task) and describes task-related activated brain areas. Participant demographics/health status (healthy adults 18–65) are not specified, so include for full-text review to confirm I3.

This is a radiology review article about posttreatment imaging for musculoskeletal neoplasms (MRI, PET/CT), not a task-based fMRI study. It is a review and focuses on clinical oncology patients rather than healthy adults — clearly meets exclusion criteria E1, E3, and E4.

This study is PET imaging of norepinephrine transporter ligands, not an fMRI study and does not involve task-based problem-solving in healthy adults. It therefore does not meet the fMRI requirement for inclusion.

The abstract reports task-based fMRI measuring parietal lateralization during a mental calculation (problem-solving) task in healthy adult participants (ages 20–23), and it reports task-related parietal activation and behavioral effects of stimulation. These elements satisfy the review criteria.

Event-related task-based fMRI examining explicit rule induction (categorization/problem-solving) with feedback; reports learning-dependent brain activations (fronto-polar cortex, hippocampus). Participants are humans (typical healthy adult sample). Meets all inclusion criteria.

Task-based fMRI study using a non-verbal Wisconsin Card Sorting Test in 14 healthy volunteers, examining executive/problem-solving processes and reporting task-related activations in prefrontal and parietal regions. Satisfies all inclusion criteria; no exclusion criteria apply.

This is a PET detector efficiency algorithms/methods paper, not a task-based fMRI study of problem-solving in healthy adults. It does not involve fMRI task activation or human problem-solving subjects. Exclude as a non-fMRI study.
This is a review article on anatomic laryngeal imaging (CT/MRI/PET), not an original task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults; it does not report task-related brain activation.

This abstract describes a Monte Carlo simulation for electron/positron transport in water (radiation biology), not an fMRI study and involves no human participants or problem-solving tasks. Exclude due to non-fMRI methodology and no healthy adult sample.

This is an engineering/methods paper about PET scanner image reconstruction and system modeling, not a task-based fMRI study. No human participants or problem-solving tasks are reported. Does not meet inclusion criteria I1–I4. Exclusion criteria E4 (non-fMRI study) and E3 (no healthy adult sample) apply.

This study is a PET imaging study of myocardial blood flow using 82Rb, not a task-based fMRI study of problem-solving in healthy adults. It does not involve fMRI or cognitive/problem-solving tasks, so it does not meet the review objective.

Study is a PET/CT clinical imaging study in prostate cancer patients assessing PSA kinetics and recurrence. It is not task-based fMRI, does not involve healthy adult participants, and does not report task-related brain activation.

This is a single-case report of an 18-year-old with Asperger syndrome (clinical sample), not a group of healthy adults performing a problem-solving task. Therefore it does not include a healthy adult sample required for the review.

Abstract describes task-based fMRI in healthy adults and reports activation, but tasks are language tasks (verb/antonym generation, picture naming) not clearly problem-solving/reasoning. Unclear whether tasks meet the review’s ‘problem solving’ criterion, so include for full-text review to confirm eligibility.

Abstract describes a task-based fMRI study of human analogical reasoning that reports task-related brain activation across task phases. The abstract does not specify participant ages or explicitly state healthy adult sample; include for full-text review to confirm I3 (18–65 healthy adults).

Task-based fMRI in (presumed) healthy adult subjects reporting task-related activation. However tasks are verbal working memory rather than clearly problem-solving/reasoning tasks (I2 unclear). Include for full-text review to confirm relevance.

Task-based simultaneous EEG-fMRI study using mental arithmetic (a problem-solving task) that reports task-related brain activations. Abstract does not specify participant ages or health status; include for full-text review to confirm healthy adult (18–65) sample.

Study is a clinical retrospective study of peritoneal dialysis in patients with terminal kidney disease; it does not involve task-based fMRI, nor healthy adult participants. Therefore it does not meet the meta-analysis objectives.

This is a synthetic chemistry methods paper describing oxidative PET and radical cyclisations; it is not an fMRI study, does not include human participants or problem-solving tasks. It therefore fails the review inclusion and meets E4 (non-fMRI study) and E3 (no healthy adult sample).

This is a clinical imaging article/review about gastrointestinal stromal tumors and diagnostic/staging imaging, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving. It involves patient imaging (GIST), not healthy adult participants, and is not fMRI-based.

Task-based human fMRI reanalysis reporting task-related activations including calculation tasks (a problem-solving task). Healthy adult sample and exact ages are not specified in the abstract, so include for full-text confirmation.

Study uses FDG-PET/CT imaging in lung cancer patients to detect pancreatic metastases; it is not an fMRI study, does not involve problem-solving tasks, and does not include healthy adult participants.

Task-based fMRI study of mental arithmetic reporting task-related brain activations and including healthy control group. Age range of controls (18-65) not specified, so include for full-text review to confirm eligibility.

The abstract describes a task-based fMRI study of arithmetic problem solving in humans and reports task-related brain activation patterns. It clearly meets I1, I2, and I4. The abstract does not explicitly state participant ages or that they are healthy adults, so I3 could not be confirmed from the abstract alone; therefore included for full-text review.

The study tested 14 patients with epilepsy on language fMRI tasks (language lateralization), not healthy adults performing problem-solving or reasoning tasks; thus it lacks a healthy adult sample relevant to the review objective.

This is an imaging methods paper about analytical probability matrices for 2D SPECT (system/methods development). It is not a task-based fMRI study, includes no human participants performing problem-solving tasks, and reports no task-related brain activation. Exclusion: non-fMRI study and no healthy adult sample.

Study uses a memory match/mismatch task (encoding/retrieval) rather than a problem-solving or reasoning task, so it does not meet I2. It is task-based fMRI with healthy adults and reports activation, but the task domain is outside the review scope.

The abstract describes chemical reagent development and analytical detection methods; it does not involve human participants or fMRI. Therefore it is not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults.
This is a PET imaging methods study (myocardial FDG PET) using a BGO-based scanner, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It does not meet the fMRI requirement.
Abstract reports a PET imaging study (not task-based fMRI). The study therefore does not meet the fMRI requirement for this review.

This study evaluates FDG-PET staging in esophageal carcinoma (clinical oncology), not task-based fMRI of problem solving. It involves patients with cancer rather than healthy adult participants and uses PET (non-fMRI).

The abstract describes a clinical treatment-planning system for heavy ion radiotherapy and clinical use in cancer patients. It is not a task-based fMRI study, includes patients (not healthy adult participants), and reports no task-related brain activation results relevant to problem-solving or reasoning tasks.

Participants are patients with extra- or intracranial artery disease (not a healthy adult sample) and performed a motor task, not a problem-solving/reasoning task. Thus it does not meet the review’s population or task requirements.

This is a task-based fMRI study in healthy adults reporting task-related activations (encoding/recognition of visuospatial scenes). However, the task is episodic memory recognition rather than an explicit problem-solving or reasoning task, so inclusion is uncertain; include for full-text review to confirm relevance to problem-solving definition.

This is a phantom-based PET imaging physics study of yttrium-90, not a task-based fMRI study. It has no human participants or problem-solving/reasoning task, so it fails the fMRI and healthy adult sample criteria.
This is an FDG-PET study of brown adipose tissue distribution, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It does not meet the fMRI or problem-solving criteria.

The study reports PET imaging in patients undergoing radiolabelled somatostatin analogue therapy and investigates metabolic effects of amino acid infusion. It is not a task-based fMRI study, involves patients rather than healthy adult problem-solving participants, and does not report task-related brain activation.

This is a PET/CT imaging study of thoracic lesions, not a task-based fMRI investigation of problem solving in healthy adults. It does not involve fMRI or healthy adult problem-solving tasks, so it clearly fails inclusion.

This is a retrospective clinical study of radiofrequency ablation for lung metastases; it is not a task-based fMRI study, does not involve healthy adults performing problem-solving tasks, and reports no brain activation data.

This is a pediatric review/guideline about Wilms tumour imaging, not a task-based fMRI study of problem-solving in healthy adults. It involves no fMRI task data, no healthy adult participants, and appears to be a review; therefore it meets multiple exclusion criteria.

This abstract is a broad historical/theoretical review of numerical cognition and neuroimaging findings, not an original task-based fMRI experiment. It lacks methods, participant/sample details, and primary task-related activation results. Therefore it meets the exclusion for reviews/meta-analyses.

This is a theoretical/methodological PET imaging note, not a task-based fMRI study; it does not involve human problem-solving tasks or report task-related brain activation. It therefore meets exclusion criterion E4 (non-fMRI study).

This is a meta-analysis (ALE) of PET/fMRI studies and includes a resting-state connectivity follow-up; it is a review/meta-analytic study rather than a primary task-based fMRI investigation of problem solving. It does not examine problem-solving/reasoning tasks in healthy adults. Exclude under E1.

Task-based fMRI measured neural responses while participants performed exact and approximate math problems (a problem-solving task), and reports task-related activation differences (reduced left IFG). The abstract does not specify participant ages or explicitly state 'healthy adults', so full-text review is needed to confirm I3.

This study is a proton therapy dose-reconstruction feasibility study using PET and Monte Carlo simulation on a tissue phantom. It is not an fMRI study and involves no human (healthy adult) participants performing problem-solving tasks, so it fails inclusion criteria.

This study reports 18F-FDG PET/CT imaging of breast cancer molecular subtypes (clinical oncology imaging); it is not an fMRI study and involves cancer patients rather than healthy adult participants. Therefore it meets exclusion criteria for non-fMRI methods (E4) and lacking a healthy adult sample (E3).

This is a PET/CT study of (18)F-choline in prostate cancer patients—non-fMRI imaging and involves patients with prostate carcinoma rather than healthy adults. It does not meet the fMRI or healthy adult criteria.
Study used positron emission tomography (PET), not fMRI. Therefore it is a non-fMRI study and fails inclusion criterion I1; exclusion criterion E4 applies.

The study describes Monte Carlo dosimetry using PET-CT and SPECT-CT phantoms/model images—not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving—and does not include healthy adult participants. It therefore fails the fMRI requirement and lacks a healthy adult sample.

This is a theoretical/review-style paper on mirror neurons and therapeutic hypnosis, not an empirical task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It does not report task-related brain activation or a healthy adult sample. Exclusion criteria E1 (review/non-original) and E4 (non-fMRI study) apply.

Abstract reports a task-based fMRI experiment (Experiment 2) examining neural activity during a problem-solving task (insight vs noninsight) and reports task-related brain activation results. Age range not specified in the abstract, so full text needed to confirm participants meet the healthy adult (18–65) criterion.

The study describes validation of a PET method for calculating cerebral glucose metabolism (FDG-PET). It is not an fMRI study, does not involve task-based problem-solving in healthy adults, and does not report task-related fMRI activation. Excluded as a non-fMRI study.

The study reports PET imaging methodology using 76Br with phantoms and small-animal imaging; it is not an fMRI study and includes no healthy adult human participants or task-based brain activation results.

Preclinical medicinal chemistry/PET radioligand study in non-human primates; not a task-based fMRI study and does not include healthy adult human participants or report task-related activation results.

This is a review article summarizing PET and MR activation studies rather than an original task-based fMRI experiment of problem solving in healthy adults. It meets exclusion criterion E1 (review/meta-analysis).

Task-based fMRI study of multi-digit addition with behavioral and neurofunctional results reported; participants appear to be human subjects performing problem-solving (addition). Age/sample not specified in abstract, so include for full-text review to confirm healthy adult sample.

This study is a PET/SPECT dosimetry investigation in patients with neuroendocrine tumours, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving. It involves no healthy adult participants and reports no task-related brain activation. Does not meet review inclusion criteria.

The study used positron emission tomography (PET), not task-based fMRI, so it fails the fMRI modality requirement. Although it reports task-related activations in healthy adults performing mental calculation, the non-fMRI method excludes it.

The study concerns PET image reconstruction and ringing artifacts, not a task-based fMRI investigation of problem solving in healthy adults. It is a non-fMRI imaging methods paper and does not report task-related brain activation in human problem-solving participants.

This study evaluates PET scanner image quality using phantoms and reports scanner performance metrics. It is not a task-based fMRI study, does not involve human participants performing problem-solving or reasoning tasks, and does not report task-related brain activation.

This is a nephrology review article about peritoneal transport tests, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving. It is a review (E1) and involves non-fMRI content (E4); no healthy adult fMRI task data are reported.
This study used functional near-infrared spectroscopy (fNIR), not task-based fMRI; therefore it is a non-fMRI neuroimaging study and does not meet the fMRI inclusion requirement.

This is a task-based fMRI study using an arithmetic (subtraction) task, so I1 and I2 are clearly met. The abstract does not specify participant ages or whether a healthy adult control group (18–65) is included, so inclusion cannot be confirmed from the abstract alone. Include for full-text review to determine I3 and I4.

This is a chemistry/materials study of a BODIPY fluorescent sensor with computational analysis; it does not involve human participants or task-based fMRI, so it is not relevant to problem-solving fMRI in healthy adults.

This is a medical physics simulation study of Y-90 dose kernels using egsnrc and mcnp5, not a task-based fMRI study. It involves no human participants performing problem-solving tasks and reports no fMRI activation results. Exclusion criteria E4 (non-fMRI study) and E3 (no healthy adult sample) apply.

This study uses 18F-FMISO PET to model tumor hypoxia in cancer patients; it is not an fMRI study nor does it investigate problem-solving in healthy adults. Therefore it fails inclusion criteria for fMRI methods and healthy adult sample.

Participants are patients with temporal lobe epilepsy (no healthy adult sample) and the task is a language verbal fluency paradigm rather than a problem-solving/reasoning task. Therefore the study does not meet the review’s population or task requirements.

Study reports PET-CT monitoring of proton therapy in cancer patients, not task-based fMRI. Participants are patients with tumors, not healthy adults. Thus it fails fMRI requirement and healthy adult sample requirement.

The study used PET imaging [(15)O] H2O rather than fMRI, so it does not meet the fMRI requirement for this review. Participants were healthy adults performing reasoning tasks and reported task activations, but being a non-fMRI modality excludes it.

Study uses an event-related oddball task (EEG-fMRI) rather than a problem-solving or reasoning task, so it does not meet inclusion criterion I2. Other key aspects (task-based fMRI, task activations) are present.

This is a PET/CT oncology study of 18F-FDG uptake in locally advanced breast cancer patients. It is not a task-based fMRI study, involves patient (not healthy adult) samples, and does not report task-related brain activation for problem solving.

Abstract describes a methodological permutation-test approach applied to fMRI data and does not report a task-based problem-solving experiment, participants, or task-related activation. There is insufficient information about tasks or healthy adult samples to determine eligibility, so include for full-text review.

This is a chemistry paper describing fluorescent sensor development and cellular imaging; it is not a task-based fMRI study and involves no healthy adult human participants. Therefore it does not meet the review criteria.

This is a systematic review/meta-analysis of (18)F-FDG PET in colorectal cancer patients (clinical imaging), not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It is a review (meta-analysis), involves patient populations (no healthy adult sample), and uses PET rather than fMRI.

Study investigates FET-PET in glioma patients (clinical diagnostic PET imaging after treatment) not task-based fMRI, and participants are patients rather than healthy adults. Does not meet the review objective.

This is a cardiac (18)F-FDG PET imaging study assessing left ventricular volumes/mass, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It involves non-fMRI imaging and does not report task-related brain activation.

This is a clinical PET/CT study of recurrent colorectal cancer patients (not healthy adults), it is not an fMRI study, and it does not assess problem-solving tasks or report task-related brain activation. Thus it fails key inclusion criteria.

This is an animal imaging study using micro-PET/micro-CT in transgenic mice investigating lung tumours — not a human task-based fMRI study of problem solving. It therefore has no healthy adult sample and is not an fMRI study.

This is a cost-effectiveness analysis of PET-CT in melanoma patients, not a task-based fMRI study. It involves clinical imaging and patient populations with metastatic disease, not healthy adults performing problem-solving tasks. Does not meet the fMRI/problem-solving/healthy adult inclusion criteria.

This is a SPECT receptor binding study in patients with schizophrenia, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It includes no healthy adult sample and is a non-fMRI modality.

Task-based event-related fMRI study using a multiplication verification task (problem-solving) in healthy participants and reports task-related activation and effective connectivity. Age range not specified in the abstract, so include for full-text review to confirm adult (18-65) sample.

The study maps β-amyloid distribution and relates it to cognitive performance in a healthy adult lifespan sample; it does not describe task-based fMRI or a problem-solving/reasoning task and appears to use amyloid imaging (non-fMRI). Exclude under E4 (Non-fMRI study).

Abstract reports a task-based fMRI experiment in humans performing nonverbal reasoning/problem-solving and presents task-related brain activation results. The abstract does not specify participant ages or explicitly state 'healthy adults', so inclusion of a healthy adult sample needs confirmation from full text; therefore include for full-text review.

Task-based fMRI study with adult participants performing algebraic problem-solving; reports task-related activation differences in prefrontal and parietal cortices. This meets inclusion criteria for task-based fMRI, problem-solving task, healthy adult sample, and reported brain activation results.

Clear task-based fMRI study in human adults performing multiplication problem-solving; reports task-related neural activation differences between groups, satisfying all inclusion criteria based on the abstract.

This is a methodological fMRI reliability paper applied to auditory and verbal working memory tasks, not a problem-solving or reasoning task; it therefore fails inclusion criterion I2 (problem-solving task).

This study is a PET/CT and MRI evaluation of oral/oropharyngeal cancer using surgical specimens. It is not a task-based fMRI study, nor does it involve healthy adults performing problem-solving or reasoning tasks. Therefore it meets exclusion criteria E4 (non-fMRI) and E3 (no healthy adult sample).

This study reports comparisons of cardiac imaging modalities in patients with coronary artery disease and severe left ventricular dysfunction — it is not a task-based fMRI study and involves a patient (not healthy) sample. Therefore it does not meet inclusion for fMRI or healthy adults.

This is a chemistry/cell-imaging study describing a fluorescent probe and imaging in cell lines (A549, ATII). It is not a task-based fMRI study, involves no human participants performing problem-solving tasks, and reports no brain activation data. Exclusion criteria E3 and E4 apply.

This paper describes a radiation flux modelling tool (RayMan) for urban bioclimate; it is not an fMRI study, does not involve task-based neuroimaging or problem-solving tasks, and includes no healthy adult fMRI data. Therefore it does not meet the fMRI-based inclusion criteria.

This is an environmental water-footprint study comparing bottled and tap water in Italy. It is not a task-based fMRI study, includes no human participants performing problem-solving or reasoning tasks, and reports no brain activation results; therefore it fails the review inclusion criteria.

This is a fluorine-18 FDG PET imaging study of breast cancer patients, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It involves clinical patient samples and a different imaging modality.

This is a PET whole-body radiation dosimetry study of a radioligand in adults, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving. It does not involve fMRI or problem-solving tasks, so it fails inclusion and meets exclusion E4 (non-fMRI study).

This is a clinical study of peritoneal dialysis ultrafiltration in patient population, not a task-based fMRI investigation of problem solving. It does not include healthy adult participants nor report task-related brain activation.
Animal behavioral study of dogs using a behavioral task; no fMRI was performed and there are no human adult participants. Does not meet the review criteria; exclusion criteria E3 and E4 apply.

This study evaluates 18F-FDG PET in patients with gastric MALT lymphoma (a clinical patient sample), not task-based fMRI in healthy adults, and does not report task-related brain activation for problem-solving. It therefore does not meet inclusion criteria.

This study reports PET/MRI imaging of prostate cancer patients, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving. It involves a clinical patient population (prostate carcinomas) and uses PET/MRI rather than task-based fMRI in healthy adults, so it does not meet the review objectives.

This is a systematic review/meta-analysis of 18-F-FDG PET for endometrial cancer follow-up, not an original task-based fMRI study. It is a review (E1) and involves PET, not fMRI (E4), so does not meet inclusion criteria about task-based fMRI in healthy adults.

This study reports case series of malignant progression after gamma knife radiosurgery (clinical neurosurgery), not task-based fMRI. It does not involve problem-solving tasks, healthy adult fMRI participants, nor report task-related brain activation. Exclusion based on non-fMRI modality and absence of healthy adult sample.

This is a biochemical/methods paper describing a fluorescent protein conformation indicator; it is not an fMRI study, does not involve human participants or problem-solving tasks. Therefore it does not meet the review's requirements.

This is a task-based fMRI study in humans examining arithmetic problem-solving and reports task-related brain activation. The abstract does not specify participant ages or explicit statement of healthy adult sample, so include for full-text review to confirm criterion I3 (healthy adults 18-65).

This is an AAPM Task Group report on monitor unit calculations in radiation oncology, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving. It does not involve fMRI, nor does it include human healthy adult participants performing problem-solving tasks.

Abstract describes a task-based fMRI method for overt speech/reading aloud but does not report a problem-solving or reasoning task or confirm healthy adult sample. Insufficient information to determine if it meets the review's focus on problem solving; include for full-text review.

This is a methodological SPECT imaging paper using simulations/Monte Carlo and reconstruction algorithms; it is not an fMRI study, nor does it involve healthy adult human participants or a problem-solving task. Therefore it does not meet the review objective.

This study examines regional cerebral glucose metabolism in Alzheimer's patients (dyscalculia), likely using PET, and does not involve task-based fMRI or healthy adult participants. It therefore does not meet the review’s focus on task-based fMRI problem-solving in healthy adults.

This is a diffusion MRI (white-matter FA) study in children with abacus training, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving, and the sample consists of children (not healthy adults). Does not meet inclusion criteria I1 or I3 and is a non-fMRI study.
The study participants are children (dyscalculic and typically developing), so it does not include a healthy adult (18–65) sample. Therefore it fails the review’s required population criterion.

The study reports task-based fMRI in 7–9-year-old children and compares them to adults but does not include a healthy adult (18–65) sample in the methods described. It therefore fails inclusion criterion I3 (no healthy adult group).

This is an analytical chemistry study using surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy to detect melamine in pet food. It does not involve fMRI, human participants, or problem-solving tasks. Excluded for non-fMRI study and no healthy adult sample.

The study evaluates plastic materials for radiotherapy dosimetry and contains no task-based fMRI, no human participants performing problem-solving tasks, and is not within the review scope. It is a non-fMRI materials/physics study.

The study reports a single-case patient with brain lesion (ATH) and uses fMRI on the patient; no healthy adult participant group is reported, so it fails inclusion criterion I3 (no healthy adult sample).
This is a resting-state functional connectivity fMRI study (no task), not a task-based problem-solving study. It therefore meets exclusion criterion E2 despite including healthy controls.
The study uses calibrated fMRI during a bilateral finger-tapping motor task, not a problem-solving or reasoning task; thus it does not meet inclusion criterion I2 (problem-solving).

The study is a task-based fMRI of risk-taking (Balloon Analog Risk Task), not a problem-solving or reasoning task. While it involves healthy adult participants and reports task-related activations (I1, I3, I4), it does not meet I2 (problem-solving/reasoning), so it should be excluded from a review of problem-solving fMRI studies.

The study uses fMRI to examine speech motor control, not a problem-solving or reasoning task. It therefore does not meet inclusion criterion I2 (problem-solving/reasoning), so it is not relevant to the review objective. The abstract suggests task-based fMRI and task-related activations (I1, I4) but lacks any problem-solving task.

This is a clinical PET/CT study of thyroid cancer recurrence (131I-negative differentiated thyroid carcinoma), not a task-based fMRI study of problem-solving in healthy adults. Participants are patients, and the imaging modality is PET/CT, so E3 and E4 apply.

This is a PET/CT imaging study of neuroendocrine tumor patients (retrospective), not a task-based fMRI study of healthy adults performing problem-solving tasks. Therefore it is non-fMRI and does not include healthy adult participants.

The abstract describes a task-based fMRI study in neurologically normal participants performing social and mechanical reasoning/problem-solving tasks and reports task-related reciprocal activation/deactivation results. Thus it satisfies I1 (task-based fMRI), I2 (problem-solving/reasoning tasks), I3 (healthy adults/neurologically normal participants), and I4 (reports task-related brain activation).

This is an instrumentation/physics study of scintillation crystals for PET detectors, not a task-based fMRI experiment. The abstract reports no human participants performing problem-solving or reasoning tasks and provides no brain activation results. Therefore it does not meet the review objective.

This is a chemistry/photophysics study of a synthesized molecule; it does not involve humans or fMRI task-based problem solving. It does not meet the review focus on task-based fMRI in healthy adults.

Abstract describes a task-based fMRI study that reports task-related brain activation. However, the task is working memory rather than an explicit problem-solving/reasoning task, and the abstract does not specify healthy adult participants, so include for full-text review to determine I2 and I3.

This is a PET image reconstruction methods paper using a mathematical phantom; it is not an fMRI task study, includes no human participants or problem-solving tasks, and does not report task-related brain activation.

The study uses structural MRI and resting-state functional connectivity analyses in chess experts; no task-based fMRI or task-related activation during a problem-solving task is reported. This meets exclusion criterion E2 (resting-state/connectivity-only).

This study is a PET imaging investigation of myocardial glucose uptake after heart transplantation in (presumably) patient population; it is not a task-based fMRI study and does not involve healthy adults performing problem-solving or reasoning tasks.
Study used EEG (electroencephalogram), not task-based fMRI. The review targets fMRI studies of problem solving, so this non-fMRI modality meets exclusion criterion E4.

Event-related task-based fMRI with task-related BOLD results (I1, I4). The task involves resolving referential ambiguity which authors interpret as problem-solving, but the abstract does not explicitly state participant ages or that the task was designed as a problem-solving/reasoning task. Full-text needed to confirm I2 and I3.

Task-based fMRI study of students performing algebra-like problem-solving that reports task-related brain activation (two networks) and predictors of mastery. Participants described as students (presumed healthy adults); full text can confirm ages/health. Meets inclusion criteria.

Abstract text not provided. Title indicates neural correlates of the carry effect in multi-digit addition (a problem-solving task) and likely reports brain activation, but no explicit mention of task-based fMRI or healthy adult sample. Include for full-text review to confirm imaging modality and sample.
This study reports PET (18F-FDG) imaging and gene expression in cancer patients, not task-based fMRI in healthy adults. It is a non-fMRI study and does not include a healthy adult sample.

The paper is a theoretical physical chemistry study of proton-coupled electron transfer; it involves no fMRI, no human participants, and does not study problem-solving. It fails inclusion criteria I1–I4. Exclusion criteria E3 (no healthy adult sample) and E4 (non-fMRI study) apply.

This study uses 18F-FDG PET and CT/MRI in patients with oral squamous cell carcinoma (clinical imaging in patient population), not task-based fMRI in healthy adults performing problem-solving tasks. It therefore lacks a healthy adult sample and is a non-fMRI study incompatible with the review objective.
This is a PET imaging methods study assessing reproducibility of [11C]L-deprenyl-D2 binding; it is not an fMRI study nor task-based problem-solving. Exclude based on E4 (non-fMRI study).

This study is an 18F-FDG PET imaging analysis of tumor heterogeneity in cancer patients; it is not an fMRI study, does not involve problem-solving tasks, and does not include healthy adult participants. Therefore it does not meet the review inclusion criteria.

This is a PET imaging study of preoperative evaluation in patients with brain gliomas, not a task-based fMRI study of problem-solving in healthy adults. Participants are clinical (glioma) cases and the modality is PET, so it fails inclusion for healthy adult fMRI task studies.
The article is a quantitative meta-analysis of fMRI studies (a review), not an original task-based fMRI study with participant-level data; it meets exclusion criterion E1.

The study is a radiation dosimetry/Monte Carlo simulation paper about positron-emitting radionuclides and dose measurements; it is not a task-based fMRI study, includes no human participants performing problem solving, and reports no brain activation results.

This study used ERP/EEG wavelet analyses to examine cortical connectivity during word tasks—not task-based fMRI—and focuses on connectivity rather than fMRI activation during problem solving. Therefore it meets exclusion criteria for non-fMRI studies and connectivity-only studies.

Not a task-based fMRI study and does not include human healthy adult participants. It reports in-beam PET measurements in PMMA blocks (phantom) for proton therapy monitoring, not problem-solving fMRI. Exclusion criteria E3 and E4 apply.

Abstract describes multimodality anatomical and metabolic imaging (CT, MRI, PET/SPECT) for radiotherapy planning — not an fMRI study, no task-based problem-solving paradigm, and not conducted in healthy adult participants. Exclude due to non-fMRI methodology and absence of healthy adult sample.

Task-based fMRI study reporting adaptation and mental-calculation localizer activation in intraparietal and frontal regions. Subjects appear to be healthy humans (typical adult participants). The study reports task-related brain activation and includes a problem-solving/reasoning component (mental calculation).

This is a PET imaging physics study using Monte Carlo simulations of DOI PET; it is not an fMRI study and involves no human participants or task-based problem-solving. It does not meet the review objective.

This is a chemistry/fluorescent probe development study with no fMRI, no human participants, and not a problem-solving task. It does not meet the review objective of task-based fMRI in healthy adults.

The abstract describes a task-based fMRI study (BART) reporting task-related VMPFC activation. However, the task is a risk-taking/decision-making task rather than an explicit problem-solving or reasoning task, and participant ages/health status are not specified. Include for full-text review to confirm whether the task qualifies as problem solving and that the sample includes healthy adults (18–65).

This study is a PET imaging kinetic modeling study of benzodiazepine receptor binding in healthy subjects using [(11)C]iomazenil. It is not a task-based fMRI study and does not involve problem-solving or reasoning tasks, so it is a non-fMRI study and ineligible.

The abstract describes a task-based fMRI study in human participants (n=16) who performed multiplication problem-solving; it reports task-related brain activation results across conditions. Participants appear to be healthy adults. Thus all inclusion criteria are met.

This is an animal (rat) PET-CT study of bone remodeling using 18F-fluoride and 18F-FDG, not a human task-based fMRI study of problem solving. It does not include healthy adult human participants and is not an fMRI task study.
Study uses task-based fMRI of voluntary movement initiation, not a problem-solving or reasoning task. It therefore does not meet inclusion criterion I2 (problem-solving task).

This is a PET study using 11C-flumazenil pharmacokinetic/metabolite correction methods, not a task-based fMRI study of problem-solving in healthy adults. It does not involve fMRI task activation or problem-solving tasks.

Experiment 1 reports task-based fMRI data during a verbal problem-solving task and the abstract describes task-related activation (right anterior superior temporal gyrus) for insight vs noninsight solutions. The abstract does not specify participant ages or explicit healthy adult status, so include for full-text review to confirm I3 (healthy adults 18–65).
This is a PET/CT imaging study measuring standardized uptake values (F-NaF PET/CT). It is not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults, so it is excluded as a non-fMRI study.

This is a veterinary retrospective study of pet rabbits measuring plasma sodium and glucose; it is not a task-based fMRI study and includes no human adult participants. It does not meet the review objectives.

This is a PET (H2[15]O) study evaluating cerebral blood flow methods, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It is therefore a non-fMRI study and meets exclusion criterion E4.

Task-based fMRI study reporting task-related brain activation during visuospatial analogical reasoning. Methods indicate human participants performed a reasoning/problem-solving task. The abstract does not explicitly state participant ages or that they were healthy adults, so include for full-text review to confirm I3 (18-65 healthy adults).

This study is a methodological paper on voxel S values for radiotherapy dosimetry, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It does not report fMRI task activations or involve problem-solving tasks. Exclusion criterion E4 (non-fMRI study) applies.

This is a PET imaging/physics study evaluating a neck-shield's effect on image SNR for 3D PET with O-15, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving. It does not report task-related fMRI activation in healthy adults. Excluded as a non-fMRI study.

Abstract discusses imaging/registration and image fusion for radiotherapy (CT, MRI, PET/SPECT). It is not an fMRI study nor a task-based problem-solving study in healthy adults; therefore it meets the non-fMRI exclusion.

This is an animal PET tracer study in anesthetized monkeys, not a task-based fMRI study and it does not include healthy adult human participants. Therefore it meets exclusion criteria E3 (no healthy adult sample) and E4 (non-fMRI study).

The study is a PET/CT dosimetry study in differentiated thyroid cancer patients, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving, and does not include healthy adult participants. It therefore fails the fMRI requirement and the healthy adult sample requirement.

This is a PET image reconstruction study using MR/CT anatomical priors and clinical patient data; it is not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving and does not include healthy adult problem-solving participants.
This is a PET/CT study of 90Y treatment in patients with liver metastases—it's not a task-based fMRI study and participants are clinical patients rather than healthy adults.

This is a PET/SPECT autoradiography validation study measuring cerebrovascular reactivity to acetazolamide—not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It therefore fails the modality requirement (non-fMRI).

This is a Rubidium-82 PET-CT study in a canine model of myocardial blood flow. It is not an fMRI study and does not involve human (healthy adult) participants or problem-solving tasks, so it does not meet the review criteria.

Abstract describes imaging techniques (CT, MRI, PET) for radiation therapy planning and reads like a review; it is not a task-based fMRI study, reports no task-related brain activation, and includes no healthy adult task-performing sample. Thus it meets multiple exclusion criteria.

This is a PET/CT imaging study in patients with gastroenteropancreatic neuroendocrine tumors, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. Participants are patients (not healthy adults), and the modality is PET, not fMRI.

This is a clinical PET/CT study of colorectal liver metastases focusing on staging and management in patients. It is not a task-based fMRI study, and it does not include healthy adult participants performing problem-solving tasks or report task-related brain activation.
This is a PET/CT methods paper describing an iodine-124 imaging artifact; it is not an fMRI study and does not report task-based problem-solving fMRI in healthy adults.
The study examines simple hand and tongue motor tasks (movement paradigms), not problem-solving or reasoning tasks; therefore it does not meet inclusion criterion I2 (problem-solving/reasoning).
Single-case task-based fMRI study of a stroke patient; no group of healthy adult participants is included, so it fails inclusion criterion I3 (at least one group of healthy adults).

Study uses PET/SPECT functional imaging for radiotherapy planning in lung cancer patients (clinical patients, not healthy adults) rather than task-based fMRI; therefore does not meet fMRI or healthy adult criteria.

This is a phantom FDG-PET method evaluation (attenuation correction) and not a task-based fMRI study. It involves no human participants or problem-solving tasks, so it does not meet the review inclusion criteria.

This is a technical PET/CT image-registration study using phantoms for radiotherapy dosimetry. It does not involve task-based fMRI, no human participants performing problem-solving tasks, and reports no task-related brain activation.

This is a PET imaging study of the serotonin transporter in subjects with a history of alcoholism (C-11 McN5652), not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It is non-fMRI and focuses on alcoholic participants rather than healthy adult problem-solving tasks.

This is an animal (rat) study using Doppler OCT to image retinal hemodynamic responses, not a task-based fMRI study, and it does not include human adult participants or problem-solving tasks. Exclude due to non-fMRI methodology and absence of healthy adult sample.

This is a PET study of endothelial dysfunction in patients with primary antiphospholipid syndrome; not a task-based fMRI investigation of problem solving in healthy adults. No healthy adult problem-solving fMRI data reported.

This is a methodological PET imaging study using phantoms and algorithm development, not task-based fMRI in human participants performing problem-solving. It does not include a healthy adult sample or fMRI task-related activation results.

Abstract reports task-based fMRI in human subjects performing finger opposition and a mathematical serial calculation task; results relate task difficulty to brain activity complexity. This meets I1 (task-based fMRI), I2 (includes a reasoning/math task), and I4 (reports task-related brain activation/complexity). Participant ages/health status are not specified, so include for full-text review to confirm I3.

This study used PET (positron emission tomography), not fMRI, therefore it meets exclusion criterion E4 (Non-fMRI studies). Although it involves a problem-solving task with healthy adult controls and reports task-related brain activation, the imaging modality disqualifies it for a task-based fMRI meta-analysis.
This is an animal (canine) PET/CT sentinel node mapping feasibility study, not a task-based fMRI study in human adults. No healthy adult human participants and not fMRI—exclude.

The abstract describes peritoneal dialysis formulae and nomograms; it is not an fMRI study, contains no task-based brain imaging, no problem-solving or reasoning tasks, and reports no task-related brain activation in healthy adults.

This study is a PET/CT oncology treatment response study in colorectal liver metastases patients, not a task-based fMRI study of problem-solving in healthy adults. It involves patient population and non-fMRI imaging modalities.

This is an engineering/physics study describing PET detector algorithms and Monte Carlo experiments, not a task-based fMRI study. It involves no human participants or task-related brain activation data. Excluded for being non-fMRI and lacking a healthy adult sample.

This study reports MRI/(18)F-FDG PET imaging in patients with cervical compressive myelopathy (clinical imaging of a patient population), not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It uses PET/MRI, includes patient sample, and does not report task-related fMRI activations.

Abstract reports fMRI during the Wisconsin Card Sorting Test in individuals with anorexia nervosa; it focuses on a patient population and does not mention any healthy adult control group. Therefore it lacks a healthy adult sample required for the review (E3).

The article is a narrative review of geriatric depression and treatment/neuroimaging advances, not a primary task-based fMRI study of problem-solving in healthy adults; thus it meets the review exclusion criterion.
Preclinical PET imaging study in baboons using a radioligand. It is not an fMRI study and includes no human healthy adult participants or any task-based problem-solving paradigm.

This is a PET image-processing methods paper (algorithms for 3D PET attenuation correction and rebinning), not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It does not report fMRI task activations.

Event-related task-based fMRI reporting task-related OFC activation (meets I1 and I4). However, the Iowa Gambling Task is a decision-making/gambling task and the abstract does not explicitly indicate a problem-solving/reasoning paradigm or report participant ages. Insufficient information to confirm I2 and I3, so include for full-text review.

Task-based fMRI study in healthy adult participants performing cognitive tasks including fluid reasoning/problem-solving; reports task-related activation analyses and results. Fits all inclusion criteria for problem-solving task-based fMRI in healthy adults.

This is an experimental physics study of aluminum plasma and x-ray diagnostics on a laser facility; it involves no human participants and is not an fMRI study. Does not meet the review’s task-based fMRI or healthy adult participant requirements.
This is a chemistry/fluorescence study of BODIPY dyes with preliminary intracellular imaging in HeLa cells; it is not a task-based fMRI study and includes no healthy adult human participants.

This is an engineering/technical study describing a PET detector and a statistical positioning algorithm. It is not an fMRI study, includes no human participants performing problem-solving tasks, and reports no task-related brain activation.

Task-based fMRI study investigating arithmetic problem solving and transfer; reports task-related brain activation results. The abstract does not explicitly state participant ages or clearly state 'healthy adults', so include for full-text review to confirm criterion I3.

This is a methodological paper describing Fast-FENICA for resting-state fMRI; it is not a task-based fMRI study nor involves problem-solving tasks in healthy adults. Excluded under resting-state/connectivity-only criterion.

Task-based fMRI study in (presumably) healthy adults reporting task-related activations, but it is unclear whether the artificial grammar learning task qualifies as a problem-solving/reasoning task (I2). Include for full-text review to determine eligibility.

Abstract discusses conceptual neurological aspects of learning in children/learning disorders with no mention of fMRI methods, task-based problem-solving, or healthy adult participants. Does not meet fMRI or adult-sample criteria.

This study is an FDG-PET/CT imaging study of breast cancer patients, not a task-based fMRI investigation of problem solving. It does not involve healthy adult participants or report task-related fMRI activation. Therefore it fails inclusion criteria and meets exclusion criteria.

The study is not a task-based fMRI investigation of problem solving: it reports Monte Carlo simulations and PET/CT voxel-size dosimetry using phantoms and clinical FDG PET/CT data. No task-related brain activation or healthy adult problem-solving sample.

This is a PET/CT imaging study in patients with multiple myeloma (oncology population), not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It uses PET tracers and involves diseased participants, so it fails the fMRI and healthy-sample criteria.
Animal study using nude rat tumor xenografts and a chemoradionuclide therapy plus RF ablation; not an fMRI study and contains no healthy adult human participants. Does not meet the review objective.

Study is a PET imaging investigation in advanced non-small cell lung cancer patients (oncology), not task-based fMRI, and does not include healthy adult participants or report task-related brain activation for problem solving. Not relevant to review.

The study used PET imaging (not fMRI), so it fails the non-fMRI exclusion criterion. Although it involves human healthy adults and task-related activation, the imaging modality disqualifies it for an fMRI-focused review.

This is a veterinary study of cats with diabetes and quality-of-life assessment; it is not a task-based fMRI study in humans and contains no healthy adult participants. Does not meet the review objective.

This is an analytical chemistry methods paper about measuring fructans in food/pet food — not a task-based fMRI study, contains no human problem-solving participants, and reports no brain activation results.

This is an 18F-FDG PET/CT study of advanced gastric carcinoma relating HER2 expression to SUV. It is not a task-based fMRI study, does not involve problem-solving tasks, and does not include healthy adult participants. Therefore it meets exclusion criteria.

Study is an animal/veterinary paper on dogs with diabetes and evaluates a quality-of-life tool; it is not a human task-based fMRI study and does not include healthy adult participants (18–65). Also, it is not an fMRI study.

This is a chemistry/fluorescent probe development study involving molecular design and photophysical measurements, not a task-based fMRI study or human participant research. It contains no healthy adult sample and is not an fMRI study.

Task-based fMRI study of mental calculation (serial addition) vs verbal rehearsal in 16 healthy subjects with reported task-related activation differences in premotor cortex. Fits problem-solving domain and reports activation results. Age range not stated but participants described as healthy subjects; include for full-text confirmation.

Study concerns radiation dose estimates for PET imaging probes (nuclear medicine), not task-based fMRI or problem-solving tasks in healthy adults. It is a non-fMRI imaging/physics study; therefore it does not meet the review objective.

The study is a PET-CT medical physics feasibility study of 90Y radioembolization in cancer patients—not a task-based fMRI study and not conducted in healthy adults. It therefore fails the fMRI requirement and the healthy adult sample criterion.
Preclinical PET tumor study; not an fMRI study and does not include healthy adult human participants. Does not meet the review objective of task-based fMRI in healthy adults.

This study is a PET imaging study of non-small cell lung cancer patients; it is not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving nor does it include healthy adult participants. Therefore it fails the fMRI requirement and the healthy-sample requirement.

This is a PET dosimetry case report of an 11-year-old thyroid cancer patient, not a task-based fMRI study and does not include healthy adults. It therefore fails the fMRI requirement and the healthy adult sample requirement.

Task-based fMRI study in healthy right-handed volunteers performing pantomimes of familiar and unfamiliar tools; abstract examines mechanical problem-solving vs action semantics and reports task-related brain activations. Age range not specified but participants are healthy volunteers. Include for full-text review to confirm adult age range and task details.

This is a preclinical PET imaging study describing synthesis and in vivo evaluation of an 18F-labeled uPA inhibitor; it is not a task-based fMRI study and does not include healthy adult human participants. Therefore it does not meet the review criteria.

This study is a PET radiotracer biodistribution/dosimetry Phase I study in healthy volunteers; it is not a task-based fMRI study, nor does it involve problem-solving or report task-related brain activation. Therefore it does not meet the review objective.
Study uses EEG/ERP wavelet connectivity methods rather than task-based fMRI. It is a non-fMRI neurophysiology study, so it does not meet the fMRI requirement for this review.

This study used PET (not fMRI) and investigated 5-FU pharmacokinetics in patients with gastrointestinal cancer; participants are not healthy adults. Therefore it does not meet the fMRI or healthy adult criteria.

Study is an FDG PET/CT imaging study in patients with non-small cell lung cancer, not a task-based fMRI study in healthy adults performing problem-solving tasks. Does not meet fMRI methodology or healthy adult sample criteria.

This is a small-animal PET/MR imaging study of mammary cancer bone metastasis (oncology, animal model). It is not a task-based fMRI study nor does it involve healthy adult human participants. Excluded based on non-fMRI modality and absence of healthy adult sample.

This is a materials science study using time-resolved synchrotron SAXS on polymers, not an fMRI study with human participants performing problem-solving tasks. It does not involve healthy adult humans or report task-related brain activation. Excluded for being a non-fMRI study and having no healthy adult sample.
Instrumentation development study of a beta camera; not an fMRI study and does not involve human participants performing problem-solving tasks. Therefore it fails the 'fMRI' criterion (E4).

This study is an economic analysis of rabies control measures on Flores Island, not a task-based fMRI investigation. It does not involve problem-solving tasks, report brain activation, or include healthy adult participants in an fMRI context.

The study evaluates I-124 PET image quality and prompt gamma correction — a nuclear medicine imaging methods paper. It is not an fMRI study and does not involve task-based problem solving in healthy adults, so it does not meet the review objective.

This study reports PET imaging of lung glucose metabolism, not an fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It is a non-fMRI imaging study and does not involve task-based problem-solving, so it does not meet inclusion criteria.

This abstract describes development of a radiation therapy software visualization and image-registration tool, not a task-based fMRI study. It involves no human participants or problem-solving tasks and reports no task-related brain activation. Excluded for non-fMRI study and absence of healthy adult sample.

The study describes PET detector hardware simulations and measurements, not task-based fMRI of human problem-solving. It is a non-fMRI, imaging-hardware study and does not involve fMRI or task-related brain activation in healthy adults.
Study is PET imaging in nonhuman primates (macaques), not task-based fMRI in human adults. Therefore it is a non-fMRI study and has no healthy adult human participants.

This study concerns PET imaging for boron neutron capture therapy in cancer patients, not task-based fMRI. Participants are patients with glioblastoma/melanoma, not healthy adults. Fails fMRI and healthy-adult criteria.

This is a PET imaging study in rhesus monkeys (animal subjects) using 2-[18F]FA to image nicotinic receptors. It is not an fMRI study, includes no human healthy adult participants, and does not involve a problem-solving task or report task-based activations.

This study uses 11C-choline PET/CT in prostate cancer patients with biochemical relapse—not task-based fMRI, and participants are clinical patients rather than healthy adults; therefore it fails fMRI and healthy-sample criteria.

This study is a cardiac PET imaging study (13N-NH3 and 18F-FDG) assessing ventricular volumes and mass, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It does not involve fMRI or problem-solving tasks, so it does not meet the review objective.

This is a review article about imaging techniques for colorectal liver metastases, not a task-based fMRI study of problem-solving in healthy adults. It is a review (exclusion E1) and discusses non-fMRI imaging modalities (exclusion E4).

This study focuses on group ICA of resting-state fMRI (methods comparison) rather than a task-based problem-solving or reasoning task in healthy adults. It therefore meets the exclusion criterion for resting-state/connectivity-only studies.

The study uses task-based fMRI of motor planning/pantomime (tool-use gestures), not problem-solving or reasoning tasks required by the review. Although it includes healthy adults and reports task activations, it does not address problem solving.

This is a PET imaging study validating myocardial oxidative metabolism using [11C]acetate parametric images. It is not an fMRI study and does not involve problem-solving or reasoning tasks in healthy adults, so it does not meet the review criteria.

The study used FDG-PET and structural MRI (not task-based fMRI) and examined schizophrenia patients (no healthy adult group reported). The task was attention, not a problem-solving/reasoning task. Thus it does not meet the fMRI or healthy adult inclusion criteria.

This is a business/management article (Harvard Business Review) discussing courage and decision-making, with no mention of fMRI, neuroimaging methods, experimental tasks, or participant samples. It is not a task-based fMRI study.

This is a chemistry/analytical methods paper about a fluorescent pH probe applied to bacteria; it is not an fMRI study and includes no human (healthy adult) participants. Does not address task-based fMRI problem-solving. Exclusion criteria E4 (non-fMRI) and E3 (no healthy adult sample) apply.

The abstract describes development of small-animal PET imaging methods using the quadHIDAC scanner (animal hearts), not a task-based fMRI study in humans. It involves animals rather than healthy adult participants and is a non-fMRI imaging study.
This is a clinical review about 18F-FDG PET patient preparation (not an fMRI study) and is a review article. It does not involve task-based fMRI problem-solving in healthy adults.

This study uses PET imaging of tumor hypoxia in head and neck cancer patients, not task-based fMRI, and does not involve healthy adult participants or problem-solving/reasoning tasks. Does not meet inclusion criteria I1–I4; clearly meets exclusion criteria E3 and E4.

This study is a PET imaging methods paper about liver tumor segmentation (FDG-PET), not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It does not involve problem-solving tasks or report task-related fMRI activation in healthy adults.

The study uses [15O]water PET to quantify cerebral blood flow and describes image-derived input functions; it is not an fMRI study, does not involve task-based problem-solving in healthy adults, and does not report task-related activation. Thus it fails the fMRI modality requirement.

Abstract reports a task-based fMRI study that compares rest, perceptual, and semantic retrieval tasks and reports task-related activations. However, the task is semantic retrieval (not clearly problem-solving/reasoning) and participant demographics are not specified. Insufficient detail to confirm I2 and I3, so include for full-text review.

The study uses non-human participants (bonnet macaques) and reports behavioral food-extraction tasks with no mention of fMRI; therefore it does not include healthy adult human participants nor task-based fMRI data. Exclusion criteria E3 and E4 apply.
Methods/physics MRI paper using phantom measurements; not a task-based fMRI study with human participants performing problem-solving and does not report task-related brain activation.

Preclinical PET radioligand study in rabbits; not a task-based fMRI study, no human participants or healthy adult sample, and non-fMRI imaging modality. Does not address problem-solving fMRI in healthy adults.

This is a meta-analysis of imaging modalities (CT, MRI, PET, US) for detection of lymph node metastases in head and neck cancer patients. It is not a task-based fMRI study, does not include healthy adult participants, and is itself a meta-analysis. Therefore it meets exclusion criteria E1, E3, and E4.

This study uses PET imaging in patients with laryngeal carcinoma (clinical cancer sample) and does not involve task-based fMRI or healthy adult participants. It therefore fails the fMRI requirement and the healthy-sample requirement.

This study examines prenatal screening for Down syndrome in pregnant women and does not involve task-based fMRI, problem-solving tasks, or a healthy adult cognitive sample. It is therefore not relevant to the fMRI problem-solving review.

This is a PET ligand study measuring peripheral benzodiazepine receptor binding in vivo, not a task-based fMRI study and does not involve problem-solving tasks. Therefore it is a non-fMRI study and does not meet the review objective.

The study investigates pet ownership and asthma/allergy in children aged 6-10, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. Participants are children (not healthy adults) and no fMRI methods are reported. Exclusion criteria E3 and E4 apply.

This study reports PET imaging using 18F-FLT to detect pancreatic cancer and differentiate malignant from benign lesions — it is not a task-based fMRI study and involves patients with pancreatic disease rather than healthy adults.
This is an engineering/physics study of PET detector hardware (MCP-PMT) with no human participants, and it is not an fMRI study. Does not meet the review objective.

The study uses PET imaging to assess dopamine transporter binding and scanner performance; it is not an fMRI study and does not involve task-based problem-solving in healthy adults, so it does not meet the review objective.

This is an engineering/materials study of neutron shielding using epoxy and colemanite with physical attenuation experiments; it is not an fMRI study and involves no human participants performing problem-solving tasks. Therefore it fails the fMRI and healthy adult sample criteria.

This is a technical SPECT/PET imaging methods study using LSO detectors and phantoms/animal system—no task-based fMRI, no human healthy adult participants. It does not meet the review objective of task-based fMRI in healthy adults.

This is an imaging physics study evaluating small-animal PET phantom methods and scanner performance; it is not a task-based fMRI study and involves no healthy adult human participants. Does not meet review inclusion criteria.

This is an animal (rat) micro-PET study using [18F]fluoromisonidazole and kinetic modeling, not a task-based fMRI study, and it does not include healthy adult human participants. Exclude for non-fMRI modality and no healthy adult sample.
The abstract describes PET/CT quality assurance for radiation therapy, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It does not report fMRI task-related brain activation.

This study uses PET imaging in breast cancer patients, not task-based fMRI in healthy adults. It involves a patient population and a different imaging modality, so it does not meet the review objective.

The abstract describes a single-case study of an individual with Asperger syndrome and does not report any group of healthy adult participants; therefore it fails inclusion criterion I3 (no healthy adult sample).

Abstract describes a task-based fMRI study in healthy college students performing problem-solving (novel vs. old scientific innovations) and reports task-related activations (lingual gyrus, precuneus). Thus it meets all inclusion criteria.
Participants are adolescent girls (not adults). The study therefore does not include a healthy adult sample (18-65), so it fails inclusion criterion I3 and meets exclusion criterion E3.

Abstract describes a task-based fMRI study (re-analysis of fMRI data) of mental arithmetic in healthy adults reporting task-related brain activation and connectivity; meets inclusion criteria and no exclusion criteria clearly apply. Full text can confirm age range and details.

Task-based fMRI study in healthy adult participants performing problem-solving (anagram) tasks; reports task-related activation results (right ventral lateral PFC and others). No exclusion criteria apply.

The task is finger-to-thumb tapping (motor), not a problem-solving or reasoning task, so it fails inclusion criterion I2. It is task-based fMRI in healthy adults reporting activation (I1, I3, I4), but does not address the review objective.

The abstract describes development of a physics/algorithm for proton-irradiation imaging, not a task-based fMRI study in humans performing problem-solving tasks. It is a non-fMRI technical study and does not include healthy adult participants.

The study is resting-state/connectivity focused (graph theoretical analysis of resting-state fMRI) rather than a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It does not report task-related activation. Therefore it meets exclusion criterion E2.

Task-based fMRI in human subjects with reported task-related activations and likely healthy adult participants. However, the stop-change paradigm is an executive-control/multitasking task and it is unclear from the abstract whether it qualifies as a problem-solving/reasoning task; include for full-text review to confirm.

The study is a PET/CT biodistribution and dosimetry assessment of a radiolabeled estradiol analog in healthy women; it is not an fMRI study and does not involve any problem-solving or reasoning task. Does not meet the task-based fMRI requirement.

This is a PET radiopharmaceutical dosimetry study in heart transplant patients, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It involves patient samples (OHT) and non-fMRI imaging methods.

This study is an 18F-FDG PET/CT imaging study of salivary gland tumors (clinical imaging of patient samples), not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It therefore is a non-fMRI study and does not include a healthy adult problem-solving sample.

This study uses PET imaging to measure glucose metabolism in malignant gliomas (clinical patient sample) and is not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It therefore fails the fMRI requirement and does not include healthy adult participants.

Study examines diffusion-weighted MRI and 18F-FDG PET/CT in non-small cell lung cancer patients. It is not a task-based fMRI study and does not include healthy adult participants or problem-solving tasks.

This study describes development of dynamic contrast-enhanced T1-weighted MRI for brain perfusion measurement, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in humans.
