This is a breath-hold physiological study using fMRI/fNIRS (vascular response), not a problem-solving or reasoning task. Abstract also does not explicitly report an adult healthy sample aged 18–65. It does not address the review objective of task-based problem-solving in healthy adults.

This is a chemistry/radiotracer synthesis paper about PET tracers (MCHR1) and does not report task-based fMRI of humans performing problem-solving or reasoning tasks. It involves no healthy adult fMRI sample or task-related brain activation results relevant to the review.

Abstract is a methodological paper on modelling haemodynamic response; it does not mention problem-solving/reasoning tasks, participant demographics, or task-related activation results. Information is insufficient to determine whether it used task-based fMRI in healthy adults performing problem-solving. Include for full-text review to confirm.

Task-based fMRI anagram (problem-solving) study in human participants that reports task-related brain activation (insula, PFC, ACC). Abstract does not explicitly state participant ages or health status, so include for full-text review to confirm presence of healthy adults (18–65).

The abstract describes a narrative/chapter on nuclear cardiology (SPECT/PET) for cardiac surgery in patients with coronary artery disease. It is not a task-based fMRI study of problem-solving in healthy adults, and does not report task-related fMRI activation. Therefore it meets multiple exclusion criteria.

This is a cellular/molecular imaging study using FDG-PET of macrophages and atherosclerosis, not a task-based fMRI study. It does not involve human participants performing problem-solving tasks or report task-related brain activation in healthy adults.

The study uses task-based fMRI of rhythmic motor tasks (hand-foot synkinesia), not a problem-solving or reasoning task. Although it reports task-related activation (I1, I4) and includes controls, it does not address problem solving in healthy adults.

Abstract describes a task-based fMRI study that reports task-related activations, but the task is tactile perceptual discrimination (post-decision confidence), not clearly a problem-solving or reasoning task. Because I2 (problem-solving/reasoning task) is not clearly met from the abstract, include for full-text review to confirm eligibility.

The study uses task-based fMRI of auditory categorization and training on speech-like sounds, not a problem-solving or reasoning task. While it meets I1 and I4 (task-based fMRI with reported activation), it does not address problem solving (I2) and thus does not match the review objective.

Task is musical sensori-motor learning and melody processing across development. While this is a task-based fMRI study with healthy adult participants and reports task-related activations (I1, I3, I4), it does not involve a problem-solving or reasoning task, so it fails I2.

The study uses task-based fMRI of auditory category learning and passive listening, not a problem-solving or reasoning task (fails inclusion criterion I2). Although it reports task-related fMRI results (I1, I4), it does not address problem solving in healthy adults, so it falls outside the review objective.

Task is a syntactic sentence-judgment (language) fMRI task in healthy adult bilinguals/monolinguals, not a problem-solving or reasoning task. Therefore it fails inclusion criterion I2 (problem-solving), so it is excluded from a review of problem-solving fMRI studies.

The task is a concealed information/deception test, not a problem-solving or reasoning task, so it fails inclusion criterion I2. Although it is a task-based fMRI study that reports activation (I1, I4), it does not address problem solving in healthy adults as required for the review.

This is an animal study (isolation-reared rats) using PET FDG imaging rather than task-based fMRI, and does not include healthy adult human participants. It therefore fails the requirements for human adult sample and fMRI modality.

This fMRI study uses symptom provocation in patients with OCD (contamination/washing) and does not investigate problem-solving or reasoning tasks. The abstract mentions patients and provides no evidence of a healthy adult sample. Therefore it does not meet the review objective.

Study does not involve a problem-solving or reasoning task (passive viewing of aversive images), participants are chronic smokers (not a healthy adult sample), and the analysis focuses on functional connectivity (connectivity-only), so it fails inclusion.

The study presents mathematical modeling and an in vitro macroscopic capillary experiment of CBV; it does not report task-based fMRI in human participants or any healthy adult sample performing problem-solving tasks. Therefore it does not meet inclusion criteria.

This is a single-case O-15 PET clinical report of a patient with cerebral venous sinus thrombosis. It is not a task-based fMRI study, includes no healthy adult participants, and does not report task-related brain activation for problem-solving. Therefore it does not meet the review inclusion criteria.
This is an animal model study (epilepsy) focusing on EEG-fMRI artifact suppression; no human participants or healthy adult sample, and not a task-based problem-solving fMRI study.

Animal PET study in rhesus monkeys (non-human), not an fMRI study, and does not involve healthy adult human participants or problem-solving tasks. Does not meet the review’s neuroimaging modality or population criteria.

Task-based fMRI with reported task-related activations, but the task is passive theater-watching and not explicitly a problem-solving/reasoning task (I2 unclear). Include for full-text review to confirm whether the task meets the problem-solving criterion.

The study is a methods paper addressing motion-artifact attenuation for arm-reaching fMRI experiments, not a task-based fMRI investigation of problem-solving or reasoning in healthy adults. It does not meet the review’s focus on problem-solving tasks.

This study examines EEG/fMRI correspondence for visual evoked potentials elicited by a checkerboard stimulus (sensory/visual processing), not a problem-solving or reasoning task; therefore it does not address the review objective.

This article is explicitly a review paper summarizing noninvasive techniques rather than a primary task-based fMRI experiment in healthy adults. It does not report original task-related fMRI activation results, so it meets exclusion criterion E1 (review).

The study investigates motor learning and functional connectivity between muscle representations, not problem-solving or reasoning tasks in healthy adults. It focuses on connectivity changes (connectivity-only emphasis) rather than task-based problem-solving activations relevant to the review objective.

The study is a resting-state fMRI investigation measuring ALFF in moyamoya disease patients; it does not use a task-based problem-solving or reasoning paradigm. It therefore meets exclusion criterion E2 (resting-state study).

This is a review paper about PET myocardial perfusion and translational research, not an original task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It does not involve fMRI task data or report task-related brain activations.

Case report of a single 16-year-old patient with epilepsy; not a task-based fMRI study (uses PET, MR spectroscopy, intraoperative mapping) and contains no healthy adult sample. Does not meet the review requirements.
This is a medicinal chemistry/PET radiotracer development study, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It does not involve fMRI or problem-solving tasks.

The study examines neural responses to smoking cues in smokers, not problem-solving or reasoning tasks in healthy adults. Participants are smokers (clinical behavior), so it fails the healthy adult (I3) and problem-solving task (I2) requirements. Exclude under E3.

The study examines visual-spatial attention using ERPs (with a corresponding fMRI reported) and does not involve a problem-solving or reasoning task in healthy adults. It is not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving, so it does not meet the review objective.

This is a task-based fMRI motor-mapping study using finger flexion-extension in patients with tumors (and 5 healthy volunteers), not a problem-solving or reasoning task. Ages of healthy volunteers are not reported, so cannot confirm inclusion of healthy adults 18–65. Thus it does not meet the review’s task requirement (I2) and likely fails I3.

The paper describes a biophysical model of transient oxygen delivery and fits to tissue oxygen measurements, not a task-based fMRI study of humans performing problem-solving or reasoning. It does not report task-related fMRI activation in healthy adult problem-solving, so it fails the meta-analysis objective.

This is a task-based event-related fMRI study reporting task-related activations in reward/punishment regions. The abstract does not indicate a problem-solving/reasoning task nor specify participant ages/health, so include for full-text review to determine whether I2 and I3 are met.

This is a PET study in photosensitive epileptic baboons (nonhuman animals), not a task-based fMRI study in healthy adult humans. It therefore fails the human healthy adult sample and fMRI modality requirements.

This is a task-based fMRI motor study of ankle dorsiflexion in healthy young adults that reports BOLD activation, but the task is motor movement (lower-limb dorsiflexion), not a problem-solving or reasoning task. It therefore fails inclusion criterion I2 (problem-solving/reasoning).

This is a resting-state fMRI study comparing treatment-naïve MDD patients and healthy controls; it does not involve a task-based problem-solving or reasoning paradigm. Therefore it meets the exclusion criterion for resting-state studies and is not relevant to the task-based problem-solving fMRI review.

Abstract does not describe a problem-solving or reasoning task; however it is a task-based fMRI study in humans reporting activation, and the absence of explicit problem-solving description warrants full-text review to confirm. Included for full-text screening.

This is a methods paper describing an MR-compatible wrist force device and motor force-tracking tasks, not a problem-solving/reasoning task. It does not address task-based fMRI of problem solving in healthy adults and does not report problem-solving-related activations.

The study is a task-based fMRI investigation reporting task-related activations, but the task is verbal working memory/semantic sentence-pair matching rather than a problem-solving or reasoning task, so it does not meet inclusion criterion I2. Abstract does not describe a problem-solving paradigm required for the meta-analysis.

The study examines fMRI responses to alcohol and smoking cues in episodic drinkers/regular smokers and does not involve a problem-solving or reasoning task nor a healthy adult sample. Therefore it does not meet the review objective and lacks a healthy adult group.

The study reports automated analysis of small-animal PET data (mouse atlas registration). It is non-human and non-fMRI, so it does not involve task-based fMRI in healthy adults and does not report task-related brain activation in humans.

The study uses task-based fMRI of Chinese character reading and naming (phonological/semantic training), not a problem-solving or reasoning task required by the review (I2). The abstract reports task-related activations (I1, I4) but does not examine problem-solving in healthy adults, so it is not eligible.

This is a review of small-animal FDG-PET studies of epilepsy (non-human), not a task-based fMRI study in healthy adult humans. It is a review article and uses PET in animals, so it does not meet the review's inclusion criteria.

The study uses task-based fMRI but the tasks are auditory oddball and sensorimotor, not problem-solving or reasoning. The focus is multimodal data fusion and associative networks in schizophrenia rather than task-based problem solving in healthy adults; thus it does not meet the review’s I2 criterion.

This is a methodological study of dynamic functional connectivity applied to resting-state fMRI (not a task-based or problem-solving experiment). It focuses on connectivity estimation methods and resting-state data, so it meets exclusion E2 (resting-state/connectivity-only).

Study uses task-based fMRI in healthy adults but tasks are auditory temporal/perception (gap detection, syllable discrimination), not problem-solving or reasoning. Does not meet inclusion criterion I2 (problem-solving task).

This is a narrative review of functional neuroimaging in primary headaches and discusses patient populations, not a task-based fMRI study of problem-solving in healthy adults. It meets exclusion criteria E1 (review) and E3 (no healthy adult sample).

This is a radiochemistry/PET imaging study of (18)F-labeled leptin applied in ob/ob mice, not a task-based fMRI study of human problem-solving. It involves non-human subjects and a non-fMRI modality, so it does not meet the review criteria.
This study used PET (6-[18F]fluoro-L-DOPA) imaging, not fMRI. It does not report task-based fMRI results of problem solving in healthy adults, so it fails the fMRI requirement.
Task-based fMRI with healthy adult participants performing visuospatial tasks (mental rotation qualifies as a spatial reasoning/problem-solving task); reports task-related activation results.

This is a case-based discussion of dissociated language functions associated with brain injury; it does not report task-based fMRI of problem-solving in healthy adults and does not include a healthy adult sample.

The fMRI task is self-appraisal of traits/abilities, not a problem-solving or reasoning task required by I2. Although the study is task-based fMRI (I1) and reports activation results (I4), it does not address problem solving in healthy adults (the primary review objective).

Methods paper on D-optimal design and simulation for fMRI; no human participants or task performance reported and no task-related brain activation results, so it does not include healthy adult subjects performing a problem-solving task.

This is a task-based auditory fMRI study examining venous contributions to BOLD signals, not a problem-solving or reasoning task. Although it includes healthy adults and reports activation, it does not meet I2 (problem-solving).

Focuses on sexual arousal/erectile dysfunction using visual erotic stimulation, not a problem-solving or reasoning task; thus does not meet the review objective (I2). It is a task-based fMRI study reporting activation, but irrelevant to problem-solving in healthy adults.

Abstract describes real-time fMRI neurofeedback training of insula activation, not a problem-solving or reasoning task. Insufficient information to confirm participants performed a problem-solving task; include for full-text review. Task-based fMRI and task-related activation are evident from the abstract.

Study used task-based fMRI with emotional stimuli in a sample of postpartum women, focusing on postpartum depression; it does not involve a problem-solving/reasoning task nor a healthy adult sample. Therefore it fails inclusion and should be excluded.

This is a case study of two brain-damaged patients with aphasia performing language repetition tasks; no healthy adult participants are included and the tasks are not problem-solving/reasoning. Therefore it does not meet the review’s inclusion criteria.

The study describes PET image reconstruction methods (diffusion MAP, bilateral filtering) using simulated and clinical PET data. It is not a task-based fMRI study, nor does it involve problem-solving tasks in healthy adult participants.

Although this is a task-based fMRI study with healthy adult participants reporting task-related activations, the task involves social perception/emotion (viewing approaching characters with angry/happy expressions), not a problem-solving or reasoning task required by the review (does not meet I2).

This is a case report using FDG PET imaging in sarcoma patients—not a task-based fMRI study, does not involve problem-solving tasks, nor healthy adult participants. Therefore it fails the fMRI and healthy-sample criteria.

Methodological MVPA paper applying voxel selection to visual object-recognition (Haxby) dataset. It does not investigate problem-solving or reasoning tasks, so it fails inclusion criterion I2 (problem-solving task).

This is a brief review of diagnostic methods for minor head trauma rather than an original task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It is a review article and does not report task-related fMRI activation data.

This study describes a PET hardware/educational laboratory course using PET scanners, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It does not report fMRI task-related brain activation or problem-solving tasks.

Study is task-based fMRI with healthy young adult group (23-27) and reports task-related activation. However, the task is episodic memory/recognition rather than an explicit problem-solving or reasoning task based on the abstract. Include for full-text review to confirm whether any reasoning/problem-solving components are present.
The study uses task-based fMRI but the task involves viewing social scenes after exclusion, not a problem-solving or reasoning task. It therefore does not meet the review objective (I2).

This is an fMRI+EEG connectivity study of temporal lobe epilepsy (patient sample) focused on interregional connectivity, not a task-based problem-solving study in healthy adults. It is connectivity-focused and involves patients rather than healthy adult problem-solving tasks.

Task-based fMRI with healthy adult participants and reports task-related activation (amygdala). However, the task is social/voting judgments rather than a clear problem-solving/reasoning paradigm, so include for full-text review to confirm whether it meets the review's definition of problem-solving/reasoning.

This is an FDG-PET case study of 2 patients with diabetic uremia, not a task-based fMRI study, and it does not include healthy adult participants or report task-related fMRI activations. Therefore it does not meet the review criteria.

Study uses task-based fMRI of monocular depth perception (a perceptual/shading task), not a problem-solving or reasoning task required by the review (fails I2). Abstract clearly describes perceptual experiments and fMRI activation but does not involve problem solving.

This is a review article about PET in radiation treatment planning, not an original task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It uses PET (not fMRI) and is explicitly a review. Exclusion criteria E1 and E4 apply.
This study is a PET radioligand imaging methods and data analysis paper using [(11)C]raclopride, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving and does not report fMRI task activation results.

This is a methodological/theoretical fMRI connectivity study focusing on SEM and partial correlations (effective connectivity), not a task-based problem-solving or reasoning fMRI study in healthy adults. It is a connectivity-only study, meeting exclusion criterion E2.

The abstract is a summary of clinical applications of PET with amino acid tracers for low-grade glioma patients (a summary/review), not a task-based fMRI study in healthy adults. It involves patient populations (glioma patients) and PET imaging rather than fMRI. Therefore it meets exclusion criteria E1, E4, and E3.

Abstract describes a methodological fMRI signal-processing paper (Kalman filter) without reporting human participants, task details, or problem-solving tasks. Insufficient information to confirm task-based problem-solving in healthy adults; include for full-text review.

The study is an fMRI methods paper on vascular compartments and ADC vs BOLD signal dynamics, not a task-based problem-solving or reasoning study in healthy adults. It does not investigate problem-solving tasks (I2 not met).

This study used task-based fMRI of thermal (pain) stimulation in burning mouth disorder patients and controls, not a problem-solving or reasoning task. Although it includes healthy adult controls and reports task-related activation (I1, I3, I4 met), it does not meet I2 (problem-solving), so it is excluded.

This is a task-based fMRI study with healthy adult participants and reports task-related activation (satisfies I1, I3, I4), but the tasks are motor imagery and passive shoulder motion related to shoulder apprehension, not problem-solving or reasoning tasks. It therefore does not meet I2 (problem-solving/reasoning) and is excluded.

Task-based fMRI study reporting task-related brain activations. However, the tasks are spatial learning/pointing (not clearly problem-solving/reasoning) and the abstract does not specify participant ages/health explicitly. Include for full-text review to determine whether I2 and I3 are met.

This is a PET imaging review focused on Huntington's disease, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It is a review article, uses non-fMRI methods (PET), and involves patient/population with HD rather than healthy adult participants.

This study used FDG-PET (not fMRI) and investigated metabolic changes in patients with cerebellar ataxia; it does not report task-based fMRI of problem-solving in healthy adults. Therefore it fails the modality requirement for inclusion.

Study uses visual photic stimulation (sensory task), not a problem-solving or reasoning task; thus it does not meet inclusion criterion I2. It does include fMRI data and healthy adults, but task content is not relevant to review objective.

This is a resting-state fMRI study of IBS vs healthy controls examining intrinsic brain oscillations; it does not involve any task-based/problem-solving or reasoning task nor report task-related activations. Excluded per E2.

This article is a conceptual/theoretical discussion of computational approaches and applications to human choice and neurobiology, not an original empirical task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. The abstract indicates a review-style discussion rather than reporting task methods or activation results.

Study uses a target-detection alertness task (visual/auditory), not a problem-solving or reasoning task. Although it is task-based fMRI in healthy adults and reports activation, it does not meet inclusion criterion I2 (problem-solving), so it is excluded.

The task involved passive viewing and visual mapping, not a problem-solving or reasoning task (fails inclusion criterion I2). Although it is task-based fMRI in healthy adults and reports activations (I1, I3, I4), the study does not address problem solving.

This is a review article focused on PET and SPECT radioligands for serotonin imaging in humans, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It explicitly states it is a review and concerns PET/SPECT methods, so E1 and E4 apply.
This is a resting-state fMRI connectivity study in Parkinson's disease, not a task-based problem-solving fMRI study in healthy adults. It does not report task-related activations.

This is a CT-FDG-PET image registration methods study in liver cancer patients; it is not an fMRI study and does not include healthy adults performing problem-solving tasks, so it fails the review criteria.
Participants are described as "youth" with familial alcoholism, not healthy adults (18–65). The abstract does not report a healthy adult sample, so it fails the population criterion for this review.

This is a review article about imaging cardiac stem cell therapy (a review), not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It discusses PET/SPECT/MRI reporter methods in clinical cardiology, with no problem-solving tasks or healthy adult fMRI activation results reported.

This is a review article of metabolic imaging (SPECT, PET, MRS) in mild TBI patients, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It is a review (E1), involves non-fMRI modalities (E4), and does not include healthy adult problem-solving participants (E3).

Abstract describes neuroimaging in Rett syndrome patients using MRI, MRS, DTI, and PET; it does not report task-based fMRI in healthy adults performing problem-solving tasks. No healthy adult sample and not an fMRI task study.

This is a single-case FDG-PET study in a 4.8-year-old boy with a genetic disorder. It is not a task-based fMRI study, does not include healthy adult participants, and does not examine problem-solving tasks. Therefore it clearly fails inclusion criteria.

This is an engineering/methods paper on MRI-compatible intracranial electrodes with phantom measurements and simulations; it does not report task-based fMRI in human healthy adults or task-related activation. No healthy adult participant data are presented.

The study is a PET molecular-imaging study in patients with metastatic breast cancer (clinical pharmacodynamics of endocrine therapies), not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving and does not include healthy adult participants. Therefore it is not relevant to the fMRI problem-solving review.

This study is an imaging study of brown adipose tissue using MRI and FDG-PET, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It does not involve problem-solving or report task-related brain activation relevant to the review.

Task-based fMRI study including healthy control adults performing causality judgments (a form of reasoning/inference). Reports task-related BOLD activation and connectivity results relevant to brain activation during judgments. Details sufficient to warrant full-text review for relevance to problem-solving meta-analysis.

This is a review/commentary on molecular imaging in oncology, not a task-based fMRI experiment. It discusses PET-CT and MRSI in cancer care, includes no healthy adult (18–65) task-based fMRI participants, and does not report task-related fMRI activation results.

This is a narrative/review article about IBS and central sensitization, not a primary task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It does not report task-related fMRI results in healthy adult problem-solving tasks and appears to focus on patients, so it meets exclusion criteria.

This study is a PET imaging metabolic study of skeletal muscle glucose metabolism in obesity, not a task-based fMRI study of problem-solving. It does not involve problem-solving or fMRI methods, so it fails inclusion. Exclude due to non-fMRI modality.

Abstract describes task-based functional neuroimaging of mentalization (Theory of Mind) and reports task-related mPFC activation. It is unclear from the abstract whether the task qualifies as problem-solving/reasoning or details about participant ages/health. Include for full-text review to confirm I2 and I3.

This fMRI study examines auditory verbal hallucinations in schizophrenia patients, not problem-solving or reasoning tasks, and does not include a healthy adult sample (18–65). It therefore fails the healthy adult sample requirement.

Abstract describes a visualization/methods paper for real-time volume rendering of MRI/fMRI data, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving. It does not report participants performing a problem-solving/reasoning task or task-related activation results, and no healthy adult sample is described.

Event-related task-based fMRI study reporting task-related activation during a relational reasoning (Raven-like) task. Includes a healthy adult group (ages 18–25) and reports brain activation results (RLPFC, dorsolateral PFC, parietal). Meets all inclusion criteria.

Study reports task-based fMRI in aphasic patients performing an overt language task before/after therapy. It does not include healthy adult participants nor a problem-solving task, so it fails inclusion criterion I3 (no healthy adult sample). Exclude based on E3.

Task-based hr-fMRI with participants navigating virtual mazes and reporting task-related activation; however abstract does not explicitly state the task is a problem-solving/reasoning task nor provide explicit healthy adult age range. Include for full-text review to confirm I2 and I3.

This is a book chapter/review summarizing anatomical, physiological, and imaging studies of artistic creativity rather than an original task-based fMRI investigation of problem-solving in healthy adults. It is a review article, so it meets exclusion criterion E1.
This is a task-based fMRI study of verbal working memory modulated by amphetamine, not a problem-solving or reasoning task. It therefore does not meet inclusion criterion I2 (problem-solving).
The fMRI task involved emotional image processing (funny/neutral/sad), not a problem-solving or reasoning task; therefore the study does not meet I2 (problem-solving/reasoning).

This study examined fcMRI connectivity in Parkinson's patients (no healthy adult sample) and focuses on functional connectivity/resting and task-based connectivity rather than task-based activation for problem solving. Thus it fails the healthy adult sample requirement and is a connectivity-focused study.

This article is a commentary/perspective discussing an fMRI study in Tourette syndrome rather than an original task-based fMRI investigation of problem-solving in healthy adults. It does not report primary task-based results in a healthy adult sample. Exclusion criteria E1 (review/commentary) and E3 (no healthy adult sample) apply.

The study focuses on delusions in Alzheimer disease patients and aims to examine clinical/neurobiologic correlates in AD. The abstract gives no indication of task-based fMRI or inclusion of healthy adult participants (18–65). Therefore it does not meet the review’s requirement for healthy adult task-based fMRI and should be excluded.

The study uses a social-emotional induction (feeling understood / not understood), not a problem-solving or reasoning task, so it does not meet inclusion criterion I2. The abstract confirms task-based fMRI and reports activation results (I1 and I4), but the task is not problem-solving in healthy adults. Exclude based on failure to meet I2.

This is a PET study of myocardial glucose uptake in patients with PRKAG2 cardiac syndrome, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It uses non-fMRI imaging and involves a diseased sample.

This is a resting-state fMRI study of type 2 diabetes patients investigating spontaneous brain activity; it is not task-based nor involves a problem-solving/reasoning task and does not report a healthy adult sample.

Abstract mentions methamphetamine dependence and affect/aggression but gives no information that participants performed a problem-solving/reasoning task or that a healthy adult sample (18-65) was included. Insufficient information to determine eligibility from abstract alone; include for full-text review.

Study uses PET to assess skeletal muscle perfusion, not task-based fMRI of brain activity or problem-solving. Does not investigate problem-solving tasks in healthy adults. Therefore it is a non-fMRI study and excluded under E4.

This is a review/meta-analysis synthesizing first-person reports and neuroimaging meta-analytic data on mind wandering and dreaming, not an original task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults; thus it meets exclusion criterion E1.

This is a review article on PET molecular imaging in Alzheimer’s disease (review/meta-analysis), not a task-based fMRI study of problem-solving in healthy adults. It involves non-fMRI methods and clinical AD subjects rather than healthy adult problem-solving tasks.

This is a review article about PET tracers and serotonin synthesis, not a task-based fMRI study of problem-solving in healthy adults. It is a review (E1) and focuses on PET/non-fMRI methods (E4), so it does not meet inclusion criteria.

This is a PET imaging study in mice of cancer/nanoparticle distribution; it is not a task-based fMRI study, does not involve human healthy adults or problem-solving tasks. Excluded for non-fMRI modality and no healthy adult sample.

This study uses PET imaging (not task-based fMRI) and investigates cocaine-dependent patients in a treatment setting; no healthy adult problem-solving fMRI task is reported. Thus it does not meet the fMRI/task/problem-solving/healthy-sample criteria.

The study involves patients with disorders of consciousness (not healthy adult participants) and uses PET imaging and qualitative case analyses rather than task-based fMRI, so it fails key inclusion criteria.

This is a narrative review of neuroimaging studies on sex differences in cocaine abuse rather than an original task-based fMRI study of problem-solving in healthy adults; it does not report task-related fMRI results for problem-solving.

This is a review article synthesizing functional connectivity findings in anesthetized states rather than an original task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It focuses on connectivity analyses (resting-state/functional connectivity) and is explicitly a review, so it meets E1 and E2.

This appears to be a commentary/news piece discussing Dobbins et al.'s fMRI findings on memory, not an original task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It does not report primary problem-solving or reasoning tasks and reads as a review/commentary.

Event-related, task-based fMRI study in which human participants solved Chinese 'chengyu' riddles (a problem-solving task). The abstract reports task-related brain activations (early/late periods). Participant health status and exact ages are not specified, so include for full-text review to confirm I3.

This is an overview of noninvasive diagnostic methods in children with cerebral palsy (appears to be a review/report), not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It does not include healthy adult participants or task-related fMRI results.

This is a narrative review of PET/SPECT imaging in glioma patients, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It is a review (E1), involves non-fMRI modalities (E4), and concerns clinical patients rather than healthy adult participants (E3).

The study uses a motor sequence learning (continuous tracking) task, not a problem-solving or reasoning task, so it fails inclusion criterion I2. It is task-based fMRI in healthy middle-aged adults and reports task-related activation, but does not address the review objective.

Participants are a single 3-month-old infant with perinatal stroke; no healthy adult sample and the task is visual stimulation, not problem-solving. Does not meet inclusion of healthy adults performing problem-solving.

Task-based fMRI in human participants reporting task-related activations and likely includes healthy adults. However, the abstract describes anticipatory aversion (threat/anxiety) rather than a problem-solving or reasoning task, so relevance to problem-solving is unclear. Include for full-text review to confirm I2.

The study reports resting-state functional connectivity changes following an exercise intervention in older adults, not a task-based fMRI investigation of problem-solving. It therefore meets exclusion criterion E2 (resting-state/connectivity-only).

This is an animal (isoflurane-anesthetized cats) fMRI study and does not include human participants or a problem-solving task. Therefore it does not meet the review's requirement for healthy adult human problem-solving fMRI studies.

Abstract discusses scintigraphic imaging (SPECT/PET/CT) of adrenal tumors and appears to be a review/overview. It is not a task-based fMRI study, contains no problem-solving task in healthy adults, and reports no task-related fMRI results.

This is a resting-state BOLD connectivity study of the spinal cord in healthy volunteers, not a task-based fMRI investigation of problem-solving or reasoning. It reports resting-state connectivity rather than task-evoked activation, so it meets exclusion criterion E2.

This is a historical account describing the origins of a PET laboratory, not an empirical task-based fMRI study. It reports no human participants performing a problem-solving task and no task-related brain activation. Excluded as a non-fMRI/non-empirical study and for lacking a healthy adult sample.

This study used resting-state fMRI and connectivity analyses in children with BECTS, not a task-based problem-solving fMRI in healthy adults. It does not include adult healthy participants. Therefore it does not meet the review criteria.

Title and abstract indicate a resting-state fMRI study of temporal lobe epilepsy patients (compared to epilepsy without depression and healthy controls). No task-based problem-solving or reasoning paradigm is reported. Excluded due to resting-state design (E2).
This is a resting-state fMRI study of patients with somatization disorder—not a task-based problem-solving fMRI study, and it does not include healthy adult participants.

The study is a connectivity-focused fMRI investigation of the default mode network using partial correlation during rest and a continuous working memory task. It is a resting-state/connectivity-only study and does not report task-related activation for a problem-solving or reasoning task in healthy adults.

The abstract concerns arm use after stroke and brain reorganization in stroke patients—not task-based fMRI of problem solving in healthy adults. It therefore lacks a healthy adult sample required by the review.

The study uses a task-based fMRI paradigm of direct vs averted gaze in PTSD and controls, not a problem-solving or reasoning task (I2 not met). It does include healthy adults and reports activation, but it does not address the review objective of problem-solving tasks.

The study uses task-based fMRI in healthy adults and reports activation, but the task is a visual-cued motor task rather than a problem-solving or reasoning task. It therefore does not meet inclusion criterion I2 (problem-solving task).

Abstract reports an fMRI study of auditory cortex responses to tones, not a problem-solving or reasoning task. Does not meet inclusion criterion I2 (no problem-solving task), so excluded from this review.

This is a preclinical molecular imaging study (not fMRI), with no human healthy adult participants and no task-based brain activation reporting. It does not meet the review objective of task-based fMRI in healthy adults.

This is a non-fMRI, animal (woodchuck) molecular/microarray study of hepatocellular carcinoma; it does not involve task-based fMRI or healthy adult human participants. Exclude due to non-fMRI methods and no healthy adult sample.

This is a PET (non-fMRI) study of autobiographical future/past thinking, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving. It therefore fails the meta-analysis requirement for fMRI methods and relevant task domain.

Abstract describes a lesion case study of auditory emotion recognition and does not report any task-based fMRI methods or results. Therefore it is a non-fMRI study and does not meet the fMRI inclusion criterion.

Study uses task-based fMRI of language processing in temporal lobe epilepsy patients for clinical lateralization/surgical planning. It does not investigate problem-solving/reasoning tasks and appears to involve clinical (epilepsy) patients rather than a healthy adult sample, so it does not meet the review objective.

Abstract describes PET imaging and appears to be a narrative review of PET in dementia, not an empirical task-based fMRI study in healthy adults. It is a non-fMRI paper (E4) and reads as a review (E1).

This is a narrative review of PET/CT imaging in aortic stenosis (not an original task-based fMRI study). It does not involve problem-solving tasks, healthy adult participants, or fMRI methods. Therefore it meets exclusion criteria E1 (review), E3 (no healthy adult sample), and E4 (non-fMRI study).

The abstract describes a methodological/practical report reviewing BOLD fMRI principles and implementation rather than a primary task-based study with healthy adults performing problem-solving tasks or reporting task-related activations; therefore excluded as a review.

This is a review article on PET/PET-CT in pediatric gastrointestinal oncology—non-fMRI modality, pediatric (not healthy adult) patient population, and appears to be a literature overview. It does not meet the task-based fMRI, healthy adult, or primary research criteria.

This study used H2[15]O PET (not fMRI) and applied covariance-based functional connectivity analyses during a Stroop task rather than task-based fMRI activation for problem solving. It therefore fails the fMRI requirement (E4) and is connectivity-focused (E2).
Participants passively watched natural movie clips; this is not a task-based problem-solving or reasoning fMRI study and therefore does not meet inclusion criterion I2 (problem-solving task).

This is a PET study using [(11)C]TMSX to image adenosine A2A receptors in SPMS and controls; it is not a task-based fMRI study and does not involve problem-solving tasks in healthy adults. Therefore it meets exclusion criterion E4 (non-fMRI study).

This is a narrative/review article on imaging of bone metastases, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving. It includes no healthy adult problem-solving fMRI experiments and is non-fMRI. Therefore it meets multiple exclusion criteria.

This study reports pharmacokinetics of methyl palmoxirate and PET use, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It does not involve fMRI task activation or problem-solving tasks, so it is a non-fMRI study.

This is a narrative review of nuclear myocardial perfusion imaging (SPECT/PET) discussing tracer evolution, not an original task-based fMRI study of problem-solving in healthy adults. It is non-fMRI and a review article, so it does not meet inclusion criteria.

This is a preclinical animal study using 64Cu-labeled peptoids and small-animal PET biodistribution; it is not a task-based fMRI study and does not include healthy adult human participants. Excluded for being a non-fMRI study and for lacking a healthy adult sample.

This is a narrative review of fMRI methodologies and applications in nutrition research, not an original task-based fMRI study of problem-solving in healthy adults. It does not report task-related brain activation for problem-solving tasks and meets the exclusion criterion for reviews.

This is an animal (mouse) study using dynamic FDG-PET and photodynamic therapy, not a human task-based fMRI study of problem solving. It does not include healthy adult human participants nor report fMRI task activation related to problem solving.

This is a clinical review of PET imaging in lymphoma (oncology), not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It is a review article and concerns PET, not fMRI; thus it does not meet the review’s inclusion criteria.
This paper is a methodological review of multi-echo acquisition methods in fMRI/MRSI, not an original task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It is therefore a review article.

The abstract describes a review/summary of neurophysiological and PET assessments of neuromodulation for pain, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It is a review and uses non-fMRI methods.
This is a review article on copper chelation chemistry for radiopharmaceuticals, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It is a review (E1) and not an fMRI study (E4).

This is a PET study of remifentanil effects on regional cerebral blood flow in volunteers, not a task-based fMRI investigation of problem solving. It does not assess problem-solving tasks or use fMRI.

This study is a PET/CT imaging study in patients with metastatic breast cancer (clinical patient sample), not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It involves no healthy adult participants and is not an fMRI study, so it does not meet the review criteria.

Abstract describes in vitro and small-animal in vivo evaluation using PET and radiolabeled nanoparticles, not task-based fMRI in humans. No healthy adult human sample is reported and the modality is PET rather than fMRI. Excluded for non-fMRI modality and absence of healthy adult participants.

This study used PET ([11C]DASB) to measure serotonin transporter binding in healthy individuals, not task-based fMRI. It does not describe a problem-solving or reasoning task or report task-related fMRI activations. Therefore it does not meet the fMRI task-based requirement for the review.

This is a review focusing on technical aspects of fMRI in awake rats (non-human subjects) rather than a task-based fMRI study of problem-solving in healthy adult humans. It therefore meets exclusion criteria for being a review and for not including a healthy adult human sample.

This is a task-based fMRI study in healthy adults that reports task-related activation, but the task involves evaluating attractiveness/mating motivation after mortality salience priming—not a problem-solving or reasoning task. It therefore does not meet inclusion criterion I2 (problem-solving/reasoning).

This is a pediatric case functional neuroimaging study using SPECT and FDG-PET in an infant with early myoclonic encephalopathy; it is not a task-based fMRI study and includes no healthy adult participants.

Abstract and title indicate a resting-brain/connectivity focus on risk-related behavior rather than a task-based problem-solving fMRI study. Does not describe participants performing a problem-solving or reasoning task.

Task-based event-related fMRI study presenting arithmetic equations requiring subjects to judge correct vs incorrect answers (a reasoning/problem-solving task). Reports greater brain activation for incorrect vs correct equations (task-related activation). Abstract does not explicitly state participant ages or confirm ‘healthy adults’, so full-text review is recommended to verify I3.

This article is a review (not an original task-based fMRI study) summarizing functional neuroimaging in traumatic brain injury. It does not report primary task-based fMRI results of problem-solving in healthy adults. Exclude per E1.

The study uses a monetary incentive delay reward-processing task, not a problem-solving or reasoning task; therefore it does not address the review objective. It is task-based fMRI with healthy adult participants and reports activation, but I2 (problem-solving task) is not met.

Study is a PET imaging study in non-human primates (rhesus monkeys), not a task-based fMRI study in human adults performing problem-solving tasks. Therefore it lacks fMRI modality and healthy adult human participants.

This study reports PASL MRI cerebral blood flow measurements and methodological comparisons with PET; it is not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving and does not report task-related activation. It is therefore a non-fMRI study.

Task-based fMRI with participants viewing images and captions producing insight/humor—this is a problem-solving/insight task and the abstract reports task-related brain activation. The abstract does not explicitly state participant ages/health, so include for full-text review to confirm healthy adult sample.

The fMRI task involves viewing emotional facial expressions (fear, anger, disgust), not a problem-solving or reasoning task. Although it is task-based fMRI and includes healthy adult controls with reported task-related activations, it does not meet inclusion criterion I2 (problem-solving/reasoning), so it is not eligible for this review.

Participants are criminal inmates (psychopaths and non-psychopaths), so no healthy adult sample (E3). Additionally, the task involves reward/loss processing, not a problem-solving or reasoning task, so it does not address the review objective. Exclusion is clear from the abstract.

This is a task-based fMRI study of pain modulation in fibromyalgia patients using hypnotic and non-hypnotic suggestion, not a problem-solving/reasoning task, and the sample is clinical (fibromyalgia) with no healthy adult group reported. Excluded due to no healthy adult sample and mismatch with the review objective.

This is a task-based fMRI study of reward anticipation, not a problem-solving or reasoning task (does not meet I2). It does include healthy adult volunteers and reports task-related BOLD results, but the task domain (monetary reward anticipation) is outside the review objective.

This is a PET study in vervet monkeys (non-human), not a task-based fMRI study in healthy adult humans. It therefore lacks a human healthy adult sample and does not use fMRI for task-related activation, so it does not meet the review objective.

Abstract is a review of molecular imaging in animal models of abdominal aortic aneurysm, not a task-based fMRI study in healthy adults. It is explicitly a review (E1), involves non-human/animal models (E3), and is not an fMRI study (E4).

This is a PET (H2[15]O) study of nitroglycerin-triggered migraine in patients, not a task-based fMRI study of problem-solving in healthy adults. It therefore fails inclusion criteria I1/I2/I3 and meets exclusion criteria E4 (non-fMRI) and E3 (no healthy adult sample).

The study used PET (not fMRI), involved patients with severe post-hypoxic encephalopathy (no healthy adult sample), and did not investigate a problem-solving task or report task-related fMRI activation. It therefore fails the review’s inclusion criteria and meets exclusion criteria E4 and E3.

This is a PET imaging study (18F-FDOPA and 11C-raclopride) of familial parkinsonism, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It does not report task-related fMRI activation results.

This is a biomedical PET/BAL study of peripheral benzodiazepine receptors in interstitial lung disease patients, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving. It does not include healthy adult participants performing problem-solving tasks or report task-related fMRI activations.

The task involves empathy/pain perspective-taking, not problem-solving or reasoning. Although this is a task-based fMRI study with healthy adults and reports activations (I1, I3, I4), it does not meet I2 (problem-solving/reasoning task).
This article is a quantitative meta-analysis of neuroimaging studies rather than a primary task-based fMRI experiment, so it meets exclusion criterion E1 (review/meta-analysis).
This is a review article summarizing neuroimaging literature, not an original task-based fMRI study of problem-solving in healthy adults. It meets exclusion criterion E1 (review/meta-analysis).

This is a review article summarizing neuroimaging findings in sleep disorders rather than an original task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults; therefore it meets the review/meta-analysis exclusion criterion.

Task-based fMRI study in healthy volunteers performing the number reduction problem-solving task; reports task-related brain activation results and neural differences between solvers and nonsolvers. Satisfies all inclusion criteria.

Study used high-density EEG to examine functional/effective connectivity during word reading (a language task), not task-based fMRI of problem solving in healthy adults. It is a non-fMRI, connectivity-focused study and does not address problem-solving tasks.

The abstract describes PET/SPECT radioligands for cardiac adrenoceptors and reads as a review of imaging agents, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It is non-fMRI and appears to be a review article, so it does not meet the review objective.

This is a review article about PET/CT dosimetry in children, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It is a review (E1), concerns pediatric patients not healthy adults (E3), and involves PET/CT not fMRI (E4).

Study used resting-state fMRI and phosphorous CSI to examine correlations in depression, not a task-based problem-solving/reasoning paradigm. Therefore it meets the resting-state/connectivity-only exclusion.

The study used an event-related motor task, not a problem-solving or reasoning task. While it is task-based fMRI in adult humans and reports hemodynamic activation measures, it does not address problem solving (I2 not satisfied).

This is a cardiac PET study of myocardial perfusion and angina, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It does not involve fMRI or healthy adult problem-solving participants.

The study uses fMRI to examine brain responses to acupuncture and correlate with heart rate variability—not a problem-solving or reasoning task. It does not meet inclusion criterion I2 (task is not problem solving), so it’s not relevant to the review objective.

This study uses task-based fMRI of tongue motor activation and compares BOLD with optical signals; it does not involve problem-solving or reasoning tasks in healthy adults. The abstract also does not specify a healthy adult sample (ESM suggests surgical patients).

The study uses PET imaging of neuroreceptor systems and presents an exploratory imaging/data-mining method; it is not a task-based fMRI study, nor does it involve problem-solving tasks or report task-related fMRI activation. Therefore it is a non-fMRI study and excluded.

This is a 2015 review article in medicinal chemistry about benzofuran derivatives, not a task-based fMRI study nor involving human problem-solving; it is a review and non-fMRI work. Exclusion criteria E1 (review) and E4 (non-fMRI) apply.

This is a narrative Review focused on PET/CT molecular imaging in oncology, not an empirical task-based fMRI study of problem-solving in healthy adults. It is a review article and concerns PET/CT (non-fMRI).

This is a chemical kinetics study of Ga(NOTA) formation for radiopharmaceutical chemistry; it is not a task-based fMRI study, includes no human participants or healthy adult sample, and reports no brain activation data.

This is a resting-state / connectivity fMRI study of the default mode network and its response to physical stress in concussed vs. control subjects, not a task-based problem-solving or reasoning fMRI study. Therefore it meets exclusion criterion E2.

Study examines brain activation in cocaine-dependent patients performing the Stroop task; abstract does not mention any healthy adult (18–65) sample. Therefore it does not meet the requirement for a healthy adult group (I3), meeting exclusion E3.

This is a methodological PET imaging paper using simulations and algorithm analysis, not a task-based fMRI study of human problem solving. It involves no healthy adult participants and is a non-fMRI modality.

This is a narrative review about radiolabeled antibodies and diagnostics, not an original task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It does not involve human participants performing problem-solving tasks nor report task-related fMRI activations. Exclusion criteria E1 and E4 apply.

This study is a PET imaging study of sarcoma patients (clinical oncology), not a task-based fMRI study of problem-solving in healthy adults. It uses FDG-PET and patient samples rather than healthy adult participants, so it fails the fMRI requirement and the healthy adult sample requirement.

This is a narrative feature/review on radiometals in nuclear oncology, not an original task-based fMRI study. It does not report human task-based fMRI data, has no healthy adult participants performing problem-solving tasks, and contains no task-related brain activation results.

This is a narrative review about imaging amyloid plaques with PET and MRI, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It is a review article (E1) and does not report task-based fMRI data (E4).

This is not an fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults; it discusses FDG-PET/CT imaging in breast cancer and appears to be a review. Does not involve task-based fMRI, problem-solving tasks, or healthy adult samples.

This is not a task-based fMRI study: it uses F-18 FDG PET/CT imaging in diabetic Charcot neuro-osteoarthropathy. The abstract does not report problem-solving tasks or healthy adult participants. Excluded for being a non-fMRI study and for lacking a healthy adult sample.

This is a review article about spontaneous BOLD fluctuations/resting-state connectivity, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It is a review (E1) and focuses on resting-state/connectivity (E2).

The study uses a motor learning/unilateral movement task rather than a problem-solving or reasoning task, so it does not meet inclusion criterion I2. It is task-based fMRI in healthy adults and reports activation results (I1, I3, I4 satisfied).

The task is associative learning/memory, not a problem-solving or reasoning task required by the review (I2 not met). The study is task-based 7T fMRI in healthy adults and reports activation (I1, I3, I4 satisfied). No exclusion criteria (E1–E4) are met.

This is a first-person historical account of the discovery of BOLD fMRI, not a task-based study of problem solving in healthy adults. The abstract does not describe participants performing problem-solving or reasoning tasks nor report task-related activation in healthy adult groups.

This is a PET case report of a pituitary incidentaloma, not a task-based fMRI study. It does not involve problem-solving or reasoning tasks nor a healthy adult sample. Therefore it does not meet inclusion criteria.

This is a psychophysical study of taste stimulus delivery techniques, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving. It does not report task-related brain activation in healthy adults and appears to be a non-fMRI behavioral/psychophysics study, so it does not meet the review objective.

Study uses task-based fMRI of visual encoding/recognition (memory), not a problem-solving or reasoning task; thus it fails inclusion criterion I2. It does include healthy subjects and reports activation, but the task does not address the review objective.

Study is about nuclear cardiac imaging in patients with left ventricular dysfunction (clinical patient population), not task-based fMRI in healthy adults. It appears to be a review/perspective rather than an original fMRI study. Fails inclusion criteria I1, I2, I3 and meets exclusion criteria E4 (non-fMRI), E3 (no healthy adult sample), and likely E1 (review).

The article is a review about PET/SPECT nuclear imaging in breast cancer, not an original task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It therefore meets E1 (review) and E4 (non-fMRI modality).

This is a PET study measuring resting-state glucose metabolism in epileptic infants/children (and some adult controls), not a task-based fMRI study of problem-solving in healthy adults. No task-related fMRI activation data are reported.

This study describes PET scintillator light-transport simulations and surface characterization; it is not an fMRI study and involves no human participants or healthy adult sample. Therefore it is a non-fMRI study and does not meet participant criteria.

This article is a review of the literature on neuroprogression in bipolar disorder, not an original task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It therefore meets the exclusion criterion for reviews/meta-analyses.

This article is a review/meta-analysis of neuroimaging studies of self-reflection (not an original task-based fMRI study of problem solving). It therefore meets exclusion criterion E1 (reviews or meta-analyses).

This study is an economic/activity-based costing analysis of 18F-FDG production, not a task-based fMRI investigation. It does not involve human participants performing problem-solving tasks nor report task-related brain activation. Therefore it is a non-fMRI study and has no healthy adult sample relevant to the review.

This study is a methodological evaluation of scatter correction techniques for 3D PET imaging, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults; no fMRI task-related brain activation results are reported.

This is a PET study of Alzheimer’s patients assessing glucose metabolism; it is not task-based fMRI, does not involve healthy adult participants, and does not report task-related fMRI activations for problem-solving.

The task is a self-evaluation (personality/illness terms) in schizophrenia patients and controls, not a problem-solving or reasoning task, so it does not meet inclusion criterion I2. It is a task-based fMRI study reporting task-related activation (I1, I4); healthy controls are included but ages not specified (I3 unclear). Excluded because the task does not address problem solving in healthy adults.
Resting-state fMRI in anesthetized children — not a task-based problem-solving study and does not include healthy adult participants. Meets exclusion criteria E2 and E3.

The study is about automated PET tumour/lesion delineation methods and appears to be a methodological/review paper for clinical imaging. It is not a task-based fMRI study, does not involve problem-solving tasks or healthy adult participants, and reads like a review. Exclusion criteria E4 (non-fMRI) and E1 (review/meta-analysis) apply.

The study uses task-based fMRI in healthy adults but investigates empathy for emotions (happy, sad, anxious) rather than problem-solving or reasoning tasks, so it does not meet the review’s required task domain (I2).

This is a narrative review of placental pathology in pre-eclampsia and IUGR, not an empirical task-based fMRI study. It is a review (E1), not an fMRI study (E4), and does not include healthy adult participants performing problem-solving tasks (E3).

The study examines anticipatory neural responses to romantic caress, not problem-solving or reasoning tasks, so it does not meet inclusion criterion I2. It is a task-based fMRI study reporting task-related activations (I1, I4), but the task is irrelevant to the review objective.

The study participants are boys aged 10–18 with no healthy adult (18–65) sample, so it does not meet the requirement for healthy adults (E3). The task also assesses social/affective processing rather than problem-solving, but the clear exclusion is lack of adult healthy participants.

Participants are anesthetized rhesus monkeys (non-human), and the task is olfactory stimulation rather than problem solving in healthy adult humans; therefore the study does not include the required healthy adult sample or relevant task.

This study used PET imaging to measure glucose uptake in adipose tissue and does not involve fMRI or task-based cognitive/problem-solving paradigms. It therefore does not meet the meta-analysis focus on task-based fMRI problem-solving in healthy adults.

This is an animal PET study in rats measuring P-glycoprotein function; it is not a task-based fMRI study and includes no human healthy adult participants. Exclude due to non-fMRI modality and absence of human adult sample.

The abstract describes sequential PET/MRI feasibility in small animals using a benchtop 1T scanner—this is not a human task-based fMRI study, includes no healthy adult participants, and does not report task-related fMRI activation.

This is a PET imaging study ([11C]DASB-PET) of serotonergic transmission in HIV-infected patients, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. The sample comprises HIV-infected participants (no healthy adult group) and the modality is non-fMRI, so it clearly fails inclusion criteria.

Study investigates neural predictors of psychotherapy outcome in patients with persistent depression, not task-based problem-solving fMRI in healthy adults. Population appears clinical (depressed) and not the target healthy adult sample or task domain.

This study investigates bone marrow cell therapy in acute STEMI patients using PET and MRI. It is not a task-based fMRI study, does not involve problem-solving tasks, and the sample comprises patients rather than healthy adults.

Task-based event-related fMRI study using the Chinese Remote Associates Test (a problem-solving/insight task), compares meditation vs relaxation, reports task-related brain activation. Although the abstract does not explicitly state participant ages, it describes healthy adult cognitive testing; full text can confirm age range. Meets inclusion criteria and should be retained for full-text review.

Abstract describes an overview of molecular imaging and luminescence techniques in traditional Chinese medicine; it reads as a review and does not report a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It is non-fMRI and appears to be a review article.

This is a narrative review of neuroimaging methods in repetitive brain trauma, not an original task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It does not report task-related fMRI results for problem-solving in healthy adult participants and meets the review exclusion criterion.

Tasks were opening the eyes and finger movements, not problem-solving or reasoning tasks; therefore the study does not address the review objective despite being a task-based fMRI study of healthy adults.

The article is a review of MRI techniques (not an original task-based fMRI experiment) and therefore meets the exclusion criterion for reviews/meta-analyses. It does not report original task-related activation in healthy adults.

The study investigates haemodynamic changes related to seizures/epileptic activity (clinical context) rather than a task-based problem-solving paradigm and does not report data from healthy adult problem-solving participants. Therefore it fails the requirement for a healthy adult sample performing a problem-solving task.

This is a review article evaluating brain imaging contributions to panic disorder, not an original task-based fMRI study of problem-solving in healthy adults. It meets the exclusion criterion for reviews/meta-analyses.

Study investigates EMG-fMRI for tremor and surgical targeting in patients, not task-based problem solving in healthy adults. Does not include healthy adult problem-solving tasks, so it fails the healthy-sample and task-type requirements.

This is a task-based fMRI study with healthy adults reporting task activations (I1, I3, I4), but the task is memory/learning, not problem-solving or reasoning (I2 not met). Therefore it does not address the review objective.
This is an animal PET study in cats of decompressive hemicraniectomy; it is not an fMRI study and includes no healthy adult human participants. Therefore it does not meet the review objective.

This is a molecular/developmental neuroscience study using transgenic models and chromatin assays focused on transcriptional regulation of serotonin neurons; it does not involve task-based fMRI, problem-solving tasks, or healthy adult human participants.

The study is a PET imaging/radiochemistry paper on a drug and its C-11-labeled metabolites, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It is a non-fMRI study and therefore excluded.

This is a PET imaging study using [(18)F]FLT to monitor immune responses in cancer patients, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It is non-fMRI and does not include healthy adult participants performing problem-solving tasks.

This study is a chemistry paper describing synthesis and photophysical characterization of fluorescent sensor molecules (BODIPY polyamide receptors) for Hg2+ detection. It does not involve fMRI, human participants, or problem-solving tasks, so it does not meet the review inclusion criteria.

This article is a review article about fMRI adaptation methods and neural representations, not an original task-based fMRI study of problem-solving in healthy adults. It does not report task-related problem-solving activation in a healthy adult sample. Excluded as a review.

This is a clinical report of gallbladder cancer metastases, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It does not involve fMRI, problem-solving tasks, or healthy adult participants.

This is a PET case report of a patient with pulmonary alveolar microlithiasis, not a task-based fMRI study. No problem-solving task, no healthy adult sample, and no task-related fMRI activation results are reported. Excludes per E3 and E4.

This study used NIRS and transcranial Doppler (non-fMRI modalities) and measured hemodynamic relationships during hyper/hypocapnia, not task-based fMRI of problem-solving. It therefore does not meet the fMRI requirement.

Abstract reports a task-based fMRI study in humans performing insight/analytic problem-solving, reports task-related brain activation (ACC), and involves adult participants. Therefore it meets I1–I4 based on the abstract.

This is a review article about cochlear implants using positron emission tomography (PET). It is a review (E1) and focuses on PET rather than fMRI (E4); it does not report task-based fMRI results of problem-solving in healthy adults.

Abstract describes schizophrenia patients and poor insight; it does not indicate a sample of healthy adults performing a problem-solving/reasoning task. Therefore it does not meet the requirement for a healthy adult sample relevant to the review.

Task-based fMRI study (Study 2) examining insight problem solving in human participants and reporting task-related activations (angular gyrus, MPFC). Age range/healthy adult status not specified in abstract; full text needed to confirm I3.

Methods paper applying improved PVE correction to ASL fMRI in patients with asymptomatic carotid occlusive disease during rest and motor activation — not a problem-solving task and participants are patients (no healthy adult sample).
The study is resting-state fMRI and focuses on deconvolution/effective connectivity methods, not a task-based problem-solving or reasoning fMRI study in healthy adults.

This is a review article that synthesizes simulation and connectivity studies (resting-state and effective/functional connectivity) rather than an original task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. Exclude on review/connectivity grounds.

This study uses fMRI adaptation to study object-selective cortical responses (LOC) — it is not a problem-solving or reasoning task study (fails I2). It reports fMRI results but addresses object perception, not problem solving in healthy adults.

This is an animal (rat) study using 7T BOLD fMRI with forepaw stimulation and pharmacological manipulation. It does not include human participants or healthy adults performing problem-solving tasks, so it fails the human adult sample criterion.

This is a review article focused on multimodality oncologic imaging (PET/SPECT/CT/MR), not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It is explicitly a review and does not report task-related fMRI results in healthy adults.

Although this is a task-based fMRI study in healthy adults reporting task-related activations (amygdala, ACC, hippocampus, insula), the task is fear conditioning (startle/US-expectancy), not a problem-solving or reasoning task. It therefore fails inclusion criterion I2.

Abstract explicitly states this is a 'review article' and discusses EEG/connectome and cognitive training rather than reporting an original task-based fMRI study in healthy adults. Therefore it is a review (E1) and non-fMRI (E4).

This is a resting-state fMRI connectivity study in children with ADHD and child healthy controls, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. Therefore it meets exclusion criteria E2 (resting-state/connectivity-only) and E3 (no healthy adult sample).

This study is a resting-state fMRI investigation comparing eyes-open vs eyes-closed spontaneous activity and uses fALFF and SVM; it does not involve any task-based problem-solving or reasoning tasks, so it meets exclusion criterion E2.

The study investigates visuospatial/perceptual deficits in Parkinson’s disease patients (cognitively unimpaired PD) and does not include healthy adult participants or report problem-solving task-based fMRI in healthy adults. Therefore it does not meet the review population criterion.

The study uses a task-based fMRI appetitive conditioning paradigm (reward learning), not a problem-solving or reasoning task; it therefore does not address the review objective (I2 not met). Healthy adult controls are included and task activations are reported, but the task type excludes it from this review.

The article is a clinical review discussing FDG-PET for thoracic vascular disease (non-fMRI). It does not report task-based fMRI in healthy adults nor any problem-solving task-related activations. Therefore it meets exclusion criteria for being a review, non-fMRI, and not including healthy adult samples performing tasks.

This article is a literature review of Na18F PET/CT for detecting bone metastases in oncology. It is a review (E1), uses PET imaging not fMRI (E4), and does not involve task-based problem-solving in healthy adults (E3).

This paper describes ChIA-PET sequencing and chromatin interactions (genomics), not task-based fMRI nor human problem-solving tasks. It is a non-fMRI study, so it does not meet the review eligibility.

This study uses [18F]-FDG PET (not fMRI) and investigates olanzapine-induced acute akathisia rather than a problem-solving task; it likely involves clinical patients rather than healthy adult participants.

This is a resting-state fMRI connectivity study comparing DMN connectivity in schizophrenia patients and controls; no task was performed and no problem-solving/reasoning task is reported. Thus it meets exclusion criterion E2 (resting-state or connectivity-only study).

This study is not a task-based fMRI study: it reports PET/CT imaging of carotid plaques in a single 75-year-old stroke patient, not healthy adults performing problem-solving tasks. Therefore it fails the fMRI requirement and has no healthy adult sample.
Study involves 41 Alzheimer disease patients (no healthy adult group) and examines frontal hypometabolism (likely PET), not a task-based fMRI problem-solving study. Does not meet inclusion criteria.

This is a review of resting-state fMRI in neonates, not a task-based fMRI study of problem-solving in healthy adults. It is a review article (E1), focuses on resting-state methods (E2), and studies neonates rather than healthy adult participants (E3).

This is a materials-science study of epoxy/PET interface using spectroscopy; it is not an fMRI study, involves no human participants performing problem-solving tasks, and reports no task-related brain activation.

Task-based fMRI of 3D shape perception where participants made perceptual judgments of slanted planes; this is a perceptual judgment task, not a problem-solving or reasoning task. Meets I1 and I4 but does not satisfy I2 (not a problem-solving task).

This is a review article focusing on intrinsic (resting-state) BOLD fMRI and clinical applications rather than a task-based problem-solving study in healthy adults. It meets exclusion criteria E1 (review/meta-analysis) and E2 (resting-state/connectivity focus).

This is a review article discussing methods for assessing visceral hypersensitivity in IBS, not an original task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It does not report task-related fMRI results in healthy adults.

The abstract describes a review of transcranial magnetic stimulation methods in schizophrenia, not an original task-based fMRI study of problem-solving in healthy adults. It is a review/meta-article (E1) and focuses on TMS/neurophysiologic methods rather than fMRI (E4).

Preclinical mouse xenograft imaging study using bioluminescence and FDG-PET; not a task-based fMRI study and contains no human healthy adult participants. Does not meet fMRI (I1) or human healthy adult (I3) inclusion criteria.

This study is a PET/CT imaging study of postoperative patients (Taylor Spatial Frame) and does not use fMRI or involve healthy adult participants performing problem-solving tasks. It is therefore a non-fMRI study and does not include a healthy adult sample.

This is a TMS motor-mapping feasibility study in awake children under 3 years. It is not an fMRI study and does not include healthy adult participants or a problem-solving task. Therefore it meets exclusions for non-fMRI methods and no healthy adult sample.

This is a review article discussing MRI methods in multiple sclerosis rather than a primary task-based fMRI experiment. It does not involve healthy adult participants performing problem-solving tasks. Exclusion criteria E1 and E3 apply.

This is a resting-state fMRI connectivity study comparing patients with frontal lobe epilepsy to controls; it does not report task-based activation or problem-solving tasks. Meets exclusion criterion E2 (resting-state/connectivity-only).
Study uses task-based fMRI of interpersonal emotion regulation, not a problem‑solving or reasoning task as required by the review (I2 not met).

Abstract reports task-based fMRI BOLD responses to visual stimuli in healthy adult volunteers and reports task-related activation, but it does not specify a problem-solving or reasoning task. Insufficient information to determine I2 from the abstract, so include for full-text review.

The study examined motor cortex activation in stroke patients during arm-focused therapy and did not involve healthy adult participants or problem-solving tasks. Therefore it does not include a healthy adult sample required by the review.

The study is a task-based fMRI experiment of induced perceptual insight (an insight/problem-solving task) in human participants and reports task-related brain activation (amygdala). The abstract does not specify participant ages/health; full-text needed to confirm I3.

This is a resting-state connectivity study in patients with cluster headache, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It does not involve a problem-solving task and does not include healthy adult participants. Therefore it meets exclusion criteria E2 and E3.

This article is a review of DTI and DTI+fMRI findings in schizophrenia and genetic risk, not an original task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It is therefore a review/meta-analysis and does not meet the review’s objective.

Abstract reports fMRI work in ADHD and schizophrenia using a stop (inhibition) task and discusses patient group comparisons. It does not involve a problem-solving/reasoning task nor does it report a healthy adult sample (18–65). Therefore it fails to meet the review’s key criteria.

This fMRI study examines aversive classical conditioning and reports functional connectivity analyses rather than problem-solving or reasoning. It is a connectivity-focused task-based fMRI study, meeting exclusion criterion E2 (resting-state or connectivity-only studies).

This is a psychosocial intervention study protocol for schizophrenia patients, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It involves no fMRI, no healthy adult sample, and does not report task-related brain activation.

This is a molecular enzymology/biotechnology study on engineered bacterial cutinase with no human participants and no neuroimaging. It is not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults.

This is an animal (mouse neonate) study of serotonergic neuron development and breathing; it is not an fMRI study and does not include healthy adult human participants, so it fails key inclusion criteria for the review.
