This article discusses imaging diagnostics of bone sarcomas (clinical/radiologic context) rather than task-based fMRI of problem solving. It does not involve healthy adult participants or report task-related fMRI activations. Therefore it fails the review’s inclusion criteria.

Participants are patients with Alzheimer's and age-matched healthy controls described as "normal healthy elderly" (not healthy adults aged 18–65). Tasks reported (semantic association and n-back working memory) are not problem-solving/reasoning tasks. Does not meet the review population and task requirements.

This is a task-based fMRI study of auditory tone perception in Huntington's disease and controls, not a problem-solving or reasoning task, so it fails inclusion criterion I2. The abstract clearly describes auditory stimulation rather than problem solving.

Non-human (macaque) study focusing on dopamine effects in visual cortex; does not involve human adults or a problem-solving task. Therefore it fails the requirement for a healthy adult human sample and the target task domain.

Preclinical SPECT cell-tracking study in immunodeficient mice; not a human fMRI study, does not involve healthy adult participants, and does not examine task-based problem solving. Does not meet the review’s fMRI/human/problem-solving criteria.

This is a resting-state simultaneous fMRI/LFP study in rats (non-human) and does not involve a task-based problem-solving paradigm in healthy adult humans. Excluded for being resting-state and for not having a healthy adult human sample.

This study used PET and SPECT (not fMRI) and investigated patients in a persistent vegetative state rather than healthy adults. It therefore fails the modality requirement and the healthy adult sample requirement.

This article is a review of neuroimaging in arthritis pain (not an original task-based fMRI study of problem solving) and focuses on patient populations rather than healthy adults, meeting exclusion criteria E1 and E3.

The study sample consists of adolescents (11 FH+, 7 FH-) with no adult (18–65) participants, failing the healthy adult population requirement. The task is an emotional Stroop (emotional conflict), not a problem-solving/reasoning task. Therefore exclusion criterion E3 (no healthy adult sample) applies.

This is a review of molecular imaging in gliomas (PET/MR/optical), not a task-based fMRI experiment of problem solving in healthy adults. It is a review article, does not report task-based fMRI data, and does not include healthy adult participants.

The study uses resting-state fMRI to examine amygdala connectivity after neurosteroid administration, not a task-based problem-solving or reasoning fMRI paradigm. It is therefore excluded as a resting-state/connectivity-only study.

The study is a task-based fMRI connectivity (Granger causality) investigation of saccade and vergence eye movements, not a problem-solving/reasoning task. It is a connectivity-only study, meeting exclusion criterion E2.

This is a resting-state functional connectivity study in early-stage Parkinson’s patients (with healthy controls), not a task-based fMRI investigation of problem-solving or reasoning in healthy adults. It therefore does not meet the review’s requirement for task-based problem-solving fMRI and should be excluded.

This is a task-based fMRI study reporting task-related neural activation associated with insight-driven reconfiguration of memories. The abstract does not explicitly state participants were healthy adults or that the task was a formal problem-solving/reasoning task, so full-text review is needed to confirm I2 and I3.

This is a resting-state functional connectivity study in schizophrenia spectrum disorders (patient sample), not a task-based fMRI of problem-solving in healthy adults. It therefore meets exclusion criteria E2 (resting-state/connectivity-only) and E3 (no healthy adult sample).

The study used a working memory task, not a problem-solving or reasoning task targeted by the review (does not meet I2). It is a task-based fMRI study in humans reporting task-related activation, but the task domain falls outside the review objective.

The study is an fMRI investigation of activity during rest/interoception and emotions in healthy subjects, not a task-based problem-solving or reasoning paradigm. It therefore qualifies as a resting-state style study and does not meet the review’s focus on task-based problem-solving tasks (E2).

This is an fMRI study of visual processing in amblyopia, not a problem-solving or reasoning task in healthy adults. Although it uses task-based fMRI and includes controls, it does not address problem-solving, so it does not meet I2.
exclusion_criteria_applied: Does not involve a problem-solving/reasoning task (fails I2)

abstract_screening_results.reason: This is a software description/update paper about image analysis tools; it does not report a task-based fMRI study, includes no human participants or task-related activation results. Therefore it does not meet inclusion criteria for task-based fMRI problem-solving studies in healthy adults.

This is a materials/chemistry study of chiral gold nanoparticles with no human participants or neuroimaging. It is not a task-based fMRI study, so it fails inclusion and meets exclusion criteria for non-fMRI work and absence of healthy adult sample.

This is a task-based fMRI study that includes a healthy adult control group and reports task-related brain activations. The abstract does not clearly specify that the tasks are problem-solving/reasoning; therefore include for full-text review to determine if I2 is met.

Abstract describes PET radiopharmaceuticals and appears to be a review article; it does not report any task-based fMRI, nor any problem-solving/reasoning tasks in healthy adults. Excluded per E1 (review) and E4 (non-fMRI study).

This study examines motor sequence learning using dynamic causal modeling to assess effective connectivity (cortico-striatal-cerebellar interactions) during a serial reaction time task. It is a connectivity-focused fMRI study and does not investigate problem-solving or reasoning tasks in healthy adults. Exclude per E2 (connectivity-only studies).

This is an auditory fMRI study in songbirds (non-human animal model) and does not examine problem-solving or reasoning tasks in healthy adult humans. It therefore fails to include a healthy adult human sample and does not address the review objective.

The study is a resting-state fMRI connectivity investigation in stroke patients (with healthy controls) examining hippocampal functional connectivity and effects of cognitive therapy. It does not involve a task-based problem-solving or reasoning paradigm in healthy adults, so it meets exclusion E2 (resting-state or connectivity-only).

Task-based fMRI in healthy adults studying adaptation to degraded speech with behavioral comprehension tests; however participants performed perceptual/listening training, not a problem-solving or reasoning task, so it fails inclusion criterion I2.

This is a resting-state EEG-fMRI study examining resting state networks and electrophysiological correlation patterns during a long resting scan, not a task-based problem-solving or reasoning fMRI experiment. Excluded per E2 (resting-state/connectivity-only study).

Abstract describes a task-based fMRI study in 19 healthy adults and reports region-level/spatio-temporal activation and classification results, but the tasks (motor imagery vs. spatial navigation) are not clearly problem-solving or reasoning. Insufficient detail to confirm I2—include for full-text review.

The study examines PET and SPECT imaging of substance abuse and reads as an overview/review rather than a task-based fMRI experiment in healthy adults. It is non-fMRI and appears to be a review, so it does not meet the review’s modality or design requirements.

The study used task-based fMRI in healthy adults and reports activation, but the experimental task is free-viewing of movie clips measuring empathy, not a problem-solving or reasoning task (fails inclusion criterion I2).

This paper discusses PET-based brain function analysis (not fMRI) and appears to be a narrative discussion of advantages/limitations based on authors' experiences. It does not report task-based fMRI of problem solving in healthy adults or task-related fMRI activations. Therefore it meets exclusion criteria E4 (non-fMRI) and E1 (review/discussion).

This is a survey study of pet owner choices and marketing in veterinary practices; it is not an fMRI study nor does it involve task-based neuroimaging or report brain activation. Excluded for being a non-fMRI study.

This is a chemistry/materials study of synthesized compounds and photoinduced electron transfer; it involves no human participants, no task-based fMRI, and no problem-solving or reasoning tasks. It does not meet the review inclusion criteria.

This paper discusses FDG-PET/PET-CT in gastrointestinal stromal tumors (oncology), not task-based fMRI, reports no healthy adult participants, and appears to be a review/overview rather than original fMRI research. Therefore E1, E3, and E4 apply.

This is a materials/colloid science study of wetting on polymer surfaces with no human participants and no fMRI methods. It does not meet the review focus on task-based fMRI in healthy adults. Excluded for being a non-fMRI study and having no healthy adult sample.
Animal optogenetic-fMRI study examining hippocampal networks and seizure-like activity; not a human task-based fMRI investigation of problem solving. No healthy adult participants are included.

This is a methods/connectivity-focused paper proposing a permutation framework for comparing brain networks and applies it to functional networks. It does not describe a task-based problem-solving or reasoning fMRI study, nor report task-related activation in healthy adults. The focus is on network-level connectivity methods rather than task activation.

This is a methods paper on EEG/fMRI artefact removal; it does not report a task-based problem-solving or reasoning fMRI task or task-related brain activation in healthy adults. It focuses on signal-processing methodology, not cognitive task activations.

Molecular biology study in yeast (Saccharomyces cerevisiae); no human participants, no fMRI imaging, and not a problem-solving task. Does not meet inclusion criteria for task-based fMRI in healthy adults.

This is a theoretical photophysics/chemistry study on rhodamine dyes with no human participants and no fMRI methods. It does not meet the review’s task-based fMRI or healthy adult sample requirements.
This is a review article on PET/SPECT imaging of paragangliomas, not a task-based fMRI study. It does not involve healthy adults performing problem-solving tasks and is not primary fMRI research.

The study is a clinical dermatology investigation of patch testing and a literature review, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It does not meet the review objective (non-fMRI).

This is an arterial spin labeling and PET study of cerebral blood flow and arterial transit time in normal subjects; it is not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving and does not report task-related fMRI activations. Excluded as a non-fMRI study.

This is a transcranial Doppler physiological study assessing cerebrovascular responses during rebreathing; no task-based fMRI, no problem-solving/reasoning task, and no task-related brain activation are reported. It is a non-fMRI study.

This is a resting-state fMRI study in concussed individuals (subacute mTBI), not a task-based problem-solving/reasoning fMRI study; participants are not healthy adult controls. Thus it meets exclusion criteria E2 and E3.

This is a single-case FDG PET report in a lactating patient with a pulmonary hydatid cyst, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It is a non-fMRI clinical case and does not include healthy adult sample performing problem-solving tasks.

Single-case study of a patient with logopenic primary progressive aphasia (not healthy adults). Task is a language/picture-naming intervention, not a problem-solving or reasoning task. Does not meet the review’s requirement for healthy adult participants performing problem-solving tasks.

This is a resting-state fMRI study examining effects of cranial electrotherapy stimulation on resting brain activity and connectivity in healthy adults; it does not involve any task-based problem-solving or reasoning tasks. Therefore it meets exclusion criterion E2.

The study uses task-based fMRI of saccade and vergence eye movements, not a problem-solving or reasoning task; it does not meet inclusion criterion I2. Therefore it is not relevant to the review objective.

The abstract describes a methodological/theoretical framework and discussion of combining neuroimaging with linguistic analysis rather than reporting an original task-based fMRI experiment. It provides no information about participants, problem-solving tasks, or task-related activation results. This meets the exclusion for reviews/methods papers.

Abstract only mentions cocaine use disorder and impaired insight; it does not indicate a problem-solving/reasoning task, confirm task-based fMRI, or report inclusion of healthy adult participants. Insufficient information to determine eligibility, include for full-text review.

Animal (rabbit) study using CT and PET imaging of tumor response; not an fMRI study and does not include healthy adult human participants performing problem-solving tasks. Does not meet inclusion criteria (non-fMRI, non-human).

This is a review article focused on resting-state fMRI in animal and non-human primate models, not a task-based fMRI study of problem-solving in healthy adult humans. It meets exclusion criteria E1 (review) and E2 (resting-state).

This is not a task-based fMRI study. It focuses on connectivity methods and is applied to resting-state MEG data in an ASD sample; no healthy adult group or task-related activation is reported. Thus it is a resting-state/connectivity study, non-fMRI in practice, and lacks a healthy adult sample.

The abstract centers on resting-state deactivation and effective connectivity between pgACC and sgACC and its relation to glutamate; it is a connectivity-focused study rather than a task-based problem-solving/reasoning investigation. This meets exclusion criterion E2 (resting-state or connectivity-only studies).

This is a genomics/epigenomics study using ChIA-PET and ChIP-seq to predict DNA looping (ERα interactions). It is not a task-based fMRI study, does not involve human participants performing problem-solving, and is a non-fMRI study. Exclude based on E4.
This is a chemistry study of nickel-mediated C–F/C–H bond activation with no human participants or fMRI methods reported. It does not involve task-based brain imaging or healthy adult subjects.

Abstract focuses on reward-circuit function in high-BMI individuals with compulsive overeating and frames eating as addiction; it does not specify a task-based fMRI problem-solving paradigm nor report participant details. Insufficient methodological detail to determine eligibility, so include for full-text review.

The study uses task-based fMRI of sweet taste hedonic evaluation, not a problem-solving or reasoning task required by the review. Although it includes healthy young adults and reports task-related activation, it does not address problem solving.
The study uses task-based fMRI of perceptual 3D shape adaptation (depth perception), not a problem-solving or reasoning task; therefore it does not meet inclusion criterion I2.
exclusion_criteria_applied: Does not meet I2 (not a problem-solving/reasoning task)

abstract_screening_results.reason: The study uses visual stimulation to characterize BOLD signal dynamics, not a problem-solving or reasoning task in healthy adults. It does not meet inclusion criterion I2 (problem-solving task).

The tasks described (heartbeat counting and tone counting) assess interoceptive/exteroceptive awareness, not problem solving or reasoning; thus the study fails inclusion criterion I2. It is a task-based fMRI study in healthy participants and reports activation, but the task does not match the review objective.
This is an animal (rat) fMRI study of electroacupuncture and amphetamine, not a human task-based fMRI study of problem solving. No healthy adult participants or problem-solving task are included.

This is a clinical review of imaging in acute stroke rather than an experimental task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It does not report task-related fMRI results or include healthy adult participants performing problem-solving tasks.

Abstract describes a task-based fMRI study and reports task-related activation, but the task is memory/source monitoring (not clearly a problem-solving/reasoning task) and participant ages/health status are not specified. Include for full-text review to confirm whether participants are healthy adults (I3) and whether the task meets the problem-solving/reasoning requirement (I2).

Abstract reads as a narrative review of smooth pursuit neurophysiology/neuroanatomy, discussing models and future fMRI work rather than reporting an original task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. Exclude as a review and not relevant to problem solving.

Abstract explicitly identifies the paper as a review of functional and structural MRI findings in pain. It is not an original task-based fMRI study of problem-solving in healthy adults. Exclude based on E1 (review).

Abstract describes a brief review article summarizing findings about prefrontal cortex rather than reporting original task-based fMRI experiments in healthy adults. It is therefore a review and not a primary fMRI study relevant to problem-solving tasks.

This article addresses practical/technical aspects of 18F-FDG PET supply and logistics, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It uses PET, not fMRI, so it does not meet the review's modality requirement.

This is a review of radiolabelled steroid derivatives for PET imaging in breast cancer, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It is a review (E1), involves non-fMRI imaging methods (E4), and does not include a healthy adult sample (E3).

This is a chemistry/materials study of a novel photochromic molecule; it reports no human participants, no task-based fMRI or cognitive/problem-solving tasks. Therefore it is a non-fMRI study and has no healthy adult sample.

This article is an integrative review of fMRI findings on personality, not an original task-based fMRI study of problem-solving in healthy adults. It meets the exclusion criterion for reviews/meta-analyses and does not address problem-solving tasks.
The study uses fMRI to investigate brain responses to itch (cowhage vs histamine), not a problem-solving or reasoning task; therefore it does not meet inclusion criterion I2.
This study is a PET imaging study in mice, not a task-based fMRI study, and does not include healthy adult human participants. It therefore fails the review's fMRI and human adult criteria.

Event-related task-based fMRI study reporting task-related activations (caudate, medial temporal lobe). Abstract does not clearly state participants are healthy adults in the 18–65 range nor that the task qualifies as problem-solving/reasoning, so include for full-text review.

This is an atmospheric modeling study of thermal bioclimate using WRF/RayMan, not a task-based fMRI experiment. It reports no human participants performing problem-solving tasks and contains no fMRI or brain activation results.

The study centers on dopamine effects on frontal–striatal functional connectivity during a working memory task, reporting connectivity changes rather than task-evoked activation and not addressing a problem-solving/reasoning task. This meets the exclusion for connectivity-only studies.

Task-based fMRI in healthy adults with reported task-related activations. The paradigm involves dual-tasking and short-term memory (same-different tasks) but the abstract does not clearly describe a problem-solving or reasoning task (I2 uncertain). No exclusion criteria met; include for full-text review to confirm relevance.

This is a resting-state functional connectivity study comparing aMCI patients and controls; it is not a task-based fMRI study of problem-solving in healthy adults. Therefore it meets exclusion criterion E2.

Abstract describes a methodological comparison of respiratory-calibrated MRI and fNIRS in healthy adults with no mention of a task-based problem-solving or reasoning paradigm or task-related activation results. It is not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving.
The study used resting-state fMRI and connectivity analyses rather than a task-based problem-solving or reasoning paradigm, so it meets exclusion criterion E2.

This is an animal (rat) fMRI study of pharmacological stress (yohimbine), not a task-based problem-solving or reasoning study in healthy human adults. Therefore it does not include a healthy adult human sample and is not relevant to the review objective.

This is a multisite ML classification using structural and functional (connectivity) MRI from pediatric ADHD vs typically developing children; it is not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving and does not include healthy adults (18-65). Exclusion criteria E2 and E3 apply.

Abstract describes a task-based fMRI symptom-provocation paradigm and reports BOLD variability/activation results, but does not indicate a problem-solving task (I2) nor clearly report healthy adult controls (I3). Insufficient information in the abstract to determine match to the review’s problem-solving and healthy-adult criteria, so include for full-text review.

The study uses a food-choice fMRI task, not a problem-solving or reasoning task; it therefore does not meet inclusion criterion I2. It does use task-based fMRI in healthy adults and reports task-related activation, but the task is outside the review objective (problem solving).

This is an EEG–fMRI resting-state study with participants at rest (eyes closed) and no task or problem-solving/reasoning paradigm is administered, so it does not meet the task-based problem-solving objective.

The study examines BOLD time courses of magnocellular and parvocellular visual pathways in primary visual cortex—an fMRI sensory/physiology study, not a problem-solving or reasoning task in healthy adults. It does not address the review objective (problem-solving tasks).

This study reports AV-45 PET imaging of amyloid binding in Alzheimer’s patients and healthy subjects. It is not a task-based fMRI study and does not involve problem-solving or reasoning tasks, so it does not meet the fMRI/task requirements.

The study reports PET/SPECT receptor and transporter imaging, not task-based fMRI, and does not examine problem-solving tasks in healthy adults. Therefore it does not meet the fMRI task-based inclusion criteria.

Study uses ICA functional connectivity analyses in incarcerated individuals with psychopathy (connectivity-only focus) and does not include a healthy adult sample performing a problem-solving task. Therefore it does not meet task-based/problem-solving/healthy-sample criteria.

This is a phase I clinical trial in pancreatic cancer testing a CD40 antibody with chemotherapy; it is not an fMRI study, does not involve problem-solving tasks, and includes no healthy adult participants.

The study is a retrospective PET imaging investigation (18F-FDG uptake) in patients with non-CNS malignancy, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving. Participants are clinical patients rather than healthy adults. Therefore it does not meet I1/I2/I3 and is clearly a non-fMRI study and has no healthy adult sample.
This is a PET/MRI physiological study of glucose uptake in elite runners, not a task-based fMRI study of problem-solving. It does not involve fMRI or problem-solving tasks.

This paper discusses the physiology of muscle functional MRI during exercise and does not investigate task-based brain fMRI of problem-solving in healthy adults; no problem-solving task or brain activation results are reported.
Animal (ferret) PET/CT study of influenza; not a human task-based fMRI study and does not include healthy adult participants. Therefore does not meet the review scope.

This article is a review discussing fear conditioning and therapeutic targets rather than a primary task-based fMRI study of problem-solving in healthy adults. Abstract identifies it as a review; it does not report original fMRI task activation results. Exclusion criterion E1 applies.

The study investigates sensory and cognitive neural plasticity in deaf and sign-language users, not problem-solving or reasoning tasks in healthy adults. The abstract does not report task-based problem-solving paradigms or clearly include a healthy adult control group (18-65). Therefore it does not meet the review objective.

This is a chemistry/photochemistry experimental study on SmI2 in THF with no human participants, no fMRI methods, and not a problem-solving task. Excluded because it is a non-fMRI study and contains no healthy adult sample.

Abstract describes neural responses to hallucinations in schizophrenia patients and does not mention a problem-solving or reasoning task, nor any healthy adult sample. Thus it fails the requirement for healthy adult participants and problem-solving tasks.

Abstract describes a review/overview of BOLD fMRI development, applications, and perspectives rather than an original task-based fMRI study with participants performing problem-solving tasks. No participant sample or task-related activation results are reported.
The study uses task-based fMRI for recognition memory, not a problem-solving or reasoning task. It therefore does not meet inclusion criterion I2 (problem-solving/reasoning).

The study uses task-based fMRI of an attentional blink / working memory task, not a problem-solving or reasoning task, so it fails inclusion criterion I2. I1 and I4 appear satisfied; I3 (healthy adult sample) is not specified in the abstract. No listed exclusion criteria (E1–E4) clearly apply.

Preclinical study in rats using PET/CT and pharmacological manipulation (MK-801); not a human task-based fMRI study and contains no healthy adult participants. Therefore it does not meet inclusion criteria.
This article is a review summarizing fTCD and fMRI techniques and cerebrovascular autoregulation, not an original task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. Exclude as a review.

This is an animal (Ela1-myc mice) FDG PET imaging study of pancreatic tumours, not a task-based fMRI study in human healthy adults and does not involve problem-solving tasks or report fMRI activations.

This is a PET/MRI study of glioblastoma patients (clinical sample), not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It does not include healthy adult participants and is not an fMRI task study.

This is a review article about PET imaging and radioligand development in neurodegenerative disease, not a task-based fMRI study of problem-solving in healthy adults. It is non-fMRI and does not involve healthy adult participants performing problem-solving tasks.

This is a PET imaging study in a nonhuman primate during deep brain stimulation, not a task-based fMRI study in healthy adult humans performing a problem-solving task. It therefore fails inclusion and meets exclusion criteria E4 (non-fMRI study) and E3 (no healthy adult sample).

This study used FDG-PET metabolic imaging in cognitively normal adults and did not use task-based fMRI or report task-related activation during a problem-solving or reasoning task. It therefore does not meet the review's fMRI task-based inclusion criteria.
This is a PET imaging methods paper using Monte Carlo simulation and scatter modelling; not a task-based fMRI study, includes no human healthy adult participants or problem-solving tasks.
The article is a narrative review of imaging in COPD, not a primary task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It meets exclusion criteria E1 (review) and E4 (non-fMRI study).

This is a narrative review discussing hormones, hypothalamus, and obesity, not an original task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It does not report task-related fMRI results. Meets exclusion: review (E1).

This is a review of molecular PET imaging in Alzheimer’s disease populations, not a task-based fMRI study of problem-solving in healthy adults. It is a review/meta-article and focuses on PET (non-fMRI) methods, so it fails inclusion requirements.

This is a computational modeling study of dopamine signaling with no task-based fMRI, no human participants, and no problem-solving task. It therefore does not meet inclusion criteria I1/I2/I3. Excluded as a non-fMRI study and for lacking a healthy adult sample.

This abstract is a review of neurostimulation mechanisms in refractory epilepsy involving patients and animals, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It therefore meets exclusion criteria E1 (review) and E3 (no healthy adult sample).

The study investigates episodic recollection (memory retrieval) and task-related functional connectivity, not a problem-solving or reasoning task. It is also connectivity-focused rather than addressing problem-solving, so it does not meet the review objective.

Participants are children (dyslexic and control), not healthy adults, and the tasks involve reading/phonological/magnocellular processing rather than problem-solving or reasoning. Does not meet the requirement for a healthy adult problem-solving fMRI sample.

Animal (rat) resting-state fMRI study examining low-frequency BOLD fluctuations; not a task-based problem-solving study, and does not include human adult participants. Excluded for being resting-state/connectivity-focused and for having no healthy adult sample.

This article is a narrative review of neuroimaging methods in HIV-associated neurocognitive disorders, not an original task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It does not report primary task-related activation results in a healthy adult sample and is therefore excluded as a review.

The abstract studies stigma resistance and emotion regulation in psychiatric patients using fMRI; it does not report a problem-solving or reasoning task and does not indicate inclusion of a healthy adult (18–65) sample. Exclude for lack of healthy adult participants.

This is a review article focused on EEG (electroencephalography), not a primary task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It is therefore both a review (E1) and a non-fMRI study (E4).

This is a task-based fMRI study of social face categorization (race/emotion) examining stereotype conflict, not a problem-solving or reasoning task. It does not meet inclusion criterion I2 (problem-solving/reasoning).

The study is a task-based fMRI and reports task-related activations, but the task is motor imagery/planning of object-directed actions rather than an explicit problem-solving or reasoning task. It is unclear from the abstract whether this qualifies as ‘problem solving’ per the review definition, so include for full-text review.

The study involves treatment-seeking adolescent smokers (not healthy adults aged 18–65) and uses a Stroop cognitive-control task rather than a problem-solving/reasoning task. Thus it lacks a healthy adult sample and does not address problem-solving in adults.

Abstract describes a 7T fMRI study reporting laminar activation profiles (task-related activation) in human V1 but does not specify a problem-solving or reasoning task or participant age range. Insufficient information to confirm I2/I3, so include for full-text review.

The study uses visual stimulation (flickering checkerboards) to probe sustained/transient visual cortex responses, not a problem-solving or reasoning task. Therefore it does not meet I2. It does meet I1, I3, and I4. None of the explicit exclusion criteria (E1–E4) apply.
Study uses FDG-PET and machine learning for epilepsy diagnosis, not task-based fMRI and does not involve problem-solving or reasoning tasks in healthy adults. Therefore it fails the fMRI requirement.

This is a review article about FDG PET quantification in oncology (a methodological review), not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It uses PET in cancer contexts and includes no healthy adult fMRI data.

Task-based fMRI study where participants made judgments about the causal efficacy of their actions (a form of reasoning), and the abstract reports task-related neural activation. Participant health/ages not specified in abstract; include for full-text review to confirm healthy adult sample.

This is a methods/simulation study producing synthetic neuronal and fMRI/EEG data to benchmark directed connectivity metrics. It does not involve human participants performing a problem-solving task nor report task-related brain activations. Therefore it meets exclusion criteria for connectivity/simulation-only studies and for having no healthy adult sample.

Abstract reports task-based fMRI in adult L2 learners with task-related activation (Broca's area). However, it is unclear whether the behavioral task qualifies as a problem-solving or reasoning task per review definition. Full-text needed to confirm I2.

This is a clinical review of frontotemporal lobar degeneration and ALS, not a task-based fMRI study. It does not report task-related fMRI results or include healthy adult problem-solving tasks. It is a review/non-fMRI clinical study focusing on patient populations.

This is a PET/MRI tumor imaging study using radiolabeled nanoparticles assessing integrin expression — not a task-based fMRI study, does not involve problem-solving tasks or healthy adult participants. Excluded as non-fMRI and no healthy adult sample.

The study samples adolescents (early adolescence), not healthy adults 18–65 (E3). It primarily examines development of functional connectivity during an action-observation task (connectivity-focused), not task-based problem solving (E2). Therefore it does not meet the review’s population or task focus.

This paper is an ALE meta-analysis of lexicality effects in reading (lexical decision and naming), not a primary task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It is a meta-analysis, so it meets exclusion criterion E1.

The study uses resting-state fMRI and machine-learning classification of schizophrenia patients (auditory hallucinations) versus controls; it does not describe a task-based problem-solving or reasoning paradigm or report task-related activations. Excluded per E2 (resting-state or connectivity-only studies).

Abstract describes small-animal molecular imaging methods (preclinical imaging), not task-based fMRI in humans performing problem-solving. Therefore it is not an fMRI study of healthy adults and does not address problem-solving tasks.

The study investigates dopaminergic changes in Parkinson's disease patients with impulse control disorders; no healthy adult sample is reported. This population does not meet the review’s requirement for healthy adults performing problem-solving tasks.

This paper is a narrative review discussing cellular mechanisms and neuroprotection, not an original task-based fMRI study in healthy adults. It does not report task-related fMRI results or include human problem-solving tasks. Exclusion applies (review article and non-fMRI content).

This is a review/integration paper discussing cellular and systems-level studies of the hippocampus rather than reporting an original task-based fMRI experiment of problem-solving in healthy adults. It meets exclusion criterion E1 (review). The abstract does not describe a problem-solving task or primary fMRI activation results in a healthy adult sample.

This is an FDG-PET study of breast carcinoma patients, not a task-based fMRI study and it does not include healthy adult participants performing problem-solving; therefore it does not meet the review criteria.
This is a bacterial cryopreservation/biotech methods study (E. coli), not a human task-based fMRI study of problem solving. It involves no healthy adult participants and is not an fMRI study.

This is a narrative review of MRI/MRS techniques rather than an original task-based fMRI study reporting brain activation during problem-solving in healthy adults. It therefore meets exclusion criterion E1 (review).

This is a narrative review of neuroimaging findings in ALS patients, not an original task-based fMRI study of problem-solving in healthy adults. It does not report task-related brain activations in healthy adult participants. Exclusion criteria E1 and E3 apply.

This is a chemistry/molecular-biology methods paper describing development of fluorescent RNA sensors and in vitro selection. It involves no human participants, no task-based fMRI, and reports no brain activation results; therefore it does not meet the review inclusion criteria.

This is a review of imaging methods for schistosomes (ultrasound, PET, fluorescence, microscopy) in animals and diseased humans; it is not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It is a review and involves non-fMRI modalities and non-healthy participants.

This is a preclinical PET radiotracer evaluation in rats and squirrel monkeys with no task-based fMRI or healthy adult human participants performing problem-solving tasks. It is a non-fMRI animal study, so it does not meet the review criteria.

This is an animal (mouse) study focusing on circadian/serotonin responses, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adult humans. It lacks human adult participants and is non-fMRI, so it fails inclusion criteria I1–I3 and I4.

This is an FDG PET/CT study of chemotherapy-related brain changes in cancer patients (chemo-brain), not a task-based fMRI study in healthy adults. It is a non-fMRI modality and does not include healthy adult participants, so it meets exclusion criteria E4 and E3.

This is a review article about neuroimaging in epilepsy, not an original task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It does not report task-related fMRI results in healthy adult problem-solving participants.

Study is a PET imaging study of PDE4 using (R)-[11C]rolipram, not an fMRI investigation or a task-based problem-solving/reasoning study in healthy adults. Therefore it fails the fMRI requirement (E4).

This study examines resting-state functional connectivity in patients with progressive supranuclear palsy, not a task-based fMRI problem-solving study in healthy adults. It uses resting-state connectivity methods and does not report a healthy adult sample.

This study used FDG-PET (not fMRI) and investigated patients with progressive supranuclear palsy rather than healthy adults performing problem-solving tasks. Therefore it does not meet the fMRI or healthy adult sample criteria.
Participants are children and the study predicts working memory development during childhood—no healthy adult (18–65) sample is included. This clearly fails the adult-sample inclusion criterion.

This is a resting-state EEG-fMRI study in patients with idiopathic generalized epilepsy; it is not task-based, does not involve problem-solving or reasoning tasks, and does not include healthy adult participants.

This is a resting perfusion fMRI study examining baclofen modulation of resting cerebral blood flow, not a task-based study of problem-solving; it does not involve a problem-solving/reasoning task (meets E2).
Animal PET study in monkeys (non-human subjects) and uses PET rather than fMRI; does not include healthy adult human fMRI data.

The study uses task-based fMRI and includes healthy controls but the task is episodic memory encoding/retrieval, not a problem-solving or reasoning task required by the review (fails inclusion criterion I2). Abstract provides sufficient information to exclude.

The study investigates Melodic Intonation Therapy in severe nonfluent aphasia (patient population) and focuses on language therapy; it does not involve problem-solving/reasoning tasks nor report data from healthy adult participants. Exclude for no healthy adult sample.

Abstract describes task-based fMRI with task-related activations, but the task is implicit perceptual sequence learning rather than an explicit problem-solving or reasoning task; participant ages/health not specified. Insufficient information to determine whether the task meets the review's definition of 'problem solving' — include for full-text review.
Study uses somatosensory stimulation in comatose patients, not a problem-solving or reasoning task in healthy adults. The task and population do not match the meta-analysis objective.

This is a task-based fMRI study examining mental rotation (a spatial reasoning task) with reported task-related brain activation. Participant health/ages aren’t specified in the abstract, so full text review is needed to confirm age range, but the study otherwise meets inclusion criteria.

This is a resting-state/connectivity study using diffusion MRI and resting-state fMRI to model structural–functional connectivity, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It does not report task-related activations.

This study used fNIRS to assess resting-state functional connectivity in adults (connectivity/resting-state and non-fMRI modality). It is not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving, so it fails the review’s modality and task requirements.

Abstract studies intrinsic/resting fluctuations and trial-to-trial motor force variability, not a problem-solving or reasoning task. Although task-based fMRI results are reported, the study does not address problem solving in healthy adults and therefore does not meet the review objective.

Although this is a task-based fMRI study with healthy adult participants and reports task-related activations (I1, I3, I4), the task involves anticipating social feedback/mentalizing, not problem solving or reasoning. It does not meet the review’s required task domain (I2).

The study is about MEG methods and practical aspects of developmental MEG studies, not a task-based fMRI investigation of problem solving in healthy adults. It appears to be a methods/discussion paper rather than original fMRI empirical research. Exclude for non-fMRI modality and as a review/commentary.

This study uses resting-state fMRI and high-dimensional ICA to examine functional connectivity in Alzheimer’s disease and healthy controls; no task-based problem-solving or reasoning task is reported. Excluded per E2 (resting-state/connectivity-only study).

This is a task-based fMRI study of emotional appraisal (fearful vs neutral faces) with carotid stimulation, not a problem-solving or reasoning task. It meets I1, I3, and I4 but fails I2 (no problem-solving/reasoning task).

The article is a review of MRI/fMRI studies of female sexual function rather than a primary task-based fMRI investigation of problem-solving in healthy adults. It meets the exclusion criterion for reviews/meta-analyses and does not address problem-solving tasks in the target population.

The study is a connectivity-focused fMRI study assessing functional network connectivity at rest and across sensory paradigms (not problem-solving). It does not report task-related activation for a problem-solving/reasoning task, meeting exclusion criterion E2.

Abstract reports task-based fMRI in normal-weight human participants and task-related neural responses; however, the task involves food-cue/responsivity rather than problem-solving. Insufficient information to confirm the task fits the review's problem-solving requirement, so include for full-text review.

Abstract reports an fMRI study in healthy samples examining reward-related processes (goal-sensitivity and outcome-expectancy) but does not specify a problem-solving or reasoning task. Unclear whether I2 (problem-solving task) is met — include for full-text review to confirm.

This is a task-based fMRI study of a motor (button-press) task that primarily compares connectivity measures (Granger causality and coherency). It does not investigate problem-solving or reasoning tasks and is a connectivity-focused study, thus excluded per E2.

The task is audiovisual emotional classification/integration, not a problem-solving or reasoning task (does not meet inclusion criterion I2). The study is task-based fMRI and reports task-related activations (I1, I4 satisfied) but it does not address the review objective of problem solving in healthy adults.

This study reports gene-expression profiling of pancreatic endocrine tumors (oncology/genomics), not a task-based fMRI experiment with healthy adults performing problem-solving tasks. It is a non-fMRI patient tumor study, so it does not meet the review objective.

Narrative review of molecular imaging (scintigraphy, PET) for Parkinsonism; not an original task-based fMRI study, does not involve problem-solving tasks or healthy adult fMRI data. Excluded as a review article and a non-fMRI study.

Study reports CNS imaging findings in patients with Erdheim-Chester disease; it is not a task-based fMRI study and does not include a healthy adult sample. Appears to be clinical/structural imaging rather than fMRI task activation.

This is a resting-state fMRI study of temporomandibular disorder patients (with healthy controls) assessing spontaneous neural activity and splint treatment effects. It is not task-based nor involves a problem-solving/reasoning task, so it does not meet the review objective. Exclusion criterion E2 (resting-state or connectivity-only studies) applies.

This is a veterinary review/synopsis about skin cancer treatments in dogs and cats, not a task-based fMRI study in humans. It is non-fMRI, involves no healthy adult human participants, and is a review article rather than original fMRI research.

This is a clinical review/opinion piece on pediatric topics (neonatal jaundice, animal injuries, immunizations); it is not a task-based fMRI study, does not involve healthy adult problem-solving tasks, and is non-fMRI. Clear exclusion as a review/non-fMRI with no healthy adult sample.

This is a narrative/practical review of intracranial cavernous angioma (neurosurgical/molecular topics), not an original task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It is a review article, meeting exclusion criterion E1.

This is a task-based fMRI study of motor/proprioceptive manipulation (FES) and connectivity (DCM) in healthy adults, not a problem-solving or reasoning task. Although it meets I1, I3, and I4, it does not address I2 (problem solving), so it falls outside the review objective.

This is a review article synthesizing behavioral, imaging, and modeling evidence on visual word recognition rather than a primary task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults; therefore it meets exclusion criterion E1 (reviews/meta-analyses).

The abstract describes a task-based fMRI paradigm and reports task-related brain activations, but it does not clearly state that participants performed a problem-solving/reasoning task or confirm a healthy adult sample. Insufficient information to determine if I2 and I3 are met, so include for full-text review.

The abstract explicitly describes a review summarizing recent fMRI studies rather than reporting original task-based experiment results. It also focuses on spatial working memory (delayed-saccades), not problem-solving/reasoning tasks. Therefore it meets the exclusion criterion for reviews/meta-analyses.

This study used resting-state fMRI in children with fetal alcohol spectrum disorders and matched controls — not a task-based problem-solving study, and participants are not healthy adults (18–65). Therefore it meets exclusion criteria for resting-state/connectivity studies and for lacking a healthy adult sample.

This is a resting-state fMRI study examining noise properties/Hurst exponent in Alzheimer’s patients and controls, not a task-based problem-solving study in healthy adults. It does not report task-related activation.

The study uses task-based fMRI with healthy adults and reports task-related activation, but the task involves viewing appetizing food images (reward sensitivity), not a problem-solving or reasoning task; thus it fails inclusion criterion I2.

This study describes development of a fluorescent sensor for detecting bacterial DNA using quantum dots—it's a materials/chemistry/biotechnology paper with no fMRI methods, no human participants, no problem-solving task, and no brain activation results.

The study used Doppler ultrasound to measure cerebral blood flow and vasodilation, not task-based fMRI or a problem-solving paradigm. It does include healthy adults but is a non-fMRI physiological study, so it meets exclusion E4 (Non-fMRI studies).
This is a PET radiotracer study focused on tumor imaging (neuroblastoma/melanoma), not a task-based fMRI study of problem-solving; it does not involve healthy adults performing reasoning tasks.
This is a PET/MRI case study of two patients with neurodegenerative aphasia, not a task-based fMRI study; no healthy adult sample is included. Does not meet I1/I2/I3/I4.

This is a review article summarizing neuroimaging findings in Parkinson’s disease (PD) and PD-MCI rather than an original task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It focuses on PD patients, not healthy adult participants.

This is a case report of FDG PET/CT in a cervical cancer patient describing altered tracer biodistribution after insulin/Vitamin C; it is not a task-based fMRI study, does not involve problem-solving tasks, and does not include healthy adult participants. Exclusion criteria E4 and E3 apply.

The task is a selective attention task, not a problem-solving or reasoning task, so it does not meet inclusion criterion I2. The study is task-based fMRI with healthy adult controls and reports activations (I1, I3, I4 met). No exclusion criteria E1–E4 apply.

Task-based fMRI in human adults reporting task-related activation. However, the task is speech repetition/vocal imitation, not a problem-solving/reasoning task; relevance to problem-solving is unclear. Recommend full-text review to confirm whether any problem-solving component is present.

The task is a social exclusion (ball-tossing) paradigm examining attachment and neural responses, not a problem-solving or reasoning task (does not meet I2). The study is task-based fMRI and reports task-related activations (I1, I4), but it does not address the review objective.
Study is about adrenal scintigraphy/CT/MR imaging, not task-based fMRI or problem-solving in healthy adults. It appears to be a non-fMRI imaging review; therefore it does not meet inclusion criteria.

This study concerns development and evaluation of PET radioligands (radiosynthesis) for α1-adrenoceptors, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It is a non-fMRI imaging methods paper, so does not meet inclusion criteria.

The study reports fMRI of classical conditioning and reward preference learning, not a problem-solving or reasoning task; thus it fails inclusion criterion I2. fMRI methods and task-related activations are reported, but the task does not address problem solving in healthy adults.
This is a narrative review article on MR imaging in schizophrenia and autism, not an original task-based fMRI study of problem-solving in healthy adults; thus meets exclusion E1.

The study uses task-based fMRI of gaze processing in individuals with autism (and comparison participants); it does not involve a problem-solving or reasoning task in healthy adults, so it does not match the review objective.

Abstract describes a methodological BOLD signal modeling framework and does not specify a problem-solving task, participant sample, or healthy adult group. Information is insufficient to determine whether the study includes task-based fMRI of problem solving in healthy adults, so include for full-text review.

Abstract does not specify a problem-solving or reasoning task nor confirm healthy adult sample or task-related activation; insufficient information to rule in or out. Include for full-text review to determine whether it meets the review criteria.

Abstract describes altered functional connectivity and network hubs in social anxiety disorder; no task-based fMRI or problem-solving task is reported. This is a connectivity-focused clinical study, not task-based problem-solving in healthy adults.

The study investigates structural-functional connectivity and uses resting-state networks and modeling; it is not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving. It focuses on connectivity/resting-state rather than task-related activations.

This is a resting-state fMRI connectivity study comparing schizophrenia patients and healthy controls, not a task-based problem-solving or reasoning fMRI study. It therefore meets the resting-state/connectivity-only exclusion.

The study is a task-based fMRI in healthy adult volunteers and reports task-related brain activations (I1, I3, I4). However, the task is a visuospatial line-bisection judgment (perceptual/allocentric), not clearly a problem-solving or reasoning task (I2 unclear). Because this is borderline, include for full-text review to confirm whether the task meets the review's definition of problem solving.
This is a review article on chromosome conformation capture (3C) technologies in genomics, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It is a review and not an fMRI study.

This is a veterinary review article about tick-borne infections in dogs. It is a review (E1), concerns non-human subjects (dogs) not healthy adult humans (E3), and is not an fMRI study (E4). It does not meet any inclusion criteria for task-based fMRI problem-solving studies.
Narrative overview/review of diagnostic imaging history; not an empirical task-based fMRI study. No human participants, no problem-solving task, and no task-related activation results reported.

This is an electron crystallography structural biology study of an algal protein complex, not an fMRI experiment and includes no human participants. It does not meet task-based fMRI or healthy adult criteria.

This study describes development of viral capsids for PET radiolabeling (molecular imaging); it is not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. Therefore it is a non-fMRI study and does not meet the review objective.

This is an inorganic chemistry experimental/DFT/NMR study of platinum complexes with no human participants or neuroimaging methods. It does not involve task-based fMRI or problem-solving in healthy adults.

This is a review article about genetically encoded molecular imaging reporters in animals/whole-animal studies, not a task-based fMRI study in human adults. It is a non-fMRI, non-human review, so it does not meet inclusion criteria.

Abstract focuses on fMRI in left temporal lobe epilepsy and language-related reorganization; it does not mention problem-solving/reasoning tasks nor clearly state inclusion of healthy adult participants. Insufficient information to determine eligibility for a review of task-based fMRI problem-solving in healthy adults, so include for full-text review.

The abstract explicitly states this is a review summarizing MR imaging techniques and findings (Part 1), not an original task-based fMRI experiment. It therefore meets the review/meta-analysis exclusion criterion.

This fMRI study examines lexical-gustatory synaesthesia (responses to words), not problem-solving or reasoning tasks, so it does not meet inclusion criterion I2. It is task-based and reports task-related activations (I1, I4), but the experimental task is not relevant to the review objective.

The study used task-based fMRI in healthy adult participants and reports task-related activation, but the task is value-based food decision/valuation rather than a problem-solving or reasoning task, so it does not meet inclusion criterion I2.

This is a review article (not an original task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults). It does not report primary task-based fMRI results in healthy adults, so it meets exclusion criterion E1.

The fMRI study examines face perception and stereotype priming, not a problem-solving or reasoning task (I2 not met). Although task-based fMRI and task-related activations are reported, the experimental task is perception under different music contexts rather than problem solving. Exclude for not addressing the review objective.

Abstract describes a task-based fMRI study with reported task-related activation (fMR-A adaptation). However, it does not specify that participants performed a problem-solving/reasoning task nor participant ages/health status. Insufficient information to confirm I2 and I3, so include for full-text review.

This is a task-based fMRI study of social reciprocity (Trust Game), not a problem-solving or reasoning task. It therefore fails inclusion criterion I2 (problem-solving task) and does not address the review objective.

This is a review of MRI findings in glaucoma and not an empirical task-based fMRI study of problem-solving in healthy adults. It summarizes clinical imaging techniques and CNS changes rather than reporting task-related activation in healthy adult participants.

This is a review article focused on myocardial ischemia in hypertrophic cardiomyopathy using PET and CMR in patient populations. It is not a task-based fMRI study, and does not include healthy adult participants. Therefore it meets multiple exclusion criteria.

This is a review/discussion of functional MR imaging methods rather than an original task-based fMRI experiment in humans performing problem-solving/reasoning tasks; no healthy adult task data or activation results are reported in the abstract.

Study is a task-based fMRI investigation of pain-processing in episodic migraine (case-control). The task involves pain stimulation, not problem-solving/reasoning, and the population is clinical (migraineurs) rather than healthy adults performing problem-solving tasks. Therefore it does not address the review objective.

This is a resting-state functional connectivity study (CRPS patients vs controls) and does not involve a task-based problem-solving or reasoning paradigm in healthy adults, meeting exclusion criterion E2.

This is an oncological PET study of Hodgkin lymphoma patients (clinical prognostic PET/TMTV), not a task-based fMRI study. It does not include healthy adult participants or task-related brain activation data. Excluded based on non-fMRI modality and absence of healthy adult sample.

This is a plant molecular biology study on citrus leaf abscission using laser microdissection and transcriptional analysis; it involves no human participants or fMRI methods. Therefore it does not address task-based fMRI in healthy adults.

The task is a sham neurofeedback/cognitive-control paradigm (attempting to modulate a moving bar), not a problem-solving or reasoning task required by the review. The study is task-based fMRI with healthy adults and reports activations (meets I1, I3, I4) but does not satisfy I2.

This is a pediatric review on metabolic and genetic causes of hypoglycaemia, not a task-based fMRI study. It involves children (not healthy adults), appears to be a review article, and does not report fMRI task activation results.

This is a review/discussion of restless legs syndrome pathophysiology (not a primary task-based fMRI study). It does not investigate problem-solving in healthy adults and reports non-fMRI methods (PET/SPECT, MRI, CSF).

The abstract concerns animal hoarding prevalence in an OCD clinic sample and does not mention fMRI, task-based problem-solving, or healthy adult participants. It therefore does not meet inclusion criteria I1–I3 and clearly meets exclusion criteria E3 (no healthy adult sample) and E4 (non-fMRI study).

Abstract describes a resting-state fMRI study of frequency-specific topological patterns; no task, problem-solving or reasoning paradigm is reported. Excluded per E2 (resting-state/connectivity-only study).
This is an auditory fMRI methods study measuring responses to tones and complex sounds, not a problem-solving or reasoning task in healthy adults; does not meet the review's required task criterion.

Abstract reports leptin therapy effects in a congenital leptin-deficient patient (a clinical case), not a task-based problem-solving fMRI study with healthy adult participants. No healthy adult sample is present.

This is a resting-state functional connectivity methods paper (connectivity-distance profiles, multisite resting-state data). It does not report task-based problem-solving or task-related activations in healthy adults.

Task-based fMRI study in healthy adults reporting task-related brain activation. The task is spatial reorientation (a cognitive/navigation task); whether it meets the review's ‘problem-solving or reasoning’ definition is ambiguous from the abstract, so include for full-text review to confirm relevance.

This is a murine (mouse) histological imaging methods paper, not a human task-based fMRI study. It does not involve healthy adult human participants nor report task-related fMRI activation. Exclusion criteria E3 (no healthy adult sample) and E4 (non-fMRI study) apply.

Abstract describes a literature overview/review of emotion processing deficits across clinical groups, not an original task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It is a review article, so excluded per E1.

The fMRI task involves moral/violent imagery (justified vs unjustified killing), not a problem-solving or reasoning task required by the review (fails I2). The study is task-based fMRI in (presumably) healthy adults and reports activations, but it does not address problem solving.

The fMRI task involved processing threatening facial expressions (emotional reactivity), not a problem-solving or reasoning task, so it fails inclusion criterion I2. It is task-based and reports activation (I1, I4) but does not investigate problem solving in healthy adults.
Study is work on macaque monkeys using electrophysiology/anatomy/behavior, not task-based fMRI in human adults. Therefore it has no healthy adult human fMRI sample and is not an fMRI study.

The abstract describes a resting-state fMRI connectivity and computational modeling study, not a task-based fMRI investigation of problem solving or reasoning. It does not involve participants performing problem-solving tasks, so it meets exclusion criterion E2 (resting-state/connectivity-only).

This is a task-based fMRI study of memory retrieval/learning (testing effect) using word-pair study and retrieval practice; it does not involve problem-solving or reasoning tasks required by the review (fails I2). Although it reports task-related activation (I4) and uses fMRI (I1), the task domain does not match the meta-analysis objective.
Methodological/review paper on fMR-adaptation rather than an original task-based fMRI study of problem-solving in healthy adults; thus meets exclusion criterion for reviews/meta-analyses.
