The study investigates cetuximab treatment for cholangiocarcinoma (oncology). It is not a task-based fMRI study and does not include healthy adult participants, so it fails the review criteria.

This study reports positron emission tomography (PET) imaging and synthesizes findings on addiction, not task-based fMRI of problem solving in healthy adults. It does not meet the fMRI requirement and focuses on drug addiction rather than problem-solving tasks.

The study uses task-based fMRI of working memory in mTBI patients and controls, not a problem-solving or reasoning task required by I2. Although it includes healthy adult controls (I3), is task-based (I1) and reports activation results (I4), it does not address problem-solving in healthy adults.
This is a molecular sialotranscriptome study of an insect (Triatoma rubida); it does not involve human participants or any fMRI task-based imaging. Therefore it is not an fMRI study.

This is a microbiological study of Salmonella in nondomestic birds; it is not a task-based fMRI study and includes no human participants. Excluded for non-fMRI methodology and lack of healthy adult human sample.

This is a review/guidance article on MRI response assessment in glioblastoma patients. It is not a task-based fMRI study, includes no healthy adult participants, and is not primary fMRI research (review of MRI techniques).

Abstract describes a clinical review on ovarian cancer management/surveillance, not a task-based fMRI study. It is a review article, involves patient populations rather than healthy adults, and contains no fMRI task-related brain activation data.

This is a clinical case/report about biopsy and diagnosis in a suspected lung cancer patient; it is not a task-based fMRI study, does not include healthy adult participants performing problem-solving tasks, and reports no task-related brain activation. Exclude for being a non-fMRI clinical patient study.

The study is about trainee ophthalmologists' opinions on improving cataract surgical rate; it is not a task-based fMRI study, does not involve problem-solving tasks or report brain activation. Therefore it is a non-fMRI study and does not meet the review objective.

This is a narrative review on imaging for pancreatic cancer (patient population), not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It is a review (E1), involves diseased patients/no healthy adult sample (E3), and does not report task-based fMRI data (E4).

This study reports veterinary toxicology phone-call data about dog and cat exposures, not a task-based fMRI study in human adults. It involves non-human subjects and is not an fMRI study, so it does not meet the review criteria.

This is a methodological VASO MRI study measuring CBV changes during a breath-holding task, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving or reasoning. It does not address the review objective (no problem-solving task).

Abstract describes a veterinarian’s experiences in corporate pet industry and ethics; it is not a task-based fMRI study, contains no human problem-solving tasks or healthy adult fMRI data, and is not an empirical fMRI report.

Clinical case report of frontotemporal dementia; not a task-based fMRI study, does not involve healthy adult participants performing problem-solving tasks, and reports clinical/neuroimaging findings but not task-related fMRI activations.

Abstract describes a review article focused on vascular risk factors and amyloid PET imaging, not a primary task-based fMRI study of problem-solving. It does not report task-related fMRI activations in healthy adults. Excluded based on E1 (review) and E4 (non-fMRI).

The study is a genomics/chromatin 3D structure methods paper using Hi-C/ChIA-PET on leukemia cells; it is not an fMRI task study, includes no healthy adult participants, and does not involve problem-solving tasks or report task-related brain activation.

The abstract describes a review article on anti-inflammatory therapies in atherosclerosis; it is not an original task-based fMRI study, does not involve problem-solving tasks, nor report fMRI activation in healthy adults. Applies exclusion criteria E1 and E4.

The article is explicitly a review synthesizing prior studies rather than reporting original task-based fMRI experiments. It therefore meets exclusion criterion E1 (review/meta-analysis) and does not provide original task-based results for screening.

This is a resting-state fMRI study of inter-hemispheric connectivity in patients with unilateral brachial plexus injury. It is not a task-based problem-solving study and does not describe healthy adult task participants. Excluded for E2 and E3.

This study is a resting-state fMRI connectivity study comparing drivers and nondrivers; it does not involve a task-based problem-solving or reasoning paradigm in healthy adults, so it does not meet the review objective.

This is a resting-state fMRI connectivity study of the default-mode network in cocaine addicts vs controls, not a task-based fMRI study of problem-solving in healthy adults. It focuses on resting-state connectivity, meeting exclusion criterion E2.

The study uses task-based fMRI of emotional picture viewing and autonomic responses, not a problem-solving or reasoning task, so it fails inclusion criterion I2 (problem-solving). It does meet I1 and I4 but the task is not relevant to the review objective.

Study reports ketamine effects in anesthetized monkeys (animal subjects), not human healthy adults performing problem-solving tasks; thus it does not include a healthy adult human sample and is not eligible for this review.

This is a review article proposing a mathematical framework (circuit to construct mapping), not a primary task-based fMRI experiment with healthy adult participants. It is a review, so it meets exclusion criterion E1.

This is a connectivity-focused study combining resting-state, MACM, and structural covariance; it is not a task-based fMRI study of problem-solving/reasoning. Therefore it meets the exclusion criterion for resting-state/connectivity-only studies.

This study uses resting-state fMRI in stroke patients to examine hand-function outcomes; it is not a task-based problem-solving study and does not include healthy adult participants. Meets exclusion criteria E2 and E3.

The task is memory encoding/retrieval of pictures, not a problem-solving or reasoning task required by the review (I2 not met). Although this is a task-based fMRI study with healthy adults and reports activation (I1, I3, I4 satisfied), it does not address the review objective.

This is a PET study measuring dopamine synthesis and resting regional cerebral blood flow (resting-state), not a task-based fMRI study of problem-solving in healthy adults. Therefore it is a non-fMRI and resting-state study.

The study investigates effective connectivity during working memory in individuals at high risk for psychosis (clinical sample) and focuses on connectivity analysis rather than task-based problem-solving in healthy adults. This makes it outside the review objective.

This study is not task-based and does not examine problem-solving: it models resting-state/structural connectivity in adolescent schizophrenia (and controls) using DTI and simulated BOLD. No healthy adult problem-solving fMRI data are reported.

The study focuses on cocaine addiction and neural circuits related to impulsivity and relapse, not on problem-solving tasks in healthy adults; therefore it does not include the required healthy adult problem-solving fMRI sample.
Animal (rat) pharmacological fMRI study; no human participants or healthy adult sample and does not examine problem-solving tasks. Does not meet population criterion for this review.

The abstract reports a task-based fMRI study including healthy adult participants and task-related activation results (I1, I3, I4). However, the task is categorization/visual attention, not clearly a problem-solving or reasoning task (I2 unclear). Include for full-text review to confirm whether the task fits the review’s problem-solving criteria.

Abstract lacks sufficient methodological detail to determine whether this is a task-based fMRI study of problem-solving that includes healthy adult participants. Title/abstract focus on decision-making in hoarding disorder but do not state the task, fMRI modality, or presence of healthy control group. Include for full-text review to assess I1–I4.

Study examines muscle functional MRI and EMG of lumbar muscles during trunk-extension exercise, not a task-based brain fMRI of problem-solving in healthy adults. It does not involve problem-solving tasks or report task-related brain activation relevant to the review.

Study focuses on anatomical/functional connectivity in schizophrenia (connectivity-focused) with no mention of a problem-solving task or healthy adult task-based fMRI results; not relevant to task-based problem-solving in healthy adults.

This is a resting-state functional connectivity study in Alzheimer's patients and healthy controls, not a task-based fMRI investigation of problem-solving or reasoning. It is connectivity/resting-state only and therefore does not meet the review objective.

Abstract focuses on TMS targets and intrinsic functional connectivity with the subgenual cingulate (connectivity/resting-state emphasis), not a task-based fMRI study of problem-solving. No evidence participants performed a problem-solving or reasoning task; thus it meets exclusion criterion for connectivity-only/resting-state studies.

This is an animal neuropathic-pain molecular/neurophysiology study (sciatic nerve ligation) examining microRNAs and DNMT3a in nucleus accumbens, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adult humans.

This study uses muscle functional MRI to measure neck muscle activation during extension exercises, not task-based brain fMRI of problem-solving. Participants perform physical exercise, not cognitive problem-solving, and no task-related brain activation is reported. Exclusion: non-brain fMRI study (E4).

This article is a narrative review (not an original empirical task-based fMRI study) focused on appetite, obesity, and menstrual-cycle effects, not on problem-solving tasks in healthy adults. Excluded as a review/meta-analysis.

Not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. The paper reports PET and TMS in chronic stroke patients performing motor rehabilitation tasks, not problem-solving in healthy adults. Therefore it fails key inclusion requirements.

Abstract explicitly indicates this is a review article summarizing PET and SPECT functional imaging findings; it is a review (E1) and focuses on non-fMRI modalities (E4), so it does not meet the task-based fMRI criteria for problem-solving in healthy adults.

The article is a review of functional neuroanatomy of episodic memory (not a primary task-based fMRI study of problem solving). It discusses PET and other neuroimaging and summarizes prior studies, so it is a review and focuses on non-fMRI modalities rather than task-based fMRI problem-solving in healthy adults.

This is a narrative/review/commentary on genetics and clinical management of primary hyperaldosteronism, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It involves patient populations and reports no task-related fMRI results.

This is an animal (mouse) proteomic study of beta-catenin activation, not a task-based fMRI study in humans performing problem-solving or reasoning tasks. It is non-fMRI and has no healthy adult human sample.

This is a molecular genomics study of a nonphotosynthetic alga (Cryptomonas paramecium); it does not involve human participants or fMRI/neuroimaging, and does not report task-related brain activation. Exclude for non-fMRI study and no healthy adult sample.

The abstract describes interactions between vascular risk and β-amyloid on cortical thickness (structural/biomarker imaging) in AD-vulnerable regions. It does not report a task-based fMRI problem-solving or reasoning task or task-related activation in healthy adults. Therefore it is not a task-based fMRI study.

Study investigates cerebral glucose metabolism in Parkinson disease dementia patients using nuclear medicine methods after cholinesterase therapy—not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving, and it does not include healthy adult participants. Therefore it does not meet the review criteria.

This is a biochemical/structural study of an RSV protein expressed in E. coli and characterized in vitro/in infected cells; it does not involve human task-based fMRI or problem-solving in healthy adults and reports no fMRI data.

This abstract is an overview/review of radionuclide cisternography and diagnostics of normal-pressure hydrocephalus, discussing SPECT/PET and radioisotope methods. It is not a task-based fMRI study, includes no healthy adult problem-solving participants, and uses non-fMRI modalities.

This is a narrative review focused on genetic and preclinical (rodent and mouse) studies of serotonergic function; it is not a task-based fMRI study of human problem-solving and reports no human adult participants or task-related fMRI activations. Exclusion criteria E1, E3, and E4 apply.

The abstract describes molecular cloning of a viral gene from duck enteritis virus (non-human molecular biology). It is not a task-based fMRI study, involves no human participants or problem-solving tasks, and therefore does not meet the review criteria.

This is a veterinary microbiology study of hemotropic mycoplasmas in cats, fleas, ticks, and rodents. It is not an fMRI study and includes no human adult participants, so it does not meet the review criteria for task-based fMRI in healthy adults.

This study is a PET radiotracer biodistribution study in nonhuman primates focusing on P-glycoprotein and chemotherapy pharmacokinetics, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adult humans.

Task-based fMRI with a healthy adult group (18–26) and reported task-related activation. The task is decision-making (gambling) which may not be classic problem-solving, but abstract alone is ambiguous regarding whether it fits the review's problem-solving definition; include for full-text review.

This is a behavioral auditory vection study with no task-based fMRI methods or reported brain activation results, and it does not involve a problem-solving task. Therefore it is a non-fMRI study and meets exclusion criterion E4.

This is a narrative review focused on pediatric brain imaging (DTI and other MRI techniques) from fetal to childhood, not an original task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It is a review and does not report task-related fMRI results in adults.

The study uses task-based fMRI and reports task-related activations, but participants are adolescent females (mean age 15.5), not healthy adults aged 18–65, and the task examines cue-reward learning/food reward habituation rather than problem-solving or reasoning. Therefore it does not meet the adult sample requirement and is outside the review objective.

The study used resting-state fMRI (ReHo) to assess brain changes and reported behavioral performance, but did not perform task-based fMRI or report task-related activation; thus it meets exclusion criterion E2 (resting-state/connectivity-only).

This is a resting-state/functional connectivity fMRI study examining lifespan trajectories of the precuneus and default network using gRAICAR; it does not involve a task-based problem-solving or reasoning paradigm.

The abstract describes a resting-state functional connectivity article focused on stroke patients and recovery, not a task-based fMRI study of problem-solving in healthy adults. It focuses on connectivity measures and clinical samples and appears to be a review/discussion. Therefore it meets exclusion criteria.

Abstract is a review/discussion of decision-making and schizophrenia (mentions prior studies and suggestions) and not an original task-based fMRI study. It does not report task-related fMRI results in healthy adults. Thus it meets exclusion for reviews (E1) and for being a non-fMRI study (E4).

Participants are male adolescents with conduct disorder and substance use disorder; no healthy adult (18–65) sample is reported. Therefore it does not meet the population criterion for this review (E3).

This study uses resting-state functional connectivity and diffusion (structural) imaging rather than task-based fMRI, and it examines syntactic language processing rather than problem-solving/reasoning tasks. Therefore it meets the resting-state/connectivity-only exclusion.

Although this is a task-based fMRI study with healthy adult participants and reports task-related activation (I1, I3, I4), the task is isometric ankle dorsiflexion (motor control), not a problem-solving or reasoning task (does not meet I2). Therefore it does not address the review objective.

The study is an animal (murine) hyperpolarized MR spectroscopy study of skeletal muscle metabolism, not a human task-based fMRI of problem solving. It includes no healthy adult participants and is not an fMRI study of the target task/domain.

The study examines intrinsic BOLD fluctuations after ingestion of sweetened beverages in lean and obese women (brain–gut/physiologic effects), not a problem-solving or reasoning task. It appears to be resting-state/physiologic imaging rather than task-based problem-solving, so it does not meet the review objective.

The study is an MRI flow analysis of a ventricular assist device in a flow circuit, not a task-based fMRI study of human participants performing problem-solving tasks. It does not include a healthy adult sample or report task-related brain activation.

Abstract lacks methodological and participant details: it names ‘functional imaging’ and clinical decision-making but does not explicitly state task-based fMRI, ages or healthy adult sample, or report task activation results. The study likely meets the review topic but provides insufficient information to confirm all inclusion criteria — include for full-text review.

This is a PET study of myocardial 13N-ammonia uptake/flow in cardiac patients undergoing revascularization, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. No problem-solving task or healthy adult sample is reported.
This is a PET imaging case study of an Alzheimer's disease patient, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It uses non-fMRI modalities and includes no healthy adult sample.

This is a narrative review of advanced cerebral monitoring techniques, not an original task-based fMRI study of problem-solving in healthy adults. The abstract describes review content and non-fMRI modalities (CT, PET, microdialysis), so E1 (review) and E4 (non-fMRI) apply.
This is not an fMRI study and investigates postoperative patients (craniectomy/cranioplasty) using TCD and FDG-PET rather than task-based fMRI; participants are not healthy adults.

Animal study using rats and FDG-microPET assessing resting-state brain activity; not a task-based fMRI study of human adults performing problem-solving tasks. Does not include healthy adult humans and is non-fMRI and resting-state focused.

This is a review of in vivo imaging in Parkinson’s disease graft trials (patients), not a task-based fMRI study of problem-solving in healthy adults. It discusses PET/SPECT and MRI broadly and is a review article, so it does not meet inclusion criteria.

This study examines cranioplasty effects on cerebrovascular regulation and metabolism in patients after decompressive craniectomy; it is not a task-based fMRI study of problem-solving and does not involve healthy adult participants. It appears to use other imaging/modalities focused on clinical neurosurgical outcomes rather than task-related fMRI activation.

This is a cellular/molecular study of glucose transporters in prostate tissue using immunohistochemistry and mRNA analysis; it is not a task-based fMRI study, nor does it involve healthy adult participants performing problem-solving tasks.

The study used voxel-based morphometry (structural MRI) and PET to examine gray matter density and dopamine metabolism in fibromyalgia; it is not a task-based fMRI study nor does it report task-related problem-solving activations.

Abstract describes cardiac imaging (SPECT/PET), biomarkers, and catecholamines in Takotsubo cardiomyopathy patients — not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving and does not report healthy adult task-related brain activation. Exclusion criteria E4 (non-fMRI) and E3 (no healthy adult sample) apply.
This study used PET (not fMRI) and investigated sexual behavior/ejaculation rather than problem-solving tasks; it therefore does not meet I1 or I2. Exclusion criterion E4 (Non-fMRI studies) applies.

Study used PET (not fMRI) and focuses on vocal production/functional connectivity rather than task-based problem solving in fMRI; thus it does not meet the fMRI requirement and is outside the review objective.

This is a non-human PET imaging study in rats using radiotracers, not a task-based fMRI study of human problem solving. It does not include healthy adult human participants or report fMRI task-related activations.

This is an animal (mouse) study investigating neuroinflammation and receptor function; no human participants or task-based fMRI problem-solving data are reported. Therefore it does not meet the review’s population or modality criteria.

Study was conducted in nonhuman primates (not healthy adult humans) and is an animal pharmacology study; abstract does not report task-based fMRI. Fails inclusion criteria for human sample and fMRI modality.

Abstract text is not provided and title alone is insufficient to determine whether this is a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. Title suggests cannabis users and structural/functional measures, but cannot confirm I1–I4 or exclusions. Include for full-text review.

This is a molecular pathology review of pancreatic neuroendocrine tumors, not a task-based fMRI study. It involves no fMRI, no problem-solving tasks, and does not include healthy adult fMRI participants. Meets E1, E3, and E4.
This is a review article about molecular imaging of apoptosis, not a task-based fMRI study of problem-solving in healthy adults. It is a review (E1) and not an fMRI study (E4).
This is a nonsystematic literature review about MRSA in animals/humans, not an empirical task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It is a review (E1) and not an fMRI study (E4).
This is a PET (H2(15)O) study of ASL production, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving. It therefore fails the fMRI requirement and does not address problem-solving tasks.

This is an in vitro molecular study of canine mammary tumor cell lines (non-human), not a task-based fMRI study. It includes no healthy adult human participants and reports no brain activation results, so it does not meet the review objectives.

This is a clinical protocol for chemosensitivity testing in stage III colon cancer patients; it is not a task-based fMRI study and does not include healthy adult participants performing problem-solving tasks. Does not meet fMRI/problem-solving/healthy adult criteria.

Not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults: the human data report PET findings on sexual activity/ejaculation and animal neuroanatomy; no problem-solving task and imaging modality is PET, not fMRI.
This is an immunology study of recombinant gliadins and T cell reactivity in coeliac disease; it is not an fMRI study and does not involve healthy adults performing problem-solving tasks.

Participants are children/adolescents (not healthy adults 18-65) and the task is social orienting/attention, not problem-solving or reasoning; therefore the study does not meet the review’s focus on problem-solving fMRI in healthy adults.

The study participants are breast cancer survivors rather than healthy adults, and the paper reports multimodal MRI (DTI, 1H-MRS, VBM) with fMRI findings referenced from a previous publication; it is not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. Excluded for no healthy adult sample and non-task fMRI modality.

The study used a verbal monitoring/language task, not a problem-solving or reasoning task, so it fails inclusion criterion I2. It is task-based fMRI, includes healthy adult participants, and reports task-related activations (I1, I3, I4), but does not address problem-solving in healthy adults.

This study uses resting-state attention-related network analysis rather than a task-based fMRI paradigm and does not report a problem-solving or reasoning task. It therefore meets the exclusion criterion for resting-state/connectivity-only studies and is not relevant to task-based problem-solving in healthy adults.

Study investigates default-mode network connectivity using ICA in mild TBI patients and healthy controls (resting-state/connectivity focus), not a task-based problem-solving fMRI study in healthy adults. Therefore it meets exclusion criterion E2.

This is a narrative overview/review about lymphoma treatments, not a task-based fMRI study. It does not involve healthy adult participants performing problem-solving tasks, nor does it report task-related fMRI activation results. Therefore it meets multiple exclusion criteria.

This is an animal (rabbit) imaging study using 123I-MIBG, not a task-based fMRI study in humans. It includes no healthy adult participants and is not an fMRI study, so it clearly fails inclusion criteria.

This is a molecular genetic study of a parasite (Baylisascaris columnaris) in non-human samples; it is not an fMRI task study, includes no healthy adult human participants, and reports no task-related brain activation. Meets exclusion criteria E3 and E4.

This is a review article focused on PET/SPECT receptor imaging and tracer methodology, not an original task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It is non-fMRI and a review, so it does not meet the inclusion criteria.

This is an animal (cat) study of cerebral venous infarction, not a task-based fMRI study in humans performing problem-solving; it does not include healthy adult participants or report task-related fMRI activations relevant to the review.

Non-human primate study (rhesus monkeys) using FDG-PET imaging rather than task-based fMRI, and does not involve healthy adult human participants performing problem-solving or reasoning tasks. Does not meet inclusion criteria for human task-based fMRI of problem solving.

This is an in vitro virology study expressing pig NF-kappaB proteins and testing effects on PRRSV in cell culture; it is not a human task-based fMRI study of problem solving, includes no healthy adult participants, and reports no fMRI brain activation results.

This is a perspective/review on biomarkers and diagnostic criteria in Alzheimer’s disease, not an original task-based fMRI study of problem-solving in healthy adults. It does not report task-related fMRI activation. Exclude as a review article.

This is a molecular biology study in yeast describing mitochondrial peptidase function; it involves no human participants and no fMRI methods. It does not meet the task-based fMRI in healthy adults requirement.

This is a resting-state functional connectivity study comparing Alzheimer’s patients and age-matched controls; no task-based problem-solving or task-related activation in healthy adults is reported. Excluded under E2 (resting-state or connectivity-only study).

This is a resting-state MEG/EEG study with alcohol challenge, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It therefore meets exclusion criteria for resting-state studies and non-fMRI modalities.

This is an Account/review article on proton-coupled electron transfer and electrochemical/photochemical studies of molecules; it is not a task-based fMRI study, includes no human participants, and is a review—meeting exclusion criteria E1, E3, and E4.
The study uses a vibrotactile somatosensory task, not a problem-solving or reasoning task, so it does not meet inclusion criterion I2.

This is a resting-state intrinsic connectivity study in fibromyalgia patients; it is not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving and includes no healthy adult sample or task-related activation results. Excluded per criteria E2 and E3.

This is a resting-state fMRI connectivity study in Parkinson’s patients (with healthy controls), not a task-based fMRI study of problem-solving in healthy adults. It does not report task-related activations. Exclusion: resting-state/connectivity-only study.

This study is not a task-based fMRI investigation of problem solving in healthy adults. It reports experimental lung FDG uptake and ventilation-perfusion mismatch after smoke inhalation (likely PET and/or animal/clinical injury model), with no problem-solving tasks or healthy adult fMRI data.

This is an animal (rat) PET study examining PDE4 binding after neurotransmitter manipulations, not a task-based fMRI study in humans performing problem-solving. It has no healthy adult human participants and is a non-fMRI study, so it clearly meets exclusion criteria.

This is an overview/review about coronary circulation in hypertension (non-fMRI) involving hypertensive patients, not task-based fMRI of problem solving in healthy adults. It does not report task-related brain activation. Exclude.

This is an animal (rat) treadmill study measuring tendon mechanics and gene expression; it is not an fMRI study and does not include healthy adult human participants. Therefore it fails the review population and imaging modality criteria.

This is a preclinical PET imaging study in tumor xenografts (animals) using radiotracer and drug interventions. It is not a task-based fMRI study, nor does it include healthy adult human participants. Therefore it fails the review's fMRI and healthy adult criteria.
This is a molecular biology/biochemistry study of HCV protein expression in E. coli; it is not a task-based fMRI study and includes no human healthy adult participants or fMRI activation results.

This is an in vitro molecular biology study of a fusion enzyme expressed in E. coli; no fMRI methodology, no human participants, and no problem-solving task are reported. It does not meet the review inclusion criteria.

This is a review of in vivo imaging in non-human primate (NHP) malaria models — not an original task-based fMRI study in humans. It includes no healthy adult participants and is not an fMRI experiment.

This is a molecular virology study of a duck enteritis virus gene and recombinant protein expression in E. coli; it does not involve fMRI, human participants, or problem-solving tasks. It clearly is a non-fMRI study with no healthy adult sample, so it is irrelevant to the meta-analysis objective.

This is a bio-inorganic materials/molecular biology study on ZnO synthesis with recombinant peptides; it does not involve fMRI, human participants, or problem-solving tasks. Exclude as non-fMRI and no healthy adult sample.

This study evaluates MR-to-CT whole-body deformable image registration and optimization; it is not an fMRI study and does not involve task-based problem-solving or report task-related brain activation. Exclude under E4 (Non-fMRI studies).

This is an early-childhood allergy/clinical epidemiology study, not a task-based fMRI study. It does not involve adults (18-65) or report task-related brain activation. Therefore it fails the review scope.
This is an EEG study of isometric motor tasks in healthy subjects, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving. It fails the fMRI requirement and does not involve problem-solving tasks.

This is a resting-state functional connectivity fMRI study (no task-based problem-solving or reasoning task) comparing aMCI patients and cognitively normal controls; therefore excluded under E2 (resting-state/connectivity-only).

This is an epidemiological study of Toxoplasma gondii in pregnant women; it is not a task-based fMRI study, does not involve problem-solving tasks, and does not report brain activation results. Exclude as a non-fMRI study.
This is an FDG-PET study measuring glucose metabolism, not a task-based fMRI study of problem-solving in healthy adults. It does not meet I1/I2 and is a non-fMRI study (E4).
Imaging modality is PET (regional cerebral blood flow), not fMRI, and tasks are motor/posturing/ankle movements—not problem-solving; thus fails fMRI requirement (E4).

This is a narrative review of antipsychotic drug mechanisms and therapeutics, not an empirical task-based fMRI study. It does not report task-related fMRI results in healthy adults. It is a review article and non-fMRI, so it meets exclusion criteria.

This study uses PET metabolic imaging in patients with mild Alzheimer’s disease, not task-based fMRI, and does not include healthy adult problem-solving participants. It therefore fails criteria I1 (not fMRI) and I3 (no healthy adult sample) — exclusion criteria E4 and E3 apply.

Study describes ASL perfusion and susceptibility-weighted imaging in acute stroke patients (clinical imaging), not task-based fMRI in healthy adults. It involves patient stroke populations and is not an fMRI task study, so it does not meet the review objective.

This is a molecular/cellular study using cell lines (HeLa, HepG2, ECa109) investigating NBPF regulation by NF-κB; it is not a task-based fMRI study, includes no healthy adult participants, and reports no brain activation results.

This is a review/book-chapter on dopamine D3 receptors and pharmacology, not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving in healthy adults. It does not report human task-related fMRI activation results.

This is an international survey of surgeons about esophageal cancer staging and neoadjuvant therapy—no task-based fMRI, no problem-solving task in healthy adults, and no brain activation results. Therefore it is not an fMRI study and does not include healthy adult fMRI data.

This study investigates neck fat and cardiovascular/metabolic risk; the abstract contains no mention of fMRI, task-based imaging, problem-solving tasks, or brain activation results. It is therefore a non-fMRI study and not relevant to the review.

Study is a molecular characterization of a parasite (Schistosoma japonicum); it is not a human task-based fMRI study, includes no healthy adult participants, and reports no fMRI data or brain activation results.

This is a post-mortem neuropathological and biochemical study, not a task-based fMRI experiment with participants performing problem-solving tasks. It does not report task-related fMRI activation in healthy adults.

This is a molecular/genomic review of gastroenteropancreatic neuroendocrine neoplasms, not a task-based fMRI experiment. It is a review article (E1), does not involve fMRI (E4), and does not include healthy adult participants performing problem-solving tasks (E3).
This is a preclinical small-animal PET/biodistribution study of 45Ti-transferrin, not a task-based fMRI study; it does not include healthy adult human participants and is not fMRI-based.

This is a hypothesis/review article about music-induced reward and genetics; it does not report a task-based fMRI study of problem-solving in healthy adults and appears to be a review/hypothesis paper.

This is a clinical case report of a single patient with a genetic neurodegenerative syndrome; it does not involve task-based fMRI nor include healthy adult participants. Does not meet the review inclusion criteria.
Study is an animal (rat) toxicology and mass spectrometry investigation, not a human task-based fMRI study of problem solving. It does not include healthy adult humans and is non-fMRI.

This is an inorganic chemistry study on iron complexes and catalytic hydrogenation; it involves no human participants or fMRI methods. It does not report task-based brain activation in healthy adults.

Not a task-based fMRI study of problem solving. The abstract describes metabolic imaging using radiolabelled tracers (PET/SPECT) and reads like a review; it does not report task-based fMRI with healthy adults performing problem-solving tasks.

This is a clinical study using magnetic resonance spectroscopy to differentiate tumor recurrence vs radiation necrosis in glioma patients. It is not a task-based fMRI study, does not involve healthy adult participants or problem-solving tasks, and does not report task-related fMRI activation.

Not a task-based fMRI study: uses PET β-amyloid imaging (non-fMRI), and participants are patients with primary progressive aphasia rather than healthy adults. Does not meet key inclusion criteria for the review.

The study uses serial PIB PET and structural MRI (not task-based fMRI) and does not report task-related fMRI activation or a problem-solving task. Participants include clinical groups (MCI, AD) rather than a task-based healthy adult fMRI sample. Exclude for being a non-fMRI study.

Clinical phase 1/2 trial in rectal cancer patients testing valproic acid with radiotherapy and chemotherapy; not an fMRI study, does not involve healthy adult participants or report task-related brain activation or problem-solving tasks.

This is an autoradiography/PET tracer binding study on autopsied brain tissue (and Alzheimer patients/controls), not a task-based fMRI study. No problem-solving task or task-related brain activation in healthy adult participants is reported.

The abstract describes synthesis and crystallographic/chemical characterization of Pd-Pt metal clusters—no human participants, no task-based fMRI, and no brain activation results. It is a non-fMRI chemistry study, so it does not meet the review objective.

This is a clinical case report comparing schizophrenia and frontotemporal dementia; no task-based fMRI methods or task-related brain activation are described, and no healthy adult sample is included. It does not address problem-solving fMRI in healthy adults.

fulltext_screening_results.reason: This is a task-based fMRI study with group-level (random-effects) whole-brain analyses using SPM8. The task is a logical reasoning/problem-solving paradigm (Aristotelian premise-conclusion contradiction identification), which qualifies as multi-step verbal/logical problem solving. Participants are healthy adults (ages reported within 25–42; mean ~31), matching the 18–65 criterion. The paper reports peak MNI coordinates for activations (e.g., MNI: –26 32 54 and others) and applies whole-brain and SVC inference. The study is empirical, not ROI-only, not resting-state/connectivity-only, and reports within-group task effects, so it meets all inclusion criteria.

This is a task-based fMRI study with group-level whole-brain analyses of arithmetic problem solving. The experiment uses an arithmetic verification task (single-digit addition) performed during fMRI with whole-brain GLM and searchlight multivoxel analyses; peak MNI coordinates are reported for clusters (e.g., hippocampus). The sample includes a healthy adult group (ages 19–22) reported separately in cross-sectional analyses. Thus the study provides whole-brain task effects for a math problem-solving paradigm in healthy adults and meets the review’s requirements.

This is a task-based, whole-brain group fMRI study of inductive (multi-step) reasoning in healthy adults, meeting I5, I6, and I7. However, the provided full text does not report peak activation coordinates (MNI or Talairach) for the problem-solving contrasts (only anatomical labels and Brodmann areas are described, with references to figures/tables but no coordinates in the excerpt). Because the study does not provide peak coordinates for contrasts of interest, it fails inclusion criterion I8 and therefore must be excluded from the meta-analysis.

This study reports a task-based fMRI investigation with group-level whole-brain analyses (whole-brain t-tests and multiple regressions) of analogical (relational/multi-step) reasoning, a clear problem-solving task. It includes a healthy adult group (ages 19–26) analyzed and reported separately from children. The paper provides peak coordinates for activation clusters (e.g., left RLPFC: −27, 50, 0; right RLPFC: 27, 45, 16; right SPL: 36, −66, 52), satisfying coordinate reporting. No exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity/resting-state only, simple non-problem-solving task, between-group-only contrasts, or non-empirical paper) apply. Therefore all required inclusion criteria are met.

This is a task-based fMRI study using whole-brain, group-level random-effects GLM analyses on healthy adult participants who performed a multi-step, recursive problem-solving task (Hit-N/Backward Induction). The manuscript reports whole-brain contrasts (rfxGLM), thresholding (FDR q<0.05), and specific peak coordinates for activations (e.g., Insula at [41,19,3], VLPFC at [47,40,3]). Participants comprise a healthy adult sample in the fMRI study (N=12). Thus it satisfies inclusion criteria I5 (task-based fMRI with group whole-brain analyses), I6 (multi-step problem-solving task), I7 (healthy adult group), and I8 (reports peak coordinates). Age ranges are not explicitly reported, leaving minor uncertainty about exact ages, but the sample is described as healthy adults.

This is a methodological rtfMRI paper developing an online spatial normalization algorithm and validating it using a finger-tapping (motor) task. It does not investigate problem-solving or multi-step reasoning across domains, nor report problem-solving task contrasts or peak coordinates. Therefore it fails the review’s core inclusion criterion (I6) and meets exclusion criterion E7 (simple/non-problem-solving task).

The study uses a probabilistic reversal learning (reinforcement learning) task and examines ventral striatal prediction error signals; it does not employ a multi-step reasoning/problem-solving task (math, verbal, logical, visuospatial, physics). Although it is a task-based fMRI study in healthy adults and reports peak coordinates for PE-related activations, the cognitive demand is simple reinforcement learning rather than the complex, multi-step problem-solving targeted by the review. Therefore it fails the core inclusion criterion I6 and meets exclusion criterion E7 (simple tasks without clear problem-solving demands).

This is a task-based fMRI study using the Raven’s Standard Progressive Matrices (a multi-step analogical/problem-solving task) with group-level, whole-brain voxel-wise analyses. The sample includes a healthy adult control group (NC; n=26, age-matched 18–50) reported separately alongside an ASD group. The methods describe whole-brain second-level analyses and voxel-wise contrasts (analytic vs baseline) and report cluster peaks in MNI space (figures and text reference MNI coordinates and peak voxels), satisfying peak-coordinate reporting. The task clearly involves multi-step reasoning (analytic RSPM items). No exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity-only, resting-state, simple tasks, between-group-only contrasts, or non-empirical paper) apply. Therefore the study meets all inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis objective.

This is a task-based fMRI study with group-level, random-effects whole-brain analyses contrasting choice vs. no-choice and RU vs. RD conditions. The task is an arithmetic computational-estimation problem-solving task requiring multi-step reasoning (deciding a rounding strategy and executing the estimate). Participants are healthy adults aged 19–30 (n=18 analyzed). The paper describes whole-brain SPM analyses and reports activation clusters for problem-solving contrasts; therefore it meets the meta-analysis requirements. Confidence is moderate because the provided excerpt does not explicitly list peak coordinate tables in MNI/Talairach space, but the methods and results describe whole-brain contrasts and cluster reporting consistent with standard reporting of peak coordinates in the full article.

This study reports a task-based fMRI experiment on solving arithmetic word problems (multi-step reasoning). Group-level whole-brain multiple regression analyses were performed in SPM8 with covariates; results report peak coordinates in MNI space and cluster-level FWE correction. Participants were healthy adult university students (n=36 analyzed, mean age ~24), satisfying the healthy adult criterion. The task clearly involves multi-step problem solving (encoding, translation, calculation). Therefore it meets all inclusion requirements for whole-brain, task-based fMRI problem-solving studies.

Although this is a task-based, whole-brain fMRI study of multi-step visuospatial reasoning (Raven's Progressive Matrices) and reports peak-based analyses, the healthy comparison group includes participants aged 14–36 and is not reported as an adult-only (18–65) sample nor are adult-only results provided. The review requires at least one healthy adult group (18–65) reported separately if mixed. Because the healthy group is mixed with minors and not separated, the study does not meet the age criterion for inclusion in the adult problem-solving meta-analysis.

Although this is a task-based, whole-brain fMRI study in healthy adult groups and reports MNI coordinates for activation clusters, the paper’s reported neuroimaging results focus on between-group contrasts (higher vs. lower mathematical competence) rather than within-group task effects or task>baseline problem-solving contrasts. The excerpt does not present whole-brain peak coordinates for the task-related problem-solving effect within healthy groups. Additionally, the experimental task is a representation-matching task with only occasional simple arithmetic (addition/subtraction of small integers), which does not clearly constitute multi-step problem-solving as defined by the meta-analysis. Therefore the study fails to meet the meta-analysis inclusion requirements and is excluded.

This is an empirical, task-based fMRI study that includes a mental calculation task (visual and auditory subtraction) which qualifies as multi-step problem-solving. The paper reports group-level, whole-brain random-effects (RFX) analyses on 81 healthy adult participants (ages 24 ±4.1), and lists activation maxima with stereotaxic coordinates (MNI) and t-values for contrasts including calculation. There are no ROI-only, connectivity-only, resting-state, between-group-only, or non-empirical limitations. Therefore all required inclusion criteria are met.

This study is excluded because it does not meet key inclusion criteria: fMRI was performed on a single 16-year-old abacus expert (n=1), not a group-level analysis of healthy adults (18–65). Therefore I5 (group-level whole-brain analyses) and I7 (healthy adult group 18–65) are not satisfied. The study does use task-based fMRI of a mathematical problem-solving task and reports Talairach coordinates (satisfying I6 and I8), but failing either I5 or I7 is sufficient for exclusion. No exclusion criteria from the list (E5–E9) directly apply (whole-brain results and peak coordinates are reported, task is not resting-state, and this is an empirical study).

This is a task-based fMRI study of multi-step problem-solving (number-series reasoning) in healthy adults with group-level whole-brain analyses (SPM8). However, the provided full text does not report peak activation coordinates (MNI or Talairach) for the problem-solving contrasts, only descriptions of clusters and a figure. Because the meta-analysis requires reported peak coordinates (I8), this study cannot contribute coordinate data and must be excluded.

This is a task-based fMRI study that performed group-level whole-brain voxelwise (random-effects) analyses using SPM5 on healthy adult participants (Chinese–English bilinguals, ages 23–35). The task is multidigit mental addition (exact and approximate), which qualifies as multi-step mathematical problem-solving. Analyses used MNI normalization and report peak coordinates in Supplementary Tables (MNI coordinates referenced), with contrasts and intersection analyses described. No exclusion criteria (e.g., ROI-only, resting-state, connectivity-only, non-empirical) apply. Therefore the study meets inclusion criteria I5, I6, I7, and I8.

This is a task-based fMRI study with group-level whole-brain random-effects analyses (SPM5) reporting voxel-wise results and cluster thresholds. The task is a verbal/logical inductive reasoning (inclusion fallacy) requiring comparative judgment—fits ‘problem-solving’ within verbal/logical reasoning domains. Participants are healthy adults (n=62 behavioral, n=15 in fMRI analyses; mean age 23.6), within 18–65. The paper reports peak MNI coordinates for activation foci (e.g., MNI: −39,15,9; 48,33,18; etc.). No exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity-only, resting-state, between-group only, or non-empirical) apply. Therefore all inclusion criteria I5, I6, I7, and I8 are satisfied.

This is a behavioral study (reaction times and strategy ratings) designed for neuroimaging compatibility but no fMRI data were collected or reported. It does not include any task-based fMRI acquisition, group-level whole-brain analyses, or reported peak coordinates (MNI/Talairach). Although the task is a multi-step arithmetic problem-solving task in healthy adults (meets I6 and I7), it fails to meet key inclusion criteria I5 and I8, so it must be excluded from a meta-analysis of whole-brain task-based fMRI studies.

Although the study includes mental-calculation (math) tasks in healthy adults and reports simultaneous fMRI and fNIRS, it does not meet key inclusion requirements for this meta-analysis. The paper focuses on fNIRS methods and group fNIRS results; while fMRI was recorded and compared qualitatively, the full text does not report whole-brain peak activation coordinates (MNI or Talairach) or detailed voxelwise peak tables for the problem-solving contrasts. Inclusion criterion I8 (reporting peak coordinates) is not satisfied, and the manuscript does not provide the formal coordinate-based whole-brain results needed for meta-analysis. Therefore the study must be excluded.

This is a task-based fMRI study using group-level, whole-brain random-effects analyses (SPM8) on healthy adult participants (ages 19–56). The experimental task is multi-step deductive (syllogistic) reasoning (problem-solving across the logical domain). The paper reports peak MNI coordinates for multiple contrasts (examples and coordinates are provided in the text and supplementary tables). No exclusion criteria apply (not ROI-only, not connectivity/resting-state, not a review, and includes within-group task effects). Therefore it meets all inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis.

This is a task-based fMRI study with group-level whole-brain analyses (I5), but it does not meet key inclusion requirements: the participant groups include 17-year-olds and adults are not reported separately (fails I7), and the paper does not report peak activation coordinates (MNI/Talairach) for problem-solving contrasts (fails I8). The mental task is very simple arithmetic (single X×Y+Z), which raises concerns about lacking multi-step/problem-solving demands (meets exclusion E7). Because ALL required inclusion criteria (notably I7 and I8) are not satisfied, the study must be excluded.

The study reports a task-based fMRI experiment using a math calculation task (exact and approximate addition) and conducts group-level, whole-brain random-effects analyses with reported peak coordinates (MNI) for contrasts (Exact>Control, Approximate>Control, interactions). Participants are healthy adults (young females, mean age ~20), meeting age and health criteria. The math task falls under problem-solving domain (mathematical reasoning), and coordinates for task contrasts and interactions are provided in tables and text. Therefore it satisfies all required inclusion criteria for the meta-analysis.

The study is a task-based fMRI experiment with group-level random-effects whole-brain analyses (FWE-corrected thresholds reported). The task is mathematical problem-solving (single-digit multiplication) which falls within the ‘math’ problem-solving domain examined by the review. Participants are healthy adults within the 18–65 age range, reported separately by group. The paper reports peak coordinates in MNI space for the critical contrasts (large vs. small multiplication problems) and provides tables/coordinates for activations. Although the task uses single-digit problems (often relying on retrieval), it is still a task-based arithmetic problem-solving paradigm and meets the meta-analysis objective. Therefore it satisfies the inclusion criteria listed below.

This is a task-based fMRI study in healthy adult participants that uses group-level whole-brain analyses (predefined ROI analyses plus an exploratory whole-brain voxelwise contrast with cluster extent thresholds). The task is a multi-step mathematical problem-solving paradigm (pyramid problems) requiring algorithmic reasoning and learning. Participants are healthy adults (ages 18–40) and fMRI results are reported at the group level. The paper reports Talairach coordinates for predefined regions and exploratory peaks. All four inclusion criteria are met and no exclusion criteria apply.

This is a task-based, event-related fMRI study in healthy adult participants (mean age 28; N=16 after exclusion) that investigates multi-digit mental addition, a multi-step mathematical problem-solving task (carry operations, unit-decade integration). Group-level whole-brain analyses were performed (SPM8 second-level analyses; parametric contrasts) and results include peak coordinates reported in MNI space across multiple contrasts (unit sum, decade sum, carry, etc.). The study is not ROI-only, not resting-state, and reports within-group task effects. Therefore it meets the meta-analysis requirements for whole-brain task-based fMRI problem-solving in healthy adults.

The study uses a task-based fMRI paradigm (BART) probing risk-taking/decision-making under simulated microgravity, not a multi-step problem-solving task (math, verbal, logical, visuospatial, physics). Therefore it does not meet inclusion criterion I6. Although it includes healthy adult participants (I7) and performs whole-brain group-level fMRI analyses (I5), the task lacks clear multi-step reasoning demands and the provided full text does not report problem-solving contrasts or peak coordinates for such contrasts (I8 unmet). Primary exclusion applies because the task is not a problem-solving paradigm (E7).

This paper reports a task-based fMRI experiment (Experiment 1) using a verbal problem-solving task (compound remote associate problems). It conducted group-level random-effects, whole-brain analyses (voxelwise contrasts with cluster thresholding) and transformed data to Talairach space. Participants were healthy adults (ages 18–29). The manuscript reports peak coordinates for the primary problem-solving/insight contrast (e.g., peak t at Talairach 44, −9, −9). Therefore it satisfies all inclusion criteria I5, I6, I7, and I8; no exclusion criteria apply.

This is a task-based fMRI study with group-level random-effects whole-brain analyses (Talairach space) contrasting problem-solving conditions (NSI vs OSI). The task involves solving scientific problems by applying learned heuristic prototypes—clear problem-solving demands requiring generation of solutions/methods. Participants are healthy adult university students (ages 19–25) reported separately. Peak coordinates (Talairach) and cluster statistics are reported in the results/tables. No exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity/resting-state, simple non-problem tasks, between-group-only contrasts, or non-empirical paper) apply.

This is a task-based fMRI study (mental addition) with group-level, whole-brain analyses (SPM8 parametric conjunctions) reporting activation peaks used as seed coordinates (MNI) and listing seed region coordinates. The task involves multi-digit addition including carry operations (multi-step arithmetic problem solving). The fMRI sample comprises healthy adults (16 right-handed males, mean age 28), reported separately from the DTI sample. Results include whole-brain activation maps and peak voxels/coordinates for problem-related contrasts. The study is empirical, not ROI-only, not connectivity-only or resting-state only (DTI was also used but fMRI whole-brain results are present). Therefore it satisfies inclusion criteria I5, I6, I7, and I8.

This is a task-based, event-related fMRI study using a matrix-reasoning (relational complexity) problem-solving task (0/1/2 relations) in healthy adult participants (N=34, age 18–46). The authors report group-level random-effects whole-brain voxel-based analyses with FDR correction and report peak activation coordinates (tables with Brodmann areas and Talairach/MNI coordinates) and ROI/whole-brain results for problem-solving contrasts (e.g., 2-relational vs 1-relational; condition contrasts). The task involves multi-step visuospatial relational reasoning, not a simple perceptual or resting-state paradigm. Thus it meets all inclusion criteria for whole-brain, task-based fMRI of problem-solving in healthy adults.

This study reports a task-based fMRI Chinese-chess problem-solving experiment (multi-step reasoning), with group-level whole-brain GLM random-effects analyses and between-/within-group contrasts. Participants are healthy adult groups (GM/M and novices, ages within 18–65). Peak coordinates are reported in MNI space (e.g., PCC 10,-52,28; caudate coordinates provided). Thus it satisfies all inclusion criteria for whole-brain task fMRI problem-solving in healthy adults.

This is an fMRI study of financial decision-making under risk, not a multi-step problem-solving task. While it uses task-based fMRI with group-level whole-brain analyses (satisfying I5) and includes healthy adult participants aged 18–65 (satisfying I7), it does not involve multi-step reasoning/problem-solving across domains (violates I6). The provided full text also does not present peak activation coordinates for problem-solving contrasts (I8 not met). Therefore it does not meet all required inclusion criteria and meets exclusion criterion E7 (simple decision task without clear problem-solving demands).

The study is a task-based fMRI investigation with whole-brain analyses including healthy adult controls, but the experimental task is a self-reflection / insight evaluation task (sentences about self/other/semantic baseline), not a multi-step problem-solving task (math, logical, visuospatial, physics, etc.). Therefore it does not meet inclusion criterion I6 (problem-solving task). No other exclusion criteria apply. Exclusion based on E7: simple/non-problem-solving task.

The study is an fMRI investigation of spectators watching live theater focusing on adhesion to fiction, empathy, metaphor, and related neural/physiological correlates. It does not employ a problem-solving task involving multi-step reasoning (math, logical, visuospatial, etc.). Although it includes task-based, group-level whole-brain analyses and healthy adult participants, the experimental task lacks clear problem-solving demands required by the review. Therefore it does not meet the review’s core inclusion criterion for problem-solving tasks and must be excluded.

This is an fMRI study of spatial learning/navigation (SOP and JRD pointing tasks) and does not involve multi-step problem-solving or reasoning tasks (math, verbal, logical, physics). Although it uses task-based, group-level whole-brain analyses and reports peak coordinates in healthy adults, it fails inclusion criterion I6. It therefore meets exclusion criterion E7 (simple/ non–problem-solving tasks).

This is an fMRI study of Theory of Mind (social cognition) using animated shapes, not a multi-step problem-solving task. Although it includes group-level whole-brain analyses (I5) and healthy adult participants (I7), it does not involve problem-solving across domains or multi-step reasoning (fails I6). Therefore it does not meet the review objective. Exclusion criterion E7 (simple tasks without clear problem-solving demands) applies.

This is a task-based, event-related fMRI study with group-level (random effects) whole-brain voxelwise analyses reporting significant activation peaks. The task is a verbal problem-solving (Chinese ‘chengyu’ riddles) requiring constraint relaxation/chunk decomposition and selection—i.e. multi-step/problem-solving reasoning. Participants are healthy adults aged 21–35. Peak coordinates are reported in MNI space (e.g., left middle temporal gyrus at (–52,–30,–2)) for insight vs. non-insight contrasts. No exclusion criteria (ROI-only, connectivity/resting-state, simple non-problem tasks, between-group-only contrasts, or non-empirical paper) apply.

This study does not examine problem-solving as defined by the review (multi-step reasoning tasks such as math, verbal, logical, visuospatial problem solving). The fMRI tasks are a reality-evaluation task (real vs. unreal picture judgments) and a recognition memory task; these involve context appraisal, perception and memory/recognition rather than explicit multi-step problem-solving. Although the study meets several methodological inclusion items (group-level whole-brain SPM analyses, includes a healthy adult control group, and reports cluster coordinates), it fails inclusion criterion I6. Therefore it should be excluded under exclusion criterion E7 (simple tasks without clear problem-solving demands).

This study does not examine problem-solving. The fMRI task is symptom provocation using trauma-related vs neutral pictures, not a multi-step reasoning/problem-solving paradigm (fails I6). Although it is a task-based, group-level whole-brain fMRI study that reports peak coordinates and includes a healthy control group (I5, I7, I8 satisfied), the experimental task assesses emotional reactivity in PTSD rather than problem-solving across domains required by the review. Therefore it should be excluded.

The study does not examine problem-solving or multi-step reasoning across domains. It reports a perceptual implicit sequence-learning task (serial color matching) rather than tasks involving multi-step mathematical, verbal, logical, visuospatial, or physics problem-solving. Although it meets several methodological criteria (task-based fMRI with group-level whole-brain analyses, healthy adult sample, and reported MNI peak coordinates), it fails the key content inclusion criterion I6 and therefore must be excluded. This also corresponds to exclusion criterion E7 (simple task without clear problem-solving demands).

This is a task-based, whole-brain fMRI study in healthy adult participants that reports peak coordinates, but the experimental task examines visuo-tactile object recognition and fMRI-adaptation (shape perception and multisensory integration), not multi-step problem-solving across domains. Therefore it fails the core inclusion criterion I6 (problem-solving task) and meets exclusion criterion E7 (simple/non-problem-solving task).

This study should be excluded because it does not meet key inclusion criteria for the review. Participants are adolescents (12–18 years), not healthy adults aged 18–65 (I7 not met). The task (Embedded Figures Task) is a visuospatial perceptual search task rather than a multi-step reasoning/problem-solving paradigm as defined by the review (I6 not met). The study does meet task-based fMRI with group-level whole-brain analyses and reports peak coordinates (I5 and I8), but all inclusion criteria must be satisfied. The most applicable exclusion criterion is E7 (simple tasks without clear problem-solving demands).

The study uses task-based, whole-brain fMRI in healthy adults but the experimental task is an emotional face-matching task (anger/fear) to probe negative-emotion-related activity, not a multi-step problem-solving or reasoning task. Therefore it does not meet inclusion criterion I6 (problem-solving task). It also does not address the review objective of problem-solving across domains. Exclusion criterion E7 (simple tasks without clear problem-solving demands) applies.

The study uses a go/no-go sustained attention task (SART), which assesses response inhibition/attention rather than multi-step problem-solving across domains. Although it includes task-based BOLD fMRI with group-level whole-brain analyses (I5) and tests healthy adults aged 20–35 (I7), it does not employ a problem-solving paradigm (fails I6) and therefore meets exclusion criterion E7 (simple task without clear problem-solving demands). Additionally, the paper does not report peak coordinates for any problem-solving contrasts (I8 not applicable). Therefore the study should be excluded from a meta-analysis of problem-solving fMRI studies.

This study is an fMRI investigation of visual attention/multiple-element processing in dyslexic and control adults, not a problem-solving task involving multi-step reasoning (e.g., math, logical, visuospatial problem solving). Although it includes task-based, group-level whole-brain analyses and healthy adult controls, it does not address the review objective of problem-solving across domains. Therefore it fails the review’s inclusion criterion I6 (problem-solving task) and is excluded under E7 (simple/non-problem-solving task).

execution_stats.search_query: ("problem solving"[Title/Abstract] OR "calculation"[Title/Abstract] OR "insight"[Title/Abstract] OR "verbal reasoning"[Title/Abstract] OR "visuospatial reasoning"[Title/Abstract] OR "deductive reasoning"[Title/Abstract] OR "inductive reasoning"[Title/Abstract] OR "fluid reasoning"[Title/Abstract]) AND ("fMRI"[Title/Abstract] OR "functional magnetic resonance imaging"[Title/Abstract] OR "PET"[Title/Abstract] OR "positron emission tomography"[Title/Abstract])
